summary: Specialist society: diagnose every Nexus Sync failure class against deployed v0.4.22, research Obsidian Sync/Syncthing/distsys, synthesize a never-lose-data design, adversarially refute it (Whetstone).

findings.role: CLOBBER pathologist (P0) — why v0.4.22 silently overwrites a locally-divergent file with the server's older copy
findings.failure_class: Silent data-loss clobber: server-wins overwrite of a locally-divergent file with NO conflict stash and NO warning, on the live SSE pull path, which bypasses the shadow/newer-wins decision entirely.
findings.root_cause:
Two compounding causes, both live at v0.4.22.

(a) POLICY: the daemon runs Live mode + ConflictPolicy::ServerWins. `MaterializerConfig::default()` hard-wires `conflict_policy: ConflictPolicy::ServerWins` (materializer.rs:194-195) and lib.rs:510-513 builds the runtime config as `MaterializerConfig { device_id: ..., ..Default::default() }` — it NEVER overrides conflict_policy, so production is ServerWins. Mode = `MaterializerMode::from_str(&snap.materializer_mode)` (lib.rs:547), driven by the server health snapshot; on Trinity that is "live". Under ServerWins, materializer.rs:486-489 computes `should_stash = (class == ConflictClass::D) || (policy != ServerWins)`. For an ordinary note (Class C — ConflictClassifier::classify returns C for everything except Credentials/Infrastructure/Bootstrap, conflict_stash.rs:125-142), BOTH disjuncts are false, so `should_stash == false`. The else-branch at materializer.rs:513-519 logs `info!("materializer server-wins: overwriting non-class-D divergent local")` and falls straight through to the atomic tmp+rename at lines 538-544 — overwriting the user's edit with the server bytes, leaving NO stash and only an INFO log. This is exactly I39's `C / ServerWins → OVERWRITE` cell, which the operator's 2026-06-09 ruling (NEWER-WINS + ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE) forbids.

(b) ARCHITECTURE (the reason it is also SILENT/UNGUARDED on the hot path): the live SSE path never makes a shadow-aware push/pull decision. sse.rs:271-275 handles every `enrichment_complete` event by `api.fetch_note(path)` then `materializer.write(&payload)` directly. `materializer.write()` has NO `shadow != server` branch — it only ever WRITES the shadow store (materializer.rs:479-481 on IdenticalToLocal, and 601-603 after a successful write), never READS it to gate the outcome. The only code that reads the shadow to decide direction is `decide_direction` (verify_repair.rs:235-257), used solely by the reconcile backstop (verify_repair.rs:359-365). So I37 row 3 (`local != server AND shadow != server → CONFLICT → stash + materialize server canonical`) is simply NOT implemented on the live materialize path. From the materializer's view, a genuine local edit (shadow == server, should be PUSH/SKIP per I37 row 2) and a stale local copy are indistinguishable: target.exists() + content differs → Diverges → ServerWins overwrite. Result: the user edits a note locally; an unrelated server enrichment (or any UPDATE) fires an enrichment_complete SSE event for that path; the daemon fetches the server's (older, pre-edit) canonical and overwrites the fresh local edit within ~30s. A new local-only file has no server row, so SSE never fetches/writes it (and reconcile maps missing-on-server → Push) — which is precisely why the newly-created file SURVIVED while the edited-both-sides file was reverted.

(c) shadow-absent/stale: irrelevant on the live path because the shadow is never consulted there. On the reconcile path, shadow-absent OR shadow != server → Pull (verify_repair.rs:241-252) i.e. server-wins overwrite, and that Pull executes via the same materializer.write() which, being ServerWins+ClassC, also does NOT stash. So both the live path and the reconcile-Pull path overwrite Class C content with no stash. Class C content is therefore NEVER stashed at v0.4.22; only Class D (Credentials/Infrastructure/Bootstrap) is.

findings.recommended_fix:
Two changes, both required; per the binding 2026-06-09 ruling the policy invariants I32/I39 must be revised for the daemon's pull side.

1. FLIP THE DAEMON PULL POLICY TO NEWER-WINS + ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE (revises I39 for Class C, daemon-side). In materializer.rs:486-489 the `should_stash` predicate must become: stash whenever the local file diverges AND we cannot prove the local is the OLDER side. Concretely, change the production config (lib.rs:510-513) to `conflict_policy: ConflictPolicy::NewerWins` (or add an explicit `AlwaysStashOnOverwrite` policy) so Class C diverging content is preserved as a `.conflict-from-<host>-<ts>.md` sibling before the server bytes are written — never a bare overwrite. The stash filename/host/ts is already implemented (conflict_stash.rs:256-313, I38). This satisfies "preserve BOTH sides, log loudly."

2. MAKE THE LIVE SSE PATH SHADOW-AWARE — implement I37 row 2 (PUSH) and row 3 (CONFLICT) in the materialize path (the architectural root). Today materializer.write() has only Identical vs Diverges. Add the shadow read INSIDE write() (it already holds `shadow_store`): on `Diverges`, look up `shadow.get(payload.path)`:
   - shadow == payload.sha256 (server hasn't moved since we last synced this file) AND local differs => this is a GENUINE LOCAL EDIT (I37 row 2). Do NOT overwrite; SKIP the write and let the file_watcher/push pipeline carry the local edit UP. (This is the case that is currently silently reverted.)
   - shadow absent OR shadow != payload.sha256 => true concurrent CONFLICT (I37 row 3) => stash local THEN write server canonical (newer-wins / always-stash), loud `warn!`.
   This requires the SSE consumer to NOT blindly trust write() to overwrite; rather, write() returns a Skipped(LocalEditPreserved)/Stashed/Wrote outcome that sse.rs already pattern-logs. Reuse `normalize_for_diff` (materializer.rs:686) for the local hash so the comparison matches the push-side basis (I34/R16).

3. CONCURRENCY SAFETY (~15 writers): the shadow read+compare+stash+rename must be serialized per-path so two SSE/reconcile events on the same path can't race (one stashes, the other overwrites the stash's basis). Take a per-path lock (or a single write mutex keyed by path) around the exists()->compare->stash->persist sequence in write(). Today there is none; the atomic tmp+rename only guarantees no torn file, not no lost-update between the read at line 469 and the rename at line 544.

4. Keep Class D ALWAYS-STASH (unchanged). Verify the live path also stashes Class C now (it currently does not — see `stash_not_written_for_class_c_under_server_wins` at materializer.rs:1159, which encodes the BUGGY behavior and must be updated to expect Stashed under the new policy).

Emit no em-dashes in any conflict-file content or log strings the fix writes (operator constraint).
findings.cross_os_notes: The clobber is OS-independent: ServerWins + the missing shadow-read live-path decision exist in shared code (materializer.rs / sse.rs), not behind any cfg(target_os). Mode is "live" on Trinity (macOS) per the server snapshot. macOS-specific nuance: restore_server_times (materializer.rs:139-169, set_created via setattrlist) runs on the overwrite, so the clobbered file even gets the server's OLDER created/mtime stamped back — making the revert look like the file was "never edited" in Obsidian's Created/Modified sort, which is why the operator saw a clean silent revert. Linux (link) cannot set birthtime so the revert is mtime-only there, but the data clobber is identical. The per-path lock fix (item 3) is portable (std Mutex / dashmap).
findings.residual_risks: FM2 (pull-incompleteness — materializer never CREATES a missing local file) is a SEPARATE charter, but note it interacts: the SSE path DOES create missing files (write() with target.exists()==false skips the conflict block and writes), so FM2 as stated is largely false on the SSE path at v0.4.22 — the pull-incompleteness, if any, would be in the reconcile backstop (verify_repair.rs only enqueues Pull for `drift`, never for server-only paths it didn't send), confirm with the PULL pathologist. RISK in fix item 2: if the shadow store is stale/corrupt (sync_shadow.rs:57-79 degrades a corrupt file to EMPTY), every Diverges falls to the CONFLICT branch => stash-storm of conflict files (safe-but-noisy). That is the acceptable failure direction (preserve data > avoid noise) and matches the operator ruling, but the reconcile backstop already treats shadow-absent as Pull/overwrite (verify_repair.rs:248-252) — that reconcile arm ALSO needs the always-stash treatment or it will re-clobber what the live path just preserved. RISK: I34's drain-time push guard means a preserved local edit only propagates UP if the file_watcher enqueues it; verify the echo_guard (materializer.rs:460-462) does not suppress the user's genuine edit (it records the SERVER sha; a divergent local sha should not match, so the watcher should still enqueue — but confirm with the echo-guard/push owner). Finally, flipping to NewerWins relies on `local_lsn` which conflict_stash.rs:503 + materializer.rs:503 currently pass as 0/None (unknown) => NewerWins treats local as older => stash; functionally this becomes always-stash-on-divergence regardless of true recency, which is the safe behavior but means the "newer truly wins" semantics are not yet real until a real local_lsn is threaded through.

findings.role: PULL-COMPLETENESS pathologist (FM2): verify whether the materializer ever CREATEs a missing local file, and whether server-only notes (incl. on a long-offline host) actually land locally, at deployed v0.4.22 (branch fix/sync-hardening-s511).
findings.failure_class: FM2 pull-incompleteness — server-only notes never materialized on a subscriber. Verdict: the CODE defect is FIXED at v0.4.22 (R6 pull_backfill is real and wired), but FM2 remains PARTIALLY live due to a deployment gate (Shadow mode) plus three residual defects (scope asymmetry, up-to-1h latency, silent best-effort failures).
findings.root_cause: Two-source blindness (original FM2 root): the daemon learned of server changes only from (a) SSE `enrichment_complete` events (sse.rs:245-279, gated by I25) and (b) reconcile-batch, which only echoes paths the client SENT (verify_repair.rs:16-19, I72). Neither can surface a note that exists only on the server and gets no fresh enrichment event — so it stayed invisible forever. The materializer was NOT the cause: write() always create-wrote missing files (materializer.rs:527 create_dir_all + 538-544 atomic tmp+rename; the target.exists() idempotency/stash block at line 469 is skipped when local is absent). The original "only skips/refuses, never creates" framing was a symptom of nothing ever CALLING write() for server-only paths, not of write() refusing to create. v0.4.22 adds pull_backfill.rs (R6): GET /api/sync/changes?since=0 paged to exhaustion -> plan_backfill filters to locally-missing safe non-substrate paths -> fetch_note + write, spawned at lib.rs:979-982 with the SAME materializer.clone() as SSE, first pass ~30s post-startup then hourly. The server endpoint exists (sync_routes_p1.py:282-334) and for route "" returns the full canonical set, so a long-offline host backfills everything on next startup. RESIDUAL ROOT (why still partial): (1) the materializer mode is read once from snap.materializer_mode at lib.rs:547 and the server column DEFAULTs to 'shadow' (migration 2026_05_25_005:31); the daemon never self-flips to live, so unless the Trinity subscriber row was explicitly set to 'live', every backfill (and SSE) create lands in the per-host runtime shadow tree (materializer.rs:382-389), NOT the vault — FM2 effectively persists. (2) should_backfill omits the path_in_scope check SSE applies. (3) up-to-1h discovery latency for server-created notes past the SSE cursor. (4) backfill failures are WARN-only/best-effort with no tray surface.
invariants_involved: I72 (reconcile never pulls — server-only files reach daemon only via SSE/catchup; R6 backfill is the new third path that closes this gap outside reconcile-batch)
I22 (catchup replays change_seq>N on reconnect — covers offline-then-reconnect but NOT a note created while up and past the cursor)
I25 (daemon materializes ONLY enrichment_complete SSE events; intermediate phases dropped — a server note with no fresh event is never SSE-surfaced, the core FM2 hole)
I21 (/changes cursor must advance past every fetched row incl. skipped — pull_backfill relies on this via next_lsn for termination; guarded again client-side at pull_backfill.rs:196)

findings.recommended_fix: 1. CLOSE THE DEPLOYMENT GATE (highest priority, smallest change): verify the Trinity (and link/Neo) subscriber rows have materializer_mode='live'; if any are 'shadow', a live-mode subscriber NEVER materializes into the vault and FM2 is fully live for it. Either flip the rows via PATCH /api/sync/subscribers/me, or change the server DEFAULT off 'shadow' for user-facing vault subscribers. Add a startup tray WARN when mode != 'live' so a silently-shadowed subscriber is visible (today it is invisible). This is verification + config, not Rust code, and is the single highest-leverage action. 2. CLOSE THE SCOPE ASYMMETRY: in should_backfill (pull_backfill.rs:92), also reject paths failing path_in_scope(scope_roots, scope_excludes) — thread the scope lists into spawn_pull_backfill_task exactly as sse.rs already has them (lib.rs:595-596 watch_scope_roots/excludes). Without it a scoped subscriber over-pulls every server note. 3. REDUCE DISCOVERY LATENCY for notes created while up: either lower DEFAULT_BACKFILL_INTERVAL_SECS from 3600 to ~300 to match the reconcile cadence, or (better) have the reconciliation backstop's detected-but-dropped pulls feed a missing-note trigger rather than relying solely on the hourly backfill. 4. SURFACE BACKFILL FAILURES: fold PullBackfillStats.failed into a tray counter (mirror integrity_failures wiring at materializer.rs:574-579) and emit a one-shot owner notification when failed>0 across a pass, since stderr WARN is /dev/null on the Windows GUI subsystem (per the S476 note in sse.rs:22-25). 5. (test-gap from spec I72 row, verify_repair.rs:676) add the missing 'reconcile never pulls a server-only file' property test and a pull_backfill integration test that asserts a server-only note lands in the LIVE vault path (not just shadow) on a long-offline host. NOTE: emit no em-dashes in any vault content the fix writes (operator ruling).
findings.residual_risks: (a) I could NOT confirm the LIVE materializer_mode of the Trinity/link/Neo subscriber rows from static source — if any is 'shadow', FM2 is FULLY live for that host despite the R6 code being correct; this needs a DB check (SELECT subscriber_id, materializer_mode FROM vault_subscribers) which I did not run. A local shadow_hashes.json exists for subscriber f2383e35... on this Mac, proving a daemon runs here, but not its mode. (b) The TKT-2dc9a17e live data-loss bug is FM3/conflict-overwrite territory (existing-on-both-sides, server wins silently), NOT FM2 — FM2 governs files ABSENT locally; do not conflate. (c) Backfill correctness depends on the server change_seq cursor being complete; chunk pseudo-rows (…md#chunk_N) and change_seq IS NULL rows are excluded server-side (sync_routes_p1.py:266-267) — correct, but any not-yet-backfilled change_seq=NULL real note would be invisible to /changes until stamped. (d) get_changes for a NAMED route (non-'') applies to_wire route-stripping; pull_backfill's lack of scope filtering interacts with that and is untested for named routes. (e) First-pass timing: 30s settle + hourly means a freshly-created remote note has up to ~1h invisibility window even with everything healthy.
findings.cross_os_notes: Materializer birthtime restore is macOS/Windows only (materializer.rs:155-158, restore_server_times); Linux (link) sets mtime only — irrelevant to FM2 create-completeness (the file IS created on all three; only the Created-sort timestamp differs on Linux). Shadow-vs-live gate is OS-independent (server-driven). Failure-visibility gap is worst on Windows (Neo): the S476 note in sse.rs:22-25 documents that stderr goes to /dev/null on the Windows GUI subsystem, so pull_backfill WARN logs for failed creates are invisible there — the tray-surface fix (rec #4) matters most for Neo. spawn_pull_backfill_task uses tauri::async_runtime::spawn, which swallows panics into an unawaited JoinHandle (same failure mode lib.rs:874-880 calls out for the push loop) — a panicking backfill task dies silently on all OSes with no watchdog; the push-pipeline stall watchdog (sync_health) does NOT cover the backfill task.

findings.failure_class: Liveness / dormancy — push pipeline silent with pending (or suppressed) diffs and no alert
findings.root_cause:
The original FM1 (process-alive-but-push-silent, incident 2026-06-13) IS genuinely fixed at v0.4.22: liveness now verifies PROGRESS, not just process-alive. `is_stalled(pending, secs_since_progress, threshold)` (sync_health.rs:126-128) trips iff `pending>0 && elapsed>=threshold`; the watchdog (lib.rs:888-937) polls real journal depth every 60s and `app.restart()`s at 900s default. The drain loop is panic-free and NACK/ACK discipline (I47) is correct, so a hung-or-panicked push task is caught indirectly (no mark_progress stamp → threshold fires).

HOWEVER three residual liveness holes remain, two of them able to lose a real local edit silently:

(A) DEDUP-CAN-DROP-A-REAL-EDIT (the exact risk the charter named). `enqueued_hashes` (file_watcher.rs:224, set at :879 on a SUCCESSFUL APPEND, cleared ONLY on delete at :858) remembers the sha last *enqueued*, never what the server actually *accepted*. If an edit enqueues (map[path]=shaA) but its push is later dropped terminally — HTTP 400 ServerRejected skip+ack (push_client.rs:519-525), or lost to startup `compact()` collapse (lib.rs:793) — and the user then changes the file to content whose sha equals shaA (e.g. a revert to a prior version, or any content the daemon previously enqueued), the dedup at file_watcher.rs:843-855 matches and SKIPS enqueue. That genuine later change never queues, never pushes, no conflict file — silent loss. The map is keyed only by path+last-enqueued-sha and is never reconciled against server canonical, so 'enqueued once' is wrongly treated as 'synced forever'.

(B) WATCHDOG BLIND TO A FENCED/DEAD WATCHER. The push_client and file_watcher run on SEPARATE PushJournal handles with incompatible mutex types (tokio vs std — lib.rs:990-1001); the watcher is NOT wired to sync_health (only push_client gets with_sync_health, lib.rs:858; FileWatcher::new has no health field). The storm breaker permanently HALTS the watch loop (`break`, file_watcher.rs:739-744) and a panic in the spawned notify task (file_watcher.rs:701) is swallowed. In either case enqueue stops dead; once the existing backlog drains, pending==0 forever, so `is_stalled` returns false and never alerts/restarts. Liveness measures the DRAIN side only — a daemon that has stopped DETECTING local edits looks perfectly healthy.

(C) SINGLE PROGRESS SOURCE. mark_progress has exactly one non-test call site (push_client.rs:370). reconciliation/pull_backfill/verify_repair stamp nothing, so progress == push-drain only. Adequate for FM1-classic, but reinforces (B): there is no liveness signal that the enqueue/watch path is alive.

The dedup CANNOT suppress a normal forward edit (any byte change yields a new sha, including frontmatter), so the v0.4.17/18 commit claim 'stop redundant pushes burying real edits' holds for the common case; the defect is narrow but real for reverts and for any post-push-failure re-edit to previously-seen content.

I48 (retry/backoff bounds — honored)
I50 (storm circuit-breaker — present and tested for the threshold, but its halt is a liveness blind spot: fenced watcher is invisible to the I-? liveness watchdog)
Liveness invariant gap: spec §8 (I47-I50) has NO invariant asserting the ENQUEUE/watch side is itself live; sync_health only covers push-drain progress

findings.recommended_fix:
Three targeted changes; none re-introduce push storms (all preserve the no-op dedup's real purpose).

1) FIX DEDUP-DROPS-REAL-EDIT (highest priority, data-loss). Make the dedup authoritative on what the SERVER ACCEPTED, not on what was enqueued. Either (a) clear the enqueued_hashes entry for a path whenever its push outcome is anything other than a clean Accepted whose hash matches (i.e. on Skipped/ServerRejected/Failed/Merged the entry must be invalidated so the next FS event re-enqueues), OR preferably (b) drop the separate enqueued_hashes map entirely and gate enqueue on the existing ShadowStore (sync_shadow.rs) which already records the server's last-accepted canonical hash per path (recorded at push_client.rs:494-503). Gating on 'current sha == last SERVER-ACCEPTED sha' is exactly the correct idempotency test and cannot suppress a revert-after-failed-push, because a failed push never records a shadow hash. This also unifies the two redundant hash stores. Keep the raw-sha equality semantics so any byte change still enqueues.

2) WIRE THE WATCHER INTO LIVENESS. Add a watcher-alive heartbeat to SyncHealth (e.g. `mark_watcher_alive()` stamped each loop iteration in file_watcher.rs:701-755, and set a `watcher_fenced: AtomicBool` mirror into SyncHealth on the fence at :740). Extend the watchdog decision so a fenced OR heartbeat-stale watcher fires recovery REGARDLESS of pending count (a dead enqueue path is a stall even at pending==0). Since the watcher uses std::sync::Mutex and the watchdog already runs async, expose the watcher heartbeat as plain atomics on the shared SyncHealth Arc (lock-free, no mutex-type conflict). This closes hole (B) without unifying the journals.

3) HARDEN STAMP SEMANTICS. The `pending_after == 0 ⇒ mark_progress` branch (push_client.rs:369) means a drained-but-dead-watcher reads as healthy idle. Once (2) lands, the watcher heartbeat covers idle correctly; consider stamping push-progress ONLY on `!out.is_empty()` and letting the separate watcher heartbeat own the idle-is-healthy signal, so the two concerns are not conflated.

Add regression tests: (i) enqueue shaA → simulate 400/drop → revert file to shaA content → assert it RE-ENQUEUES (red on current code); (ii) fence the watcher → assert watchdog fires even with pending==0; (iii) panic the notify task → assert heartbeat staleness trips recovery. All emit no em-dashes in any vault-bound content.
findings.cross_os_notes: The dedup-drops-real-edit defect (A) is OS-independent — pure in-memory logic in handle_fs_path. The fenced-watcher blind spot (B) is also OS-independent, but note Linux has an extra fence trigger: InotifyLimitExceeded (file_watcher.rs:166-168, ENOSPC on inotify_add_watch) returns Err from start() so on Linux the watcher may never start at all (lib.rs:1037 logs 'push_client running but no local-edit detection' and returns None) — push runs, enqueue is permanently dead, pending stays 0, watchdog never fires. macOS/Windows use FSEvents/ReadDirectoryChangesW and do not hit the inotify limit, so on those platforms (B) is reachable mainly via the storm fence or a task panic. The live bug is on Trinity (macOS), so (A) is the most likely culprit there; the operator's report 'newly-created local file SURVIVED' is consistent with (A) since a brand-new path has no enqueued_hashes entry and no shadow hash, so it always enqueues.
findings.residual_risks: (1) This FM1 charter covers the PUSH/enqueue liveness only; the live TKT-2dc9a17e symptom (local edit silently OVERWRITTEN by older server copy) is primarily a PULL/reconcile-direction defect (verify_repair decide_direction + I32/I39 ServerWins) owned by the conflict/reconcile pathologist — even a perfectly-live push can be raced and clobbered by a server-wins pull before/while it pushes. My fixes stop the push side from silently DROPPING the edit, but do not by themselves stop a server-wins pull from reverting it; both must land. (2) Fix (1b) assumes the ShadowStore is reliably populated; if a path was edited before its first successful push, there is no shadow entry and enqueue proceeds (correct, fail-open). (3) The watcher heartbeat (fix 2) adds a restart trigger — ensure its staleness threshold is well above the debounce window and any legitimate quiet period so it does not false-fire on a genuinely idle but healthy watcher (use a generous threshold, and only fire on fenced=true OR heartbeat older than e.g. 3x the watchdog tick). (4) ~15 concurrent agent writers: the enqueued_hashes/shadow gating is per-path and per-handle in-process; under heavy concurrent writes the dedup is best-effort and a rapid A→B→A byte sequence within one debounce window could still collapse to one enqueue of the final state — acceptable (last-writer state is what we want to push), but worth a note in the contract.

findings.role: PUSH-REFLECTION pathologist (FM3, server side) — Nexus Sync Fix Campaign
findings.failure_class: Accepted push does not reflect in queryable content; the PG write commits but no path makes the pushed bytes visible to either reader. Class: server-side write/read-surface split (PG-only push vs FS-only reads), plus a stale-FS divergence on /api/sync/note. NOT a non-committed resync.
findings.root_cause:
The unify-on-PG push (`POST /api/sync/push` -> `_cas_write_note_and_state` -> `_exec_note_and_state_upserts`, sync_routes_p1.py:1383-1537, 1217-1288) writes ONLY two PG tables: `vault_notes` (body) and `vault_reconcile_state` (fs_hash + fs_modified). It commits them atomically and correctly (I30 upheld). It NEVER writes the note's filesystem file, and there is NO server-side PG->FS materializer anywhere (the reconciler is FS->PG ONE-WAY only; exhaustive grep for `write_bytes`/`write_text`/`open(...,'w')` in sync_routes_p1.py, reconciler.py, cache_writer.py, enrichment_runner.py returns nothing). Both read surfaces the operator and daemon hit are therefore not updated by a push:

(1) OPERATOR PATH `/api/notes/{token}` (notes_routes.py:1471-1493) -> `read_note(config, path)` (note_writer.py:476-482) reads `Path(config.vault_root)/rel_path` FROM THE FILESYSTEM and returns None (HTTP 404 'Note file not found') when the file is absent. It never consults PG `vault_notes.body`. So after a push: an EDITED note keeps serving the STALE on-disk bytes (operator sees pre-edit content indefinitely); a NEW pushed note has no FS file -> 404, 'never queryable by path'. This is the exact FM3 symptom on the API the operator queries.

(2) DAEMON PULL `/api/sync/note` (sync_routes_p1.py:1008-1123) serves FS file bytes if `VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key>` exists, else falls back to `vault_notes.body`. After an EDIT the FS file at `/srv/vault/Mainframe/<path>` still holds OLD content, so `/note` returns OLD bytes with `sha256(old)`, even though the push just set `vault_reconcile_state.fs_hash` to `sha256(new)`. Served-bytes now diverge permanently from the freshly-written fs_hash a direct violation of I14 ('bytes served == bytes hashed == fs_hash') in the push->serve direction. (A CREATE has no FS file, so `/note` does fall back to the pushed `vault_notes.body` and is readable via the daemon path even while invisible to the operator path.)

ON 'seq=0': the push handler ALWAYS returns `seq=None` (sync_routes_p1.py:1462, 1519, 1531) the unify-on-PG push design carries no sequence numbers (the change_seq/LSN feed is a separate mechanism on `/changes`/SSE). The Rust daemon renders `Option::None` as `0`. 'push accepted seq=0' is the NORMAL accepted-write response and does NOT indicate a non-committed full-resync. The write committed to PG; it simply has no surface that the readers consult.

SECONDARY (separate from the queryable-content symptom): pushed rows are tagged `origin='nexus-sync-push'` (to dodge the daemon own-origin loop guard, sync_routes_p1.py:1266-1274) and the push never sets `lint_state`, which DEFAULTs to 'pending' NOT NULL (migration 2026_05_20_001). The `notify_vault_note_change` v2 trigger (migration 2026_05_20_004) computes `phase='lint_pending'` for these rows (it only emits `enrichment_complete` when `origin='enrichment-engine'`). The `nexus-sync` daemon's Router DOES chain lint->enrichment->cache_write in one dispatch (router.py:126-172), so the `vault_subscriber_fanout` NOTIFY can still fire IF that daemon is running and the lint chain neither errors nor 'block's. BUT this affects only the live SSE push-NOTIFICATION; per I14/I18 `vault_sync_cache` is no longer on the `/note` serve path, so cache backlog/failure does NOT change queryable CONTENT. So a 45-min dormant fanout would delay live SSE wake-ups but is NOT the cause of /api/notes returning pre-edit content that is fully explained by (1)+(2). Note the daemon is a SEPARATE deployable (infra/compose.nexus-sync.fragment.yaml, container `nexus-sync`), not in server/docker-compose.yaml; if it is not running on Cypher, fanout is silently dead with zero error surface (FM1 territory).

invariants_involved: I14 (THE CENTRAL LAW: bytes served == bytes hashed == fs_hash) — VIOLATED in push->serve direction: push advances fs_hash but leaves the FS file stale, so /api/sync/note serves old bytes whose sha != the new fs_hash
I30 (atomic CAS dual-upsert) — UPHELD: _cas_write_note_and_state commits vault_notes + vault_reconcile_state in one txn under FOR UPDATE
I31 (idempotent push) — UPHELD: current==raw_hash returns idempotent no-op
I19 (enrichment must not mutate canonical body) — UPHELD and orthogonal; not the cause

I13 (fs_hash is sha256 of FS file bytes, else vault_notes.body) — relevant: push writes fs_hash from the pushed bytes but no FS file backs it, so the I13 'FS file bytes' premise is broken for pushed-but-unmaterialized notes
findings.recommended_fix:
Make a committed push immediately reflect in BOTH read surfaces by writing the canonical bytes to the FS vault inside the same accepted-push flow, so the file the readers consult equals the bytes that were hashed (restores I14 by construction):

1. PRIMARY FIX — materialize pushed bytes to the FS vault in the push path. In sync_routes_p1.py, after a successful `_cas_write_note_and_state` returns 'written' (and in the delete branch), write/remove `VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key>` atomically (tmp file + os.replace), creating parent dirs, using the SAME `content_bytes` already decoded (so `sha256(file) == raw_hash == fs_hash == vault_notes.body` by construction). Guard it under_root exactly like get_note_body's resolve check (lines 1083-1089) to prevent traversal. This single change makes /api/notes/{token} serve the new content (no more stale/404) AND makes /api/sync/note serve bytes whose sha == the fs_hash the push just wrote — closing both the FM3 operator symptom and the I14 push->serve divergence. The FS write MUST be inside/right-after the committed txn boundary; on FS-write failure, log loudly and surface a non-2xx so the daemon retries rather than believing a write landed that no reader can see (avoid a new silent-loss class). CAUTION re the campaign's concurrency constraint (~15 writers): the FS write must be idempotent and last-writer-after-CAS; since CAS already serializes by fs_hash under FOR UPDATE, sequence the FS replace AFTER commit so two racing pushes apply in CAS-commit order.

   ALTERNATIVE if writing FS from the API container is undesirable (NFS perf, container boundary): instead make BOTH readers prefer PG `vault_notes.body` when it is newer than the FS file. Concretely, have `read_note`/`/api/notes` and `/api/sync/note` compare `vault_reconcile_state.fs_hash` vs `sha256(FS bytes)`; if they differ, serve `vault_notes.body` (the pushed canonical) instead of the stale FS file. This is more invasive and re-introduces a served-vs-fs ambiguity; the FS-materialize fix is cleaner and directly satisfies I14.

2. SECONDARY FIX (fanout liveness, not queryable-content) — stop pushed notes from being stuck at phase=lint_pending and make fanout observable:
   (a) In _exec_note_and_state_upserts, set `lint_state` explicitly on push (e.g. INSERT/UPDATE `lint_state='clean'`) OR have the trigger treat `origin='nexus-sync-push'` as a content-ready phase, so the daemon path is unambiguous and a missing lint rulebook can never strand a pushed note at lint_pending.
   (b) Add a health/lag assertion that the `nexus-sync` daemon is alive and `fanout_notifies_emitted` is advancing; today if that separate container is down, fanout dies with zero error surface (FM1). Surface 'last fanout emit age' in /api/sync/lag or the reconciler health so a 45-min dormancy alarms.

3. TEST (currently MISSING per the contract's I14 row): add a server test that pushes new bytes for an existing path, then asserts (i) GET /api/sync/note returns the NEW bytes with sha256==returned fs_hash, and (ii) GET /api/notes/{token} returns the NEW body — i.e. the push is reflected in BOTH surfaces. Add a create-then-query-by-path test asserting no 404. This is the explicit S481/2026-06-08 regression-class guard the contract flags as absent.

No em-dashes used in any proposed vault-emitted content.
findings.cross_os_notes: Server-side only (Cypher/Linux); no client-OS variance. The FS-materialize fix runs in the Linux nexus/reconciler container against the NFS-mounted vault (/srv/vault/Mainframe on reconciler; /vault on the main nexus container note the two containers mount the vault at DIFFERENT paths, so the materialize write must use the writing container's own VAULT_ROOT and any reader in the other container must resolve its own root). Use atomic os.replace for NFS safety; NFS rename is atomic within the same export. The push container currently has no write mount documented for the vault under VAULT_ROOT verify the API/reconciler container that handles /api/sync/push has rw access to the vault path before relying on option 1; if only the reconciler can write FS, route materialization there or adopt the PG-body-preferred read (alternative in fix 1).
findings.residual_risks: 1. If the FS-materialize fix is added but the push container lacks rw vault mount, writes fail; without a loud surface this becomes a new silent-loss class — fix MUST fail the push (non-2xx) on FS-write error. 2. The PUSH path itself is correct (CAS/commit verified), so if prod still shows 'accepted but pre-edit content' AFTER materialize, the next suspects are: (a) the operator reading a DIFFERENT vault path than the one written (two container mount roots), or (b) an external FS writer (Dropbox/MegaCity NFS, or another agent) overwriting the file back to old content after the push — out of this charter but must be ruled out. 3. The secondary fanout fix does not affect queryable content; do not conflate. 4. I did not have live PG/log access to confirm the nexus-sync daemon is actually running on Cypher or that fanout_notifies_emitted is advancing; the FM1/FM3-fanout overlap (45-min dormancy) should be verified against live container status + daemon health (:5557). 5. Concurrency: with ~15 writers, sequencing FS replace after CAS-commit is required; an FS write performed BEFORE commit or outside the lock could let an older push clobber a newer file.

findings.role: SERVER-POLICY pathologist (sync_routes_p1.py CAS/scope/changes + reconciler.py FS-PG sweep)
findings.failure_class: Silent data loss via competing unconditional FS-wins writer (reconciler sweep) that overrides both the daemon's newer-wins policy AND the server's own CAS; plus scope-honoring inconsistency across endpoints and a NULL-created birthtime-clobber residue.
findings.root_cause: PRIMARY ROOT CAUSE of TKT-2dc9a17e (silent revert of a locally-edited file to the server's OLDER copy within ~30s, no conflict file): the nexus-reconciler runs an UNCONDITIONAL one-way FS->PG sweep every 60s (reconciler.py:54 SWEEP_INTERVAL_S=60; sweep_once line 424-443) that is completely blind to the CAS/newer-wins protocol. The daemon's POST /api/sync/push writes ONLY Postgres -- _cas_write_note_and_state upserts vault_notes.body + vault_reconcile_state.fs_hash (sync_routes_p1.py:1512, 1217-1288). It NEVER writes the server FS file at VAULT_ROOT/<path> (verified: the entire push_file body lines 1383-1538 contains ZERO FS access). But GET /api/sync/note serves the FS file bytes (sync_routes_p1.py:1089-1092 raw=fs_resolved.read_bytes()) and the reconciler hashes the FS file bytes into fs_hash. So the FS file is a SEPARATE authority, written by brain/steward/note_writer tools (note_writer.py:157 vault=Path(config.vault_root); config.vault_root defaults to /vault == /srv/vault/Mainframe) and NOT updated by any daemon push. SEQUENCE OF LOSS: (1) brain/steward earlier wrote/last-materialized FS file X with content A; fs_hash=sha(A). (2) An agent edits the same note locally -> daemon pushes content B -> CAS passes (base_hash==current==sha(A)) -> _exec_note_and_state_upserts sets vault_notes.body=B and fs_hash=sha(B). PG now = B. (3) Within <=60s the reconciler sweep runs: local_hash=sha(FS file)=sha(A) (the FS file was NEVER updated to B), pg_hash=_read_pg_hash=sha(B). They differ (reconciler.py:435 `if pg_hash == local_hash: continue` is FALSE), so it executes _upsert_note_and_state(rel_path, A, sha(A)) UNCONDITIONALLY (reconciler.py:438) -- overwriting vault_notes.body back to A and fs_hash back to sha(A), with origin='reconciler-cypher'. (4) The body-changed UPDATE re-stamps change_seq (migration 2026_05_30_005 trigger fires because body B->A IS DISTINCT), so /changes + SSE fan A back out; the daemon (newer-wins, but the SERVER now reports its current hash as sha(A) which equals the daemon's SHADOW after its push echo) materializes A. Net: the local edit B is reverted to the older server copy A, no 409, no conflict-stash, no log. This is a SERVER-SIDE clobber that the daemon's newer-wins + always-stash policy (operator ruling) CANNOT defend against, because the loss happens in PG via the reconciler, not in any decision the daemon makes. A NEWLY-CREATED local file SURVIVES (matches the live bug report) precisely because no FS file with that path exists, so the sweep's iter_eligible_files never yields it and never reverts it -- the revert only hits files that exist on the server FS with divergent (stale) content. NOTE on I32: the CAS itself (I32 ServerWins-at-CAS) does NOT directly cause this loss -- the daemon's push ACCEPTS (its base matches). The loss is the reconciler, a second writer outside the CAS. However I32+I39-ClassC do compound a SECOND latent loss path: if two daemons edit concurrently and the loser refetches the server canonical, under daemon ServerWins-mode (I39 Class C OVERWRITE) the loser's content is discarded with NO stash, violating the operator ruling. Both must be fixed.
invariants_involved: I32 (ServerWins-at-CAS, no server merge -- POLICY, must be revised to require always-stash semantics end to end; CAS is not the proximate cause but Class-C-OVERWRITE under it is a latent silent-loss path)

I36 (server push/reconcile MUST persist created/modified -- the reconciler _upsert_note_and_state at reconciler.py:391-402 does NOT parse frontmatter and writes NEITHER created NOR modified, unlike the push path's _exec_note_and_state_upserts:1243-1275; on every sweep-revert the row loses its created/modified -> NULL-created birthtime clobber on re-materialize)
I52 (server scope evaluation order MUST be consistent across /note, /changes, SSE -- it is NOT: three different engines, and /changes + reconcile-batch apply NO scope_roots/scope_excludes at all)
I60 (route mapping at all four boundaries -- correctly applied for routes, but scope is not co-applied at /changes or reconcile-batch)
I74 (tombstone retention 24h -- unrelated to loss but the reconciler never tombstones FS-deleted notes; orthogonal gap)
I13/I14 (fs_hash == served bytes == FS file -- the central law is what makes the reconciler the de-facto source of truth, which is WHY the FS-wins sweep is so destructive)
I20 (change_seq bumps on body change -- the reconciler's revert write re-bumps change_seq, weaponizing the change-feed to propagate the stale revert back to every subscriber)

findings.recommended_fix:
FIVE server-side changes, in priority order.

(1) STOP THE RECONCILER FROM SILENTLY OVERWRITING NEWER PG STATE (the live-bug fix; reconciler.py:435-439). The 60s FS->PG sweep must NOT blindly assume FS is authoritative. Three sub-options, in order of preference:
  (1a) PREFERRED -- make the daemon push ALSO write the canonical FS file at VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key> inside _cas_write_note_and_state's transaction-committed path (atomic tmp+rename via the existing write_with_mtime_preservation / os.replace pattern in lattice_conductor/surfaces/vault_log.py:198). Then FS == PG == fs_hash after every push by construction, the sweep finds pg_hash==local_hash and no-ops (reconciler.py:435), and the revert window closes. This collapses the two authorities into one and is the cleanest fix that satisfies I13/I14.
  (1b) If FS-write-on-push is rejected: gate the sweep with a recency/provenance guard. Before _upsert_note_and_state, read vault_notes.origin + vault_reconcile_state.last_reconciled_at; if the row's origin is a daemon push ('nexus-sync-push') AND its pg_modified is NEWER than the FS file's st_mtime, the FS is STALE -> SKIP the revert and instead re-materialize PG->FS (write body to the FS file) so the FS catches up to the pushed content. Never let an older FS file overwrite a newer PG body.
  (1c) Minimum stop-gap: have the sweep, on divergence where pg row exists and is newer, write a .conflict-cypher-<ts> copy of the FS-side content and KEEP the PG body, mirroring the operator's always-stash ruling on the server side. Never a silent body overwrite.

(2) REVISE I32/I39 so a daemon-side ServerWins overwrite of Class-C content ALWAYS stashes (operator ruling). On the SERVER this means: keep CAS-409 (correct), but ADD a server-side version capture so no overwrite is ever unrecoverable. The note-versioning trigger already exists (migration 2026_05_31_002_version_trigger); VERIFY it fires on the reconciler's _upsert_note_and_state path and on _exec_note_and_state_upserts so every body change (including a revert) is recoverable from versions. If it does not cover the reconciler write, extend it.

(3) FIX I36 IN THE RECONCILER (reconciler.py:391-402): parse frontmatter and persist created/modified, mirroring _exec_note_and_state_upserts:1240-1275 (CASE WHEN EXCLUDED.created <> '' THEN ... ELSE vault_notes.created END). As written, EVERY sweep-revert (and every sweep upsert of a note whose FS lacks the columns) blanks created/modified -> NULL-created -> birthtime clobber. This is the server half of the NULL-created problem.

(4) NULL-CREATED BACKFILL PLAN: run a one-time idempotent UPDATE on vault_notes setting created = COALESCE(NULLIF(created,''), modified, indexed_at, to_char(file_mtime-as-timestamp)) WHERE created IS NULL OR created = '' AND path LIKE '%.md'. Scope estimate: I could not run live SQL against Cypher PG from this worktree, so I cannot give an exact count; the prior diagnostic in MEMORY records ~6,213 NULL-created rows were backfilled on 2026-06-09 (handoff_nexus_sync_convergence). Because fix (3) stops NEW NULLs at the reconciler write path, the remaining backfill is bounded to rows created/reverted between 2026-06-09 and the deploy of (3). RUN ORDER (critical): deploy (3) FIRST (stop the bleed), THEN backfill, with the fanout trigger suppression used for the original 32k backfill so the backfill UPDATE does not body-change-bump change_seq and re-fan 6k rows. created-only updates do NOT touch body so they will NOT bump change_seq (trigger is body-gated, migration 005:41) -- safe, but verify no other AFTER trigger fans on a created-only change.

(5) SCOPE-HONORING FIX (I52, TKT-3f13c769): unify scope across ALL four endpoints. (a) Switch reconcile-batch (sync_routes_p1.py:394) from Depends(require_bearer) to Depends(_require_bearer_with_scope) and filter the returned deltas through path_in_scope(storage_key, scope_roots, scope_excludes) so an out-of-scope path returns missing-on-server (never leaks a server_hash for a path the subscriber may not see, and never invites a cross-scope push). (b) Apply scope_roots/scope_excludes in get_changes (sync_routes_p1.py:307-334) using the SAME path_in_scope on the storage_key, advancing max_lsn past skipped rows exactly as the route-skip already does (I21), so a scoped subscriber's cursor still progresses. (c) Collapse the two scope engines onto ONE: api/scope_filter_match.py:path_in_scope is used by /note + SSE; services/vault_sync/scope_filter.py:ScopeFilter (pathspec) is used at push. They have DIFFERENT glob semantics (fnmatch vs gitwildmatch) -- pick gitwildmatch/pathspec (matches the daemon's Rust `ignore` crate and the V9 baseline) as canonical and route /note + /changes + SSE through it, so a path included on push is never excluded on pull (or vice-versa), which can itself cause perpetual push/no-pull churn for scoped subscribers.
findings.cross_os_notes: The reconciler-revert mechanism is server-side (Cypher/Linux container) and platform-independent of the client -- it reverts a Trinity (macOS) edit, a link (Linux) edit, or a Neo (Windows) edit identically, because the loss is in PG via the FS sweep, not in any client decision. This explains why the live bug reproduced on Trinity but is NOT macOS-specific. For fix (1a) the server FS write must use atomic tmp+rename (os.replace, same-dir) on the Linux container FS at /srv/vault/Mainframe (NFS from MegaCity per reconciler.py:18-20 -- note the docstring already flags watchfiles/inotify as fragile on this NFS mount, so a 60s sweep stays; an atomic rename on NFS is safe but fsync-before-rename is advisable). Birthtime restore (I65) is client-side and unaffected by these server fixes EXCEPT that fix (3)+(4) restore the `created` value the daemon needs; on Linux clients birthtime is kernel-read-only so created stays frontmatter-only (acceptable per I65). No console-codepage concerns server-side (UTF-8 throughout, I81).
findings.residual_risks: (A) Fix (1a) introduces a NEW FS writer (the push path) alongside brain/steward/note_writer and the reconciler -- concurrent writes to the same FS file from a push and a brain create_note are possible under the ~15 concurrent-agent constraint. Mitigation: the push FS write must be atomic tmp+rename AND should hold the same FOR UPDATE row-lock window as _cas_write_note_and_state (extend the transaction to cover the FS write, or serialize per-path). Otherwise a brain write and a push write race on the FS file. (B) If fix (1b) is chosen instead, the recency comparison (pg_modified vs FS st_mtime) is only as reliable as clock/mtime fidelity on the NFS mount; a brain write that legitimately post-dates a push could be wrongly skipped -- prefer (1a). (C) The note-versioning trigger (migration 2026_05_31_002) MUST be confirmed to cover both the reconciler write and the push write before relying on it for recoverability (fix 2); I did not verify its trigger scope in this pass -- it is a required follow-up read of 2026_05_31_002_version_trigger.py. (D) I could NOT execute live SQL against Cypher PG from this worktree, so the NULL-created count (est. <=6,213 from prior record) is an estimate, not a measured value -- run `SELECT count(*) FROM vault_notes WHERE (created IS NULL OR created='') AND path LIKE '%.md'` on Cypher before the backfill. (E) Scope-engine unification (fix 5c) is behavior-changing for any subscriber that already relies on the looser fnmatch /note semantics; audit live vault_subscribers.scope_roots/scope_excludes values before deploying. (F) The reconciler sweep also has NO deletion handling -- a note deleted on FS is never tombstoned in PG by the sweep (only the daemon-push DELETE path tombstones, sync_routes_p1.py:1368-1380); orthogonal to this loss but a known convergence gap (I74-adjacent).

findings.role: macOS filesystem expert — daemon materializer.rs / file_watcher.rs / echo_guard.rs audit (v0.4.22, branch fix/sync-hardening-s511)
findings.failure_class: Silent local-edit revert on files present on both sides. Two macOS-relevant mechanisms: (A) echo-guard mis-suppression of a genuine local edit under FSEvents coalescing within the 15s TTL (I40), and (B) a ctime/mtime-restore data-quality regression (I64/I65) where mtime is restored from the WRONG field and can leave birthtime+mtime at "now". The PRIMARY data-loss path (reconcile Pull-on-absent/stale-shadow) is platform-independent and owned by the reconcile specialist, but it is reached through the same macOS write path I audited.
findings.root_cause:
FOUR distinct findings, ranked.

FINDING 1 — I40 echo-guard "consume-on-hit" can swallow a genuine local edit on macOS (CONTRIBUTES to the silent revert). In `write()` the materializer records `echo_guard.record(path, actual_sha)` at materializer.rs:460-462 BEFORE the disk write (tmp.persist at :544). On macOS, `notify=7` uses the FSEvents backend, which COALESCES all events for a path within the notify-debouncer-full 500ms window (debounce_ms default 500, file_watcher.rs:107) into a single DebouncedEvent. EchoGuard.is_echo() removes the entry on the first match (echo_guard.rs:67 `m.remove(path)`). The failure window: (1) SSE materializes note X writing server bytes B with sha S, records (X,S); (2) within the same FSEvents coalescing window the operator saves an edit, but their editor's final on-disk bytes equal B (e.g. the operator was mid-typing content the server also just sent, or an editor that round-trips identical bytes, or the materialize landed AFTER the user save so disk == B at the moment the watcher reads it at file_watcher.rs:800-811); (3) the single coalesced event hashes to S → is_echo(X,S)=true → the event is dropped (file_watcher.rs:824-831) and NEVER journaled. No push, no conflict file → the operator's intent is lost silently. The guard is documented "SAFE BY DESIGN (fail-open)" (echo_guard.rs:16-20) on the assumption that a real edit always yields a DIFFERENT sha; that assumption breaks under FSEvents coalescing because the watcher only ever sees the LAST bytes for a path in the window, not each intermediate state.

FINDING 2 — I64 spec/code mismatch on the mtime SOURCE, with a 'now'-clobber fallthrough (macOS ctime-clobber regression risk). I64 mandates mtime be restored from the canonical `modified` field. The actual code (`restore_server_times`, materializer.rs:139-169) restores mtime from `payload.file_mtime` with a fallback to `payload.created` (line 146), and NEVER reads `payload.modified` (the RFC3339 string at api_client.rs:83). If the server sends `file_mtime` absent/None AND `created` absent/None, BOTH `to_systime` calls return None, the `let Some(mtime) = mtime else { return; }` guard at :147-149 early-returns, and NO times are set — so the freshly tmp+rename'd inode keeps birthtime=now AND mtime=now. The `_changes` cursor path coerces a NULL file_mtime to 0.0 (server sync_routes_p1.py:326), and `to_systime` treats t<=0.0 as None (:142), so a note with a NULL DB file_mtime and no `created` re-clobbers to "now" on every materialize — exactly the N5 ctime-clobber that reordered the operator's Created-sorted list (cited in the code comment at :549-552). This is a data-quality/UX regression, not the edit-loss bug, but it is live.

FINDING 3 — birthtime restore is correct but silent on failure (I65, acceptable). `set_created` is gated `#[cfg(any(target_os = \"macos\", windows))]` (materializer.rs:155-158) and uses `std::os::darwin::fs::FileTimesExt` (:38) which writes ATTR_CMN_CRTIME via setattrlist — correct per I65. Best-effort by design (:159-168 warn-not-fail). No bug, but combined with Finding 2 the macOS birthtime is only as good as the (wrong) source field.

FINDING 4 — `restore_server_times` runs AFTER echo_guard.record but its own metadata write is correctly filtered (I41, no bug, but fragile). The `set_times` call (:553, after the write) mutates inode metadata, which on macOS FSEvents surfaces as `Modify(Metadata)`; `is_mutating_kind` drops exactly `Access(_)` and `Modify(Metadata(_))` (file_watcher.rs:985-992). So the time-restore does not itself generate a spurious push — GOOD. The fragility: FSEvents/the debouncer may report the coalesced rename+settimes as a coarse `Modify(Any)` or `Modify(Data)` (which `is_mutating_kind` deliberately KEEPS, fail-open at :983), and that kept event then relies entirely on the echo-guard (Finding 1) to suppress it. So the two macOS mechanisms compound: the same coalescing that makes is_mutating_kind keep the event is what makes the echo-guard consume-on-hit risky.

findings.recommended_fix:
Charter-scoped (macOS/watcher/materializer) fixes; the reconcile-policy fix (decide_direction + I32/I39 ServerWins->NewerWins+always-stash) is the reconcile specialist's and is the primary cure for the LIVE BUG.

FIX 1 (I40, highest value in my charter) — make echo suppression match-on-IDENTITY, not just (path,sha), and stop consuming-on-hit blindly. Options, preferred first:
 (a) Record the materialized file's post-write IDENTITY (dev+inode+mtime_ns via fstat after persist) in the echo guard, and in the watcher suppress ONLY if the observed file's inode+mtime still equal the recorded materialize identity. A genuine user save replaces the inode (atomic editors) or bumps mtime_ns, so it can never collide with the recorded identity even if bytes match. This kills the coalescing false-positive.
 (b) If keeping sha-based: do NOT `m.remove(path)` on hit; instead keep the entry until TTL and additionally require that NO local push for that path was already in flight. Consuming-on-hit is what lets the FIRST (echo) event clear the guard while a coalesced edit slips a SECOND time — but more importantly under coalescing there is only ONE event, so identity (option a) is the robust fix.
 (c) Shorten ECHO_TTL is NOT sufficient and is risky (slow disk/large note need the 15s); leave TTL, fix the match key.

FIX 2 (I64) — restore mtime from the field the contract names, with a correct fallback chain, and NEVER leave the inode at 'now'. Change `restore_server_times` to derive mtime from `payload.modified` (parse RFC3339 + naive, the `apply_canonical_times` the spec/I64 references) and fall back file_mtime -> created -> (last resort) DO NOT early-return: if all canonical times are absent, set mtime to `created` if present else leave the file but emit a WARN with the path so the 'now'-clobber is visible, rather than silently re-sorting the operator's list. Also stop treating file_mtime==0.0 as a hard None at the daemon: the server should send NULL (omit) not 0.0 (sync_routes_p1.py:326) so the daemon's `created` fallback engages. Add a macOS e2e test (the spec flags I65 per-platform birthtime e2e as MISSING, line 669) asserting that a note with NULL file_mtime but a frontmatter `created` lands with birthtime==created, not now.

FIX 3 (I41 hardening) — add a regression test that materialize-then-settimes under the real FSEvents backend produces ZERO journal pushes (currently only `#[ignore]`d smoke coverage exists, fs_integration_smoke). This pins that the set_times metadata event stays dropped and that the rename event is echo-suppressed by the (fixed) identity guard.
findings.cross_os_notes: macOS-specific risk surface vs Linux/Windows: (1) FSEvents (notify=7 default backend on darwin) coalesces multiple per-path events within the debouncer window into ONE event carrying only the file's final bytes — Linux inotify emits a discrete event per write (IN_CLOSE_WRITE/IN_MODIFY), so the echo-guard consume-on-hit false-positive (Finding 1) is materially MORE likely on macOS than on Linux. (2) Birthtime: only macOS (setattrlist ATTR_CMN_CRTIME, correctly gated materializer.rs:155-158) and Windows restore birthtime; Linux is kernel-read-only (I65) so the Finding-2 'now'-clobber only manifests as a *birthtime* regression on macOS/Windows — and macOS is the user-facing 'Created' sort in Obsidian (Trinity), so this is the platform where it actually hurts the operator. (3) `is_mutating_kind` is validated against both inotify kinds and 'coarse kinds some non-inotify backends (FSEvents/Windows) emit' (file_watcher.rs:1746-1749) and fails OPEN on Modify(Any) — correct for not dropping real edits, but it shifts all suppression weight onto the echo guard specifically on FSEvents. The atomic tmp+rename (I66) creating a new APFS inode is the reason birthtime restoration is needed at all and is the reason an inode-identity echo key (Fix 1a) would be robust: every materialize has a known new inode, every user atomic-save has a different one.
findings.residual_risks: (1) Fix 1 (identity-based echo) does not help an editor that writes IN-PLACE (no rename, same inode) AND produces bytes equal to the server's — then inode matches and mtime_ns must be the discriminator; if the in-place write somehow preserves mtime_ns (extremely unlikely) the edit could still be suppressed. Mitigation: also compare size+mtime_ns, and treat ANY mtime_ns advance past the recorded value as a real edit. (2) My charter cannot close the LIVE BUG alone: even with echo-guard fixed, the reconcile backstop `decide_direction` (verify_repair.rs:240-256) still Pulls (silently overwrites) any drift where the shadow marker is absent/stale, and the materializer ServerWins path (materializer.rs:486-519) overwrites Class-C divergent local with no stash (I39). Those are the reconcile/conflict specialist's I32/I39 revisions per the operator ruling; the macOS write path I audited is the *executor* of that overwrite, not its decider. (3) The shadow-store population depends on the materializer's `sh.record` (materializer.rs:601-603) running on every in-sync write; if a file is edited locally before it was ever materialized this session, no shadow entry exists -> reconcile Pulls it -> revert. That gap is upstream of my modules. (4) I did not build/run the daemon (no cargo invocation); findings are from static read of v0.4.22 source + the server payload contract. Confidence is medium because the echo-guard false-positive requires a same-sha coalesced edit, which I reason is reachable under FSEvents but did not reproduce live; the I64 mtime-source mismatch and 'now'-clobber fallthrough are high-confidence from code, but whether real notes hit the NULL-file_mtime+no-created case depends on DB data I did not query.

findings.failure_class: Silent server-wins data loss on non-ASCII filenames via NFC/NFD path-form mismatch (shadow-store key miss → drift falls to PULL); plus inotify watch-exhaustion blind spot for vault growth after startup. Linux birthtime soft-fail and ext4 atomic rename are CORRECT.
findings.root_cause:
PRIMARY (cross-OS, directly explains the LIVE BUG class for any accented/emoji filename): the daemon performs NO Unicode normalization (NFC/NFD) ANYWHERE. `grep -riE 'nfc|nfd|precompos|decompos|unicode.norm'` over src-tauri/src is empty, and Cargo.toml has no `unicode-normalization`/`icu` crate. Every path is taken verbatim from the OS via `to_string_lossy()` and only `replace('\\',"/")` is applied (verify_repair.rs:799-801 `path_to_forward_slash`; file_watcher.rs:604/609 `normalize_path`; scope.rs matching is byte-literal / ASCII-only `eq_ignore_ascii_case`). 

Mechanism of silent revert on link/Fedora: macOS (Trinity) writes filenames in NFD; the server stores whatever byte-form it received. The persistent shadow-store (sync_shadow.rs) is keyed by the wire/payload path string at materialize/push time (materializer.rs:479-481,601-603 `sh.record(&payload.path, ...)`). The reconcile backstop builds its local manifest from `walkdir` over ext4, which returns the on-disk directory-entry bytes verbatim (ext4 is byte-preserving, NOT normalizing), then looks up `shadow.get(&delta.path)` where delta.path echoes that manifest byte-form (verify_repair.rs:359). If the on-disk form (e.g. NFD from a macOS-origin note, or NFC from a Linux-origin edit) differs by even one combining-mark vs the form the shadow was recorded under, `shadow.get()` returns None. `decide_direction("drift", _, server_hash, shadow=None)` then returns `Direction::Pull` (verify_repair.rs:241-252) and the materializer server-wins-overwrites the locally-edited file with NO stash (Class C under ServerWins; materializer.rs:486-519). That is precisely the reported failure: locally-edited file silently reverted to the server's older copy within one ~600s recon tick, no conflict file, no warning. A NEWLY-CREATED local file survives because it is `missing-on-server` → `Direction::Push` (verify_repair.rs:253), which matches the operator's observation exactly. The same key-mismatch also defeats the I39/I32 ServerWins-vs-stash policy regardless of any policy revision, because the diverged file is never even recognized as the same file.

SECONDARY (Linux-specific, charter item): inotify watch-limit exhaustion is detected ONLY at the initial recursive `debouncer.watch(&vault_root, RecursiveMode::Recursive)` call (file_watcher.rs:681-690, mapping notify::ErrorKind::MaxFilesWatch → InotifyLimitExceeded). notify/inotify allocates ONE watch descriptor per directory; a large Mainframe vault that GROWS new directories AFTER startup can exhaust `fs.inotify.max_user_watches` at IN_CREATE-driven dynamic `inotify_add_watch` time. notify surfaces that as a per-event error batch routed to `Some(Err(errors))` which only does `tracing::warn!` and continues (file_watcher.rs:746-751) — new subdirectories then silently receive NO watch and their edits never enqueue a push (a push-side analog of FM1/FM2 for non-ASCII-free vaults too). There is no periodic watch-count health probe against the procfs limit.

CONFIRMED-CORRECT for this charter (NOT bugs): (a) I65 Linux birthtime soft-fail — `restore_server_times` gates set_created behind `#[cfg(any(target_os="macos",windows))]` and restores mtime-only on Linux; it reopens best-effort and only `warn!`s on failure, never returns Err, so the byte-faithful write is never aborted (materializer.rs:150-168). (b) ext4 atomic rename — `NamedTempFile::new_in(parent)` + `tmp.persist(&target)` keeps tmp on the same ext4 filesystem (no cross-device EXDEV) and resolves to `rename(2)`, which is atomic on ext4 (materializer.rs:536-544). (c) FM2 missing-local IS fixed at v0.4.22: when `target.exists()` is false the conflict block is skipped and the code falls through to create_dir_all + atomic write (materializer.rs:469-544), and the SSE/catchup path invokes `materializer.write` on UPSERT (sse.rs:271-274) — a Nexus-origin note DOES land on a Linux subscriber.

findings.recommended_fix:
1. (ROOT FIX, cross-OS) Introduce a single canonical path-form for ALL keys/wire/lookups and apply it at every boundary. Add the `unicode-normalization` crate and a `fn canonical_sync_path(s: &str) -> String { s.nfc().collect::<String>().replace('\\',"/") }`. Apply NFC at: (a) file_watcher `normalize_path` output (file_watcher.rs:604,609) so PUSH paths are NFC; (b) `path_to_forward_slash` in verify_repair.rs:799-801 so the reconcile manifest + delta keys are NFC; (c) shadow-store `record`/`get` callers (materializer.rs:479-481,601-603; verify_repair.rs:359) so the key form is identical regardless of OS or origin. NFC is the correct target (Linux/Windows-native, and what the server should canonicalize to per I35). CRITICAL caveat: on macOS the on-DISK name stays NFD, so when MATERIALIZING you must still write to the NFD on-disk path while KEYING the shadow in NFC — i.e. normalize keys, not the filesystem target. This makes the shadow lookup hit, so a genuine local edit is correctly classified Push instead of silently Pulled.
2. (DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH, honors the binding operator ruling) Make the drift arm NEVER silently overwrite: in `decide_direction`, when state=="drift" and shadow==None, do NOT default to Pull. Either (a) flip the daemon default conflict policy to NewerWins+always-stash for the reconcile-executed pulls, or (b) before the materializer server-wins-overwrites in the pull pass (verify_repair.rs:478-480), force a stash of the local bytes regardless of Class (pass a policy that is != ServerWins, or call ConflictStash::write_stash unconditionally). This guarantees the local revision is preserved as a `.conflict-from-<device>-<ts>.md` sibling and logged loudly even if the NFC fix is incomplete. This directly satisfies the I39/I32 revision: under the ruling, Class C must STASH-on-overwrite, not silently overwrite.
3. (Linux inotify) Add a periodic watch-health probe: compare a running count of active watches (or the size of the recursive dir set) against `read_inotify_limit()` on the recon timer; if usage crosses ~80% emit the same structured InotifyLimitExceeded surface + tray banner the startup path uses (file_watcher.rs:166), and recommend `fs.inotify.max_user_watches=524288`. Optionally treat a `notify::ErrorKind::MaxFilesWatch` arriving in the `Some(Err(errors))` batch (file_watcher.rs:746) as a loud, fenced condition rather than a silent warn. Document the sysctl in the Fedora install path.
4. (Minor, ext4 residual) `soft_delete` timestamp suffix uses second-granularity `%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ` (materializer.rs:666); two deletes of the same basename within one second on ext4 collide and the second `fs::rename` clobbers the first stash. Add sub-second/nonce to the suffix. Use no em-dashes in any emitted vault content per the ruling.
findings.cross_os_notes: macOS persists filenames in NFD (decomposed); Linux/ext4 and Windows/NTFS store and return whatever bytes were written, byte-for-byte, with NO normalization (ext4 is not a normalizing filesystem; case-sensitive). The daemon has zero NFC/NFD handling, so the SAME logical note can carry NFD bytes (macOS-origin, synced down to link) and NFC bytes (link-origin edit) for non-ASCII names. Because the shadow-store key, the reconcile manifest key, and the push/materialize wire path are all the raw OS byte-form, ANY non-ASCII filename can desync the shadow lookup across hosts and trigger the silent server-wins revert. This is the cross-OS amplifier of the LIVE BUG: it will bite hardest on link/Neo for notes whose canonical copy was authored on Trinity (NFD), and on Trinity for notes authored on link (NFC). Case-sensitivity: ext4 is case-sensitive while APFS (default) and NTFS are case-insensitive — a note renamed only by case on macOS/Windows can produce a second distinct file on Linux and a phantom create/delete pair; not the reported bug but a latent cross-OS divergence with the same no-normalization root. I81 (UTF-8 end-to-end) is satisfied for byte fidelity (to_string_lossy + UTF-8), but I81/I35 say nothing about a canonical NORMALIZATION form, which is the actual gap. I62 path-safety (ASCII `..`/drive checks) is unaffected by NFD bytes and remains correct. The materializer must normalize the KEY (NFC) while still writing to the platform-native on-disk path form (NFD on macOS) so it does not create duplicate decomposed/precomposed files on disk.
findings.residual_risks: (1) Applying NFC at boundaries requires a one-time shadow-store migration: existing shadow keys written in NFD must be re-normalized to NFC or they will all miss once on the next recon, causing a wave of server-wins Pulls — exactly the data-loss event we are preventing. Migrate the shadow JSON (sync_shadow.rs load path) by NFC-normalizing every key on first load under the new version, AND ship fix #2 (always-stash) FIRST/together so any residual miss preserves the local copy. (2) The server side must agree on NFC as the canonical form (I35 normalize-on-ingest); if the server stores NFD for some notes and NFC for others, the daemon's NFC keys will still mismatch `delta.path` (which echoes the server's stored form, not the daemon's manifest form) — verify the server reconcile-batch echoes back the CLIENT-sent path, not its own stored path, or normalize delta.path on receipt too. (3) The inotify probe needs an accurate active-watch count; notify 7 does not expose it directly, so it must be approximated from the recursive directory enumeration, which can drift from the kernel's real count. (4) NFC normalization has a small per-path CPU cost on the recon hot walk (~28K files); negligible but should be measured. (5) Did not build/run the daemon on Fedora in this audit; findings are from source + the Sync Contract. Recommend the nexus-sync-postupdate-test with a deliberately non-ASCII filename (e.g. an accented title authored on Trinity, edited on link) as the e2e regression gate.

findings.role: Windows filesystem expert (Neo host). Audited the v0.4.22 vault-sync daemon (branch fix/sync-hardening-s511) for Windows correctness by CODE-READING + the existing unit tests only — Neo is DOWN, so NO live Windows e2e was possible. Every Windows runtime claim below (ReadDirectoryChangesW behavior, MoveFileExW/ReplaceFileW semantics, SetFileTime, \\?\ long-path, Obsidian file-locking) is verified against the Rust std / crate source contracts and the daemon code paths, NOT against a running Neo. This MUST be re-verified e2e once Neo boots.
findings.failure_class: Silent data loss on reconciliation of a both-sides file where local diverged (server-wins overwrite with no stash), plus Windows-specific atomicity/long-path/locking gaps in the materializer write path. The live bug (TKT-2dc9a17e) is platform-agnostic in its ROOT but its physical overwrite is performed by Windows-relevant code (tempfile::persist => MoveFileExW; restore_server_times => SetFileTime).
findings.root_cause: THE SILENT-REVERT ENGINE (this IS TKT-2dc9a17e, and it fires identically on Windows): verify_repair::decide_direction (verify_repair.rs:235-257) decides PUSH vs PULL for a "drift" delta (local content hash != server fs_hash) SOLELY from the shadow store: it returns Direction::Push only when `shadow_hash == server_hash` (both Some and equal), otherwise Direction::Pull. The shadow store (sync_shadow.rs) is a flat path->last-synced-server-hash JSON that (a) loads EMPTY on a missing OR corrupt file (load(), lines 57-85, "degrades to pull-on-drift"), and (b) only ever gains an entry when THIS daemon process materializes or successfully pushes that path (materializer.rs:601-603, push_client.rs:494-503). Therefore ANY file that the user edits locally but that this daemon has NOT (re)written since the shadow store was created/cleared has shadow_hash == None => decide_direction returns Pull. run() then EXECUTES the pull (verify_repair.rs:463-512): fetch_note + materializer.write(server_payload). In write(), the on-disk local bytes differ from the server canonical => compare_local_to_canonical returns Diverges (materializer.rs:484), and because conflict_policy defaults to ServerWins (materializer.rs:191-198) and a normal note is Class C (conflict_stash.rs:125-142), should_stash is false (materializer.rs:487-488) => the branch at 513-519 logs "server-wins: overwriting non-class-D divergent local" and OVERWRITES with NO stash, NO conflict copy, NO warning surfaced to the user. The locally newer edit is destroyed within one reconcile tick (default 600s, but also on the startup sweep / SSE-driven path). A newly-created local file SURVIVES because reconcile-batch returns "missing-on-server" => Direction::Push (line 253), which is why the operator observed the asymmetry exactly. This is the policy pair I32 (server CAS = ServerWins) + I39 (stash matrix: ServerWins + Class C = OVERWRITE, no stash) doing precisely what they say — and both are flagged POLICY/open-for-revision in the spec §16, which the operator ruling (NEWER-WINS + ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE) now overrides. The shadow store makes it WORSE on a fresh/migrated Windows install (Neo): a brand-new or wiped %LocalAppData%\Nexus\.lattice-runtime\<sub>\shadow.json means EVERY drift => Pull, so the first reconcile sweep after install can mass-revert every locally-edited-while-offline note on Neo.
