findings.recommended_fix:
Two layers; the policy layer is the actual fix for TKT-2dc9a17e, the Windows layer hardens the write path.

A) POLICY FIX (implements the binding operator ruling NEWER-WINS + ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE; revises I32-daemon-side / I39):
  1. In verify_repair::decide_direction, REMOVE the unconditional Pull on a drift with absent/stale shadow. When shadow_hash is None (unknown) the file MUST be treated as a possible local edit: route to a CONFLICT resolution that PRESERVES BOTH sides, never a silent Pull. Concretely: drift + shadow==server => Push (local edit, unchanged); drift + shadow!=server (both known) => CONFLICT (stash local, then materialize server); drift + shadow==None => CONFLICT (UNKNOWN provenance — must stash local before any overwrite). Never return Pull for a both-sides-present diverged file without stashing first.
  2. In materializer::write, change the should_stash gate so that under the NEWER-WINS policy (make it the DEFAULT, not ServerWins) a Class C divergence ALWAYS writes a .conflict-from-<host>-<ts> stash of the LOCAL bytes before overwriting (conflict_stash.rs already supports this; the materializer currently only stashes for Class D or non-ServerWins). Wire local_lsn through so NewerWins can compare; when local_lsn is unknown, treat local as a conflict and stash (matches conflict_stash.rs:225-228 'unknown local lsn => older => stash'). Emit a LOUD warn! (not info!) on every overwrite-with-stash and bump tray.conflict_unresolved.
  3. Flip MaterializerConfig::default().conflict_policy from ServerWins to NewerWins and set the daemon wire-up (lib.rs) accordingly; keep Class D always-stash. Use NO em-dashes in any emitted log/stash text per the constraint.
  4. Concurrency safety (~15 writers): the resolver decision is per-path and the stash filename is device+lsn+collision-suffixed (conflict_stash.rs:315-336), so two hosts overwriting the same note each leave a distinct .conflict-from-<host>-<lsn>[-n].md — safe. Keep the atomic tmp+rename for the stash itself.

B) WINDOWS HARDENING (materializer.rs write path; needed before trusting Neo):
  5. Replace the bare tempfile::persist with a Windows-aware atomic replace: on cfg(windows), prefer ReplaceFileW (preserves the destination's ACLs, attributes, and is the documented atomic in-place replace) with a MoveFileExW(MOVEFILE_REPLACE_EXISTING) fallback; on a sharing violation (Obsidian holding the file) retry with a short bounded backoff, then surface a real error (do NOT leave a .tmp orphan). Note: the current MoveFileExW failure under an Obsidian lock actually PREVENTS the silent revert on Windows by erroring — but it currently propagates as an opaque MaterializerError::Io and the pull is just retried next sweep, so the policy fix (A) is still required.
  6. Long-path: prefix the materialize/stash target (and the config vaults_root used by push_client/materializer) with \\?\ via a dunce::simplified/`\\?\`-canonicalize helper (add dunce as a DIRECT dep — it is already in the tree) so notes whose absolute path exceeds 260 chars do not silently fail CreateFileW on a host without LongPathsEnabled. Today there is ZERO \\?\ handling for the daemon's own paths.
  7. Make canonical_root_for_mode / the materializer traversal guard robust to the \\?\-vs-non-\\?\ mismatch: canonicalize BOTH operands the same way (or compare after dunce::simplified) so a long-path canonicalize-fallback can't false-reject a legitimate deep write.
  8. Keep using to_string_lossy()->replace('\\','/') for wire paths (I81 is clean); do NOT introduce any String::from_utf8 of an OsStr or any codepage conversion.
findings.cross_os_notes: Linux/macOS: tempfile::persist => rename(2) which IS atomic and tolerates an open reader, so the silent-revert (policy bug A) DOES fire on mac/Linux with no friction (matches the live Trinity report). Windows is DIFFERENT in two ways: (1) tempfile::persist => MoveFileExW, which is non-atomic vs an open handle and FAILS with ERROR_SHARING_VIOLATION when Obsidian holds the note open with a deny-write/deny-delete share — so on Windows the overwrite may be BLOCKED (an opaque Io error + silent retry next sweep) rather than succeeding, which paradoxically makes the data-loss window narrower on Windows but introduces an untested failure/retry path. (2) MoveFileExW does NOT preserve the destination file's ACLs/attributes/compression (ReplaceFileW does) — every server-driven write on NTFS silently resets the note's security descriptor and attributes. notify 7.0 on Windows uses ReadDirectoryChangesW, which never emits the inotify Access(Open)/Modify(Metadata) kinds that file_watcher::is_mutating_kind (v0.4.9) was written to drop, so the read-walk-storm filter (I41) is a no-op on Windows and is UNPROVEN on Neo. \\\\?\\ long-path is unhandled everywhere for the daemon's own paths (dunce is transitive-only). I81 is the one Windows area that is genuinely clean.
findings.residual_risks: 1) Neo is DOWN: every Windows runtime assertion here is from code/crate-contract reading, not live e2e — the MoveFileExW-vs-Obsidian-lock behavior, SetFileTime birthtime on real NTFS, and \\\\?\\ long-path failures MUST be confirmed on a booted Neo. 2) The shadow store can be wiped/corrupted independently of code (disk fault, partial write, manual %LocalAppData% clear) and EACH such event re-arms the mass-revert on the next sweep until the policy fix (A) lands; the fix must be safe even with an empty shadow. 3) If only the policy default is flipped but lib.rs wiring still constructs the materializer/verify_repair with an explicit ServerWins, the bug persists — verify the actual runtime wiring, not just the Default impl. 4) The ReplaceFileW change (B5) touches the most safety-critical line in the daemon; it needs its own Windows unit + e2e test (write while a second handle holds the file) before ship. 5) ~15 concurrent writers: confirm the stash-collision-suffix loop (conflict_stash.rs:328) cannot livelock under many same-second conflicts on the same path across hosts.

findings.role: External-precedent researcher: how Obsidian Sync (the system this vault natively uses) handles cross-device conflict resolution + data safety, to inform the never-lose-data fix.
findings.failure_class: Reference / comparative analysis (not a code-path failure). Maps Obsidian Sync's behavior onto FM-class silent-overwrite/resurrection failures.
findings.root_cause:
SUMMARY OF OBSIDIAN SYNC (answers to the 5 charter questions):

(1) CONCURRENT EDITS TO THE SAME NOTE -- Obsidian Sync is NOT last-writer-wins for notes. It does a real CONTENT MERGE: Markdown files are merged with Google's diff-match-patch performing a THREE-WAY MERGE (base + each device's edits) to combine concurrent changes. Only NON-markdown/binary/Canvas files use "last modified wins" (most-recent-mtime replaces earlier). Settings JSON uses key-merge (local keys applied on top of remote). So for the exact case in our LIVE BUG (a .md edited on both sides), Obsidian's default is auto-merge-keep-both-edits, never "server's older copy silently wins."

(2) SILENT LOSS + HOW CONFLICTS SURFACE -- Two user-selectable modes (Settings -> Sync -> Conflict resolution, v1.9.7+): (a) "Automatically merge" (default) -- combines all changes into one file; the only downside documented is "may create duplicate text or formatting issues that require manual cleanup" i.e. it ERRS TOWARD KEEPING TOO MUCH, never dropping an edit; (b) "Create conflict file" -- writes a SEPARATE sibling file rather than merging, named `<note> (Conflicted copy <device-name> <YYYYMMDDHHMM>).md`. Both modes preserve both sides; neither silently reverts a divergent local file to the server copy. (Caveat: the conflict-mode setting is DEVICE-LOCAL and must be set on every device.) The ONLY documented Obsidian silent-data-loss path is deletions (see Q4), not content edits.

(3) VERSION HISTORY / FILE RECOVERY -- TWO independent layers. (a) Server-side Sync Version History: the server "regularly checks for updates ... keeping a record of any changes"; every synced version is retained server-side (Standard plan 1 month, Plus 12 months; attachments 2 weeks regardless), point-in-time restore per file via "Open version history" -> pick version -> Restore. Deleted notes restorable via Sync -> Deleted files -> View -> Restore ("to its original location"), bulk-select supported. (b) Local File Recovery core plugin: per-device local snapshots, independent of Sync, survives even if Sync mangles a file. This is the safety net that makes an aggressive merge tolerable -- a bad merge is always reversible.

(4) OFFLINE + RECONNECT + DELETIONS -- Offline edits are queued locally and replay+merge on reconnect (works fully offline by design). DELETIONS ARE OBSIDIAN'S WEAK SPOT and a documented, confirmed BUG (forum 113242): Obsidian Sync has NO durable deletion tombstones. When an offline device reconnects holding a local file that another device deleted while it was offline, "Sync treats it as a new local creation and uploads it, rather than recognising a conflict between 'exists locally' and 'was deleted remotely.'" ~80 deleted files silently RESURRECTED in the reported case; moved files duplicated at old paths. Reporter's diagnosis: "Remote deletions are indistinguishable from 'file never existed remotely.'" Recommended fix in-thread: a durable deletion log of (path, timestamp) retained for a window.

(5) WHAT IS DIRECTLY ADOPTABLE -- see recommended_fix.

WHY THIS VALIDATES OUR FIX DIRECTION: Obsidian -- the reference implementation for this very vault -- chose merge-or-stash-both, NEVER silent server-wins, for content. That is exactly the operator ruling (NEWER-WINS + ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE). Our I39 Class-C-under-ServerWins = OVERWRITE (no stash) is STRICTLY LESS SAFE than even Obsidian's least-safe content path. The LIVE BUG (TKT-2dc9a17e) is the predictable result of a policy Obsidian deliberately does not ship. Conversely, the FM2 'materializer never creates a missing local file' gap is a defect Obsidian does NOT have (Nexus-origin notes would land); and our I74 tombstone design is something Obsidian notably LACKS and gets bitten by.

evidence.quote: Markdown files employ automatic merging using Google's diff-match-patch algorithm ... performs 'a three-way merge to combine conflicting changes' ... For binary files and Canvas documents ... 'The most recently modified version replaces earlier versions.'

As of v1.9.7+ ... 1. Automatic Merge (default): 'Combines changes. May create duplicate text or formatting issues that require manual cleanup.' 2. Create Conflict File: naming pattern: 'original-note-name (Conflicted copy device-name YYYYMMDDHHMM).md'
Changes made while a device is offline are queued locally and automatically sync when the device reconnects to the internet and Obsidian is open.

Standard plan: Notes retained for '1 month'; Plus plan: '12 months'; Attachments: '...two weeks' regardless of plan. ... Deleted files ... 'Deleted files' > 'View' ... restore ... 'to its original location in the filesystem.' ... 'a local version history is also available on each device if the File Recovery plugin is enabled' -- distinct from cloud-based Sync history.

Obsidian Sync lacks durable deletion tracking (tombstones). 'When Device B reconnects and finds a local file with no remote counterpart, it assumes the file is a new local creation and uploads it. Remote deletions are indistinguishable from "file never existed remotely."' ... 'Sync treats it as a new local creation and uploads it, rather than recognising a conflict between exists locally and was deleted remotely.' ~80 previously-deleted files reappeared.

Remote-vault architecture, per-file change tracking over WebSocket (wss://sync-xx.obsidian.md); AES-256-GCM E2EE; 'changes are synced as files are saved'; diff-match-patch three-way merge for Markdown, last-modified-wins for non-Markdown.

I39 matrix: '| C (content) | OVERWRITE | STASH | STASH if local_lsn < server_lsn else OVERWRITE |' -- our ServerWins Class-C path OVERWRITES with no stash, which is LESS safe than Obsidian's least-safe content path (which still merges or writes a Conflicted-copy).

I73/I74: 'Server delete = row removal, not a tombstone flag' but 'I74 Offline-delete tombstones: an AFTER DELETE trigger snapshots into [vault_deleted_notes] ... replays tombstones recreate-safely' -- Nexus DESIGNED the durable deletion log that Obsidian Sync lacks; this is the correct guard against the resurrection class.

findings.recommended_fix:
DIRECTLY ADOPTABLE FROM OBSIDIAN SYNC (ranked):

1. ADOPT NEWER-WINS-WITH-MERGE-OR-STASH AS THE DEFAULT, KILL SILENT SERVER-WINS FOR CONTENT. Obsidian's default for the exact failing case (.md edited on both sides) is a diff-match-patch THREE-WAY MERGE that keeps both edits; its fallback is a sibling Conflicted-copy file. Neither reverts the local edit. CONCRETE: flip I39 so Class C content NEVER OVERWRITEs a locally-divergent file without first stashing -- i.e. make the NewerWins/Manual column the only allowed behavior and retire the ServerWins=OVERWRITE cell (and re-examine I32's ServerWins-at-CAS for the case where shadow!=server, which is decision-table row 3 = genuine concurrent edit). This is exactly the binding operator ruling and is strictly safer than Obsidian.

2. ADOPT THREE-WAY MERGE FOR MARKDOWN, NOT BARE LAST-WRITER-WINS. We HAVE the base: sync_shadow.rs stores path->last-synced-server-hash (the merge base). With base + local + server we can run a 3-way merge (diff-match-patch / diffy / similar Rust crate) for .md, falling back to a `.conflict-<host>-<ts>` stash only when the merge is non-clean. This converts most of the ~15-concurrent-writer collisions into clean auto-merges instead of either data loss (current) or a blizzard of conflict files. Use last-writer-wins ONLY for binary/non-mergeable types, mirroring Obsidian.

3. KEEP THE CONFLICT-FILE FALLBACK + MAKE STASH LOUD. Obsidian's `<note> (Conflicted copy <device> <ts>).md` is the model for our `.conflict-<host>-<ts>` copy. ALWAYS create it on any overwrite of a divergent local file (operator's ALWAYS-STASH ruling), log loudly, and surface it (Obsidian shows conflicts in Sync history; we should at minimum WARN-log + emit a health signal so FM-class silence is detectable).

4. KEEP / HARDEN THE DURABLE DELETION TOMBSTONE (I74) -- this is where Nexus is AHEAD of Obsidian. Obsidian's confirmed resurrection bug is caused precisely by NOT having tombstones (local file with no remote counterpart -> re-uploaded). Our I74 AFTER-DELETE-trigger tombstone in vault_deleted_notes is the right design; ensure the daemon consults it so a reconnecting host's local-only file that matches a recent tombstone is treated as 'deleted remotely', not 'new local creation'. This closes the offline-delete-resurrection hole the charter's deletion scenarios worry about.

5. ADOPT A LOCAL, SYNC-INDEPENDENT RECOVERY SNAPSHOT (Obsidian's File Recovery plugin) AND/OR LEAN ON SERVER VERSION HISTORY. The reason Obsidian can ship an aggressive auto-merge at all is that BOTH a server-side version history (1-12 mo) AND per-device local snapshots exist -- any bad merge is reversible. For never-lose-data: ensure the server retains prior versions of vault_notes.body before any overwrite (so a silent revert is always undoable), and consider a cheap pre-write local snapshot in the daemon before materialize/stash. This is the backstop that makes #1-#3 safe.

NOT ADOPTABLE / AVOID: Obsidian's no-tombstone deletion model (causes resurrection) and its device-local conflict-mode setting (inconsistent fleet behavior). Set our policy server-side / uniformly, not per-host.
findings.cross_os_notes: Obsidian Sync runs the SAME diff-match-patch merge + Conflicted-copy + File-Recovery model on macOS, Windows, iOS, Android, Linux -- behavior is OS-uniform, which is the bar our Trinity/link/Neo fleet should meet (vs the current device-local conflict mode Obsidian uses, which we should NOT copy). Conflict-file naming embeds the device name to disambiguate origin across OSes; our `.conflict-<host>-<ts>` already follows this. diff-match-patch has mature ports in Rust (e.g. `diff_match_patch`/`dmp` crates) and is line/char based so it is OS-newline sensitive -- normalize CRLF/LF before merge so a Windows (Neo) vs macOS edit does not false-conflict on line endings.
findings.residual_risks: (a) Obsidian's source is closed; mechanism details come from official docs + DeepWiki (auto-generated from obsidian-help) + a reverse-engineered server -- the THREE-WAY-merge and last-modified-wins claims are consistently corroborated across all three and the official help, but exact tie-break/edge semantics (e.g. simultaneous-mtime, partial-line conflicts) are not publicly specified. (b) diff-match-patch auto-merge can still produce semantically-wrong-but-non-lossy output (duplicated paragraphs) -- acceptable under never-lose-data (nothing dropped) but needs the stash+version-history backstop so users can clean up. (c) The deletion-resurrection finding is a single (well-evidenced, git-backed) forum bug report, not official docs; treat the existence of the failure mode as confirmed and the absence-of-tombstones inference as highly likely but not officially stated. (d) Adopting 3-way merge adds a merge engine to the daemon (push/materialize hot path) -- must be deterministic and identical across hosts or two hosts could converge to different merged bytes; pin the crate + normalize inputs.

findings.role: External-system researcher: Syncthing conflict + data-safety model, adoptable patterns for a newer-wins + always-stash resolver
findings.failure_class: N/A (research charter, not a code-defect charter). The relevant LOCAL failure this research informs: silent overwrite of a locally-divergent file by the server copy (TKT-2dc9a17e) — a class Syncthing structurally cannot exhibit because it never resolves a true concurrent conflict by deletion of the loser.
findings.root_cause: Syncthing's data-safety guarantee rests on FOUR design choices we can adopt wholesale. (1) NEWER-NESS IS DECIDED BY VERSION VECTORS, NOT MODTIME. Each FileInfo carries a Vector = []Counter{ID ShortID; Value uint64}. Vector.Compare(b) returns one of five Orderings {Equal, Greater, Lesser, ConcurrentLesser, ConcurrentGreater}. The algorithm walks the merged set of device IDs once, tracking a running `result`; the moment it sees one side higher on device X after the other was higher on device Y it flips to a Concurrent* variant. So 'newer' = strict per-device dominance: A is newer than B iff every counter of A >= the matching counter of B and at least one is strictly greater. Modification time is NOT used to decide newer-ness. (2) A CONFLICT IS PRECISELY 'NEITHER VECTOR DOMINATES' (Concurrent()=true). This is the structural definition of a conflict — two independent edits that each advanced a counter the other has not seen. It cannot be faked by clock skew because it is causal, not temporal. (3) ON A TRUE CONFLICT, BOTH SIDES ARE ALWAYS PRESERVED — THE LOSER IS RENAMED, NEVER DELETED. 'When a file has been modified on two devices simultaneously and the content actually differs, one of the files will be renamed to <filename>.sync-conflict-<date>-<time>-<modifiedBy>.<ext>.' Crucially, modtime + device-ID are used ONLY as the deterministic tiebreak for WHICH side gets the conflict suffix (cosmetic), never to decide whether to keep both: 'The file with the older modification time will be marked as the conflicting file and thus be renamed. If the modification times are equal, the file originating from the device which has the larger value of the first 63 bits for its device ID will be marked as the conflicting file.' Because the rename rule is a pure function of (modtime, deviceID), every device independently produces the SAME conflict filename → the conflict copy itself syncs and converges fleet-wide with no second-order conflict. (4) DELETE-VS-MODIFY: MODIFICATION ALWAYS WINS AND THE FILE IS RESURRECTED. 'If the conflict is between a modification and a deletion of the file, the modified file always wins and is resurrected without renaming on the device where it was deleted.' (Newer docs note the symmetric case where deletion wins the vector comparison; then the surviving modified content is still preserved AS a conflict copy rather than discarded.) Net: in NO conflict path does Syncthing destroy the only copy of edited bytes. THIS IS THE EXACT OPERATOR RULING (newer-wins + always-stash-on-overwrite). Our I39 ServerWins/Class-C cell = 'OVERWRITE' (no stash) is the direct violation: it deletes the divergent loser, which Syncthing never does — and is the structural enabler of TKT-2dc9a17e. SCANNING/INOTIFY RELIABILITY: Syncthing treats the FS watcher as a latency optimization, NOT a source of truth. The watcher (fsnotify/inotify) coalesces events for fsWatcherDelayS=10s and delays delete-events a further ~1min; even with the watcher ON it 'is advised to keep regular full scans enabled, as it is possible that some changes aren't picked up' — when the watcher is enabled the periodic full rescan is merely relaxed from 1min to 60min, and the per-folder interval is randomized to 3/4..5/4 of the configured value to avoid thundering-herd rescans. So inotify is best-effort; a periodic full reconcile is mandatory backstop. This directly validates keeping reconciliation.rs as a hard backstop and NOT trusting file_watcher.rs alone.

evidence.quote: When a file has been modified on two devices simultaneously and the content actually differs, one of the files will be renamed to <filename>.sync-conflict-<date>-<time>-<modifiedBy>.<ext>. The file with the older modification time will be marked as the conflicting file and thus be renamed. If the modification times are equal, the file originating from the device which has the larger value of the first 63 bits for its device ID will be marked as the conflicting file.
If the conflict is between a modification and a deletion of the file, the modified file always wins and is resurrected without renaming on the device where it was deleted.

Syncthing keeps track of several versions of each file - the version that it currently has on disk, called the local version ... and the 'best' (usually the most recent) version of the file. This version is called the global version.

const ( Equal Ordering = iota; Greater; Lesser; ConcurrentLesser; ConcurrentGreater ) ... Compare returns the Ordering that describes a's relation to b. ... Concurrent returns true when the two vectors are concurrent.

if av.Value > bv.Value { if result == Lesser { return ConcurrentLesser }; result = Greater } — Compare walks merged device IDs tracking a running `result`; a contradiction flips it to a Concurrent* variant. It does NOT use a sum or hash. Counter struct: type Counter struct { ID ShortID; Value uint64 }.

Neither vector dominates the other → concurrent state → conflict. e.g. 'Ansible's vector has FGFXVB4's counter but not ALMIVFD's or FQH6XCV's; the deletion vector has ALMIVFD's and FQH6XCV's counters but not FGFXVB4's' — neither dominates, so a conflict is declared. (Cautionary: stale/ghost counters that can never increment can manufacture permanent false conflicts — a version-vector pitfall to guard against.)

Syncthing detects changes by regular full scans and by filesystem notifications ('watcher'); the watcher accumulates changes for 10s (fsWatcherDelayS) and delays deleted files a further ~1min. Even with the watcher enabled it is advised to keep regular full scans enabled, as it is possible that some changes aren't picked up; with the watcher on the full rescan is relaxed to 60min, and the actual interval is randomized between 3/4 and 5/4 of the configured value.

The FS watcher uses OS primitives (inotify on Linux) to detect changes as soon as they happen, but is considered unreliable despite pretending to watch in real time — periodic full rescans remain the safety net.

| C (content) | OVERWRITE | STASH | STASH if `local_lsn < server_lsn` else OVERWRITE | — our I39 ServerWins column OVERWRITES Class C with NO stash; this is exactly the loser-deletion Syncthing structurally never performs, and the enabling policy behind TKT-2dc9a17e.

[I32] Conflict policy is ServerWins-at-the-CAS (stale base loses), with NO server-side merge — the suspected root invariant; Syncthing's model argues this should become newer-wins-with-mandatory-stash.

findings.recommended_fix:
DIRECTLY ADOPTABLE INTO OUR newer-wins + always-stash RESOLVER (verify_repair.rs::decide_direction + conflict_stash.rs + I32/I39):

1. ADOPT THE STRUCTURAL CONFLICT DEFINITION, NOT A TIME COMPARISON. Decide 'newer' by causal dominance, falling back to modtime ONLY when we lack a true version vector. Concretely: keep a per-path last-synced-server-hash in sync_shadow.rs (we already do). The 3-way test (local, server, shadow) IS a poor-man's version vector: shadow==server && local!=server => local strictly newer => PUSH (no stash); shadow!=server && local==server => server strictly newer => PULL; shadow!=server && local!=server && local!=server-content => CONCURRENT => this is Syncthing's Concurrent() == TRUE => MUST stash, never overwrite. This is already encoded in I37 row 3 ('CONFLICT: stash per I38/I39, materialize server canonical') — the bug is ONLY that the I39 ServerWins column lets row-3 collapse to OVERWRITE. FIX: delete the OVERWRITE option from Class C entirely; the matrix must be STASH (Manual) or 'newer-wins-with-stash' (NewerWins) — there is no safe no-stash cell for divergent content. This satisfies the binding operator ruling and closes TKT-2dc9a17e.

2. ADOPT SYNCTHING'S DETERMINISTIC, FLEET-CONVERGENT CONFLICT FILENAME. Make the stash name a PURE FUNCTION of (loser modtime, loser host/device id) so every host computes the identical name and the stash copy itself converges with no secondary conflict — exactly Syncthing's '.sync-conflict-<date>-<time>-<modifiedBy>' property. Our I38 name '<stem>.conflict-from-<device_id>-<lsn>.md' is close; ensure the chosen suffix is reproducible across hosts (prefer a stable monotonic field over a host-local LSN if cross-host reproducibility is wanted). Tiebreak which side carries the suffix by (older-modtime loses; equal-modtime → larger device-id loses) — copy Syncthing verbatim. CRITICAL for our '~15 concurrent agent writers' constraint: deterministic naming is what stops N conflicting writers from spawning N different conflict files that then re-conflict.

3. ADOPT 'MODIFICATION BEATS DELETION, AND PRESERVE THE BYTES.' For delete-vs-modify: if local was edited and server says deleted (tombstone), DO NOT delete locally — resurrect/keep the edited file (push it up), per Syncthing. If server has content and local deleted, materialize the server content. In the ambiguous case, write the surviving edited content as a conflict copy rather than honoring a destructive delete. Add this as an explicit row to the I37 decision table (currently absent) — delete-vs-modify is a known silent-loss vector (our N-incidents) and must not fall through to a bare tombstone apply.

4. KEEP THE FULL-RECONCILE BACKSTOP; TREAT inotify AS BEST-EFFORT. Do NOT let file_watcher.rs be the sole change source. Mirror Syncthing: coalesce watcher events (we can keep our debounce), but run reconciliation.rs on a periodic interval as a guaranteed backstop because the watcher provably drops events. Randomize the interval per-host (Syncthing's 3/4..5/4 jitter) so 3 hosts + the server don't reconcile-storm in lockstep (we have a documented reconciler-storm incident). Delay applying DELETE events a grace window (Syncthing uses ~60s) to absorb editor atomic-rename/rewrite churn and avoid spurious delete-then-recreate conflicts.

5. GUARD AGAINST THE GHOST-COUNTER / STALE-SHADOW PITFALL (Issue #10590). A version-vector scheme can manufacture PERMANENT false conflicts if a counter/shadow entry can never advance (e.g., a retired host, or a shadow entry that never gets refreshed after a 409). Ensure: after any stash+materialize, the shadow is updated to the NEW server hash so the same pair does not re-conflict forever; and prune shadow entries for paths the server has tombstoned. This prevents the 'thousands of false conflicts' failure mode in our heavy-concurrent-writer environment.

NET: revise I32 from 'ServerWins-at-CAS (no merge)' to 'newer-wins by causal/shadow dominance; on genuine concurrency, ALWAYS stash both sides with a deterministic cross-host filename; modification beats deletion'. Revise I39 to remove every OVERWRITE-without-stash cell for divergent content. Both changes are exactly Syncthing's proven, data-loss-free model and exactly the operator ruling.
findings.cross_os_notes: Syncthing runs the identical conflict model on macOS/Linux/Windows (our Trinity/link/Neo). Cross-OS gotchas it solved that we must match: (1) modtime granularity differs per FS (APFS ns, ext4 ns, NTFS 100ns, FAT 2s) — Syncthing uses a configurable modTimeWindow (ModTimeEqual) so two hosts don't see a file as 'changed' purely from timestamp rounding; if our resolver ever falls back to modtime, apply a tolerance window or we will generate phantom conflicts between macOS and Windows. (2) Filename case-folding / Unicode normalization (NFC vs NFD on macOS) can make 'the same' path look divergent — normalize paths before the 3-way compare (we already have a normalization step per I35; ensure conflict filenames are emitted post-normalization). (3) inotify (Linux) vs FSEvents (macOS) vs ReadDirectoryChangesW (Windows) have different drop/coalesce behavior — the mandatory periodic full reconcile (point 4) is what makes correctness OS-independent; do not tune it away on any single platform.
findings.residual_risks: (1) Our 3-way (local/server/shadow) test is a 1-bit-per-path approximation of a true version vector; it correctly detects 'both diverged since last sync' but cannot distinguish a fast-forward chain from genuine concurrency as precisely as Syncthing's per-device counters — acceptable because our fix-direction on ANY ambiguity is 'stash, never destroy', so approximation errs safe (extra conflict copies, never data loss). (2) Deterministic cross-host conflict filenames require a field both hosts agree on; if I38's <lsn> is host-local, two hosts may emit different suffixes for the same logical conflict (secondary, non-destructive duplication) — recommend a server-assigned or modtime-derived component. (3) Delete grace-window (60s) adds latency to legitimate deletes propagating; acceptable trade vs silent loss. (4) Ghost/stale shadow entries (Issue #10590 analog) can still wedge a path into perpetual conflict if shadow-refresh-after-resolve is not airtight — must be covered by a regression test. (5) I did not read the v0.4.22 Rust to confirm whether decide_direction currently honors the ServerWins OVERWRITE cell or already stashes — that verification belongs to the daemon-resolver specialist; this charter only establishes the target model. Present_in_v0422 left 'unknown' for that reason.

findings.role: Distributed-systems researcher — never-lose-data conflict resolver for the Nexus Sync vault (1 central server, ~15 concurrent writers, intermittently-connected Tauri clients)
findings.failure_class: Silent last-writer-wins overwrite of a locally-divergent file (data loss). Two distinct manifestations of the SAME root policy: (A) the LIVE SSE/materialize hot path overwrites diverged local content with NO 3-way divergence test and NO stash; (B) the reconcile backstop does a 3-way test but, under the ServerWins stash policy, still overwrites Class-C content with no conflict copy.
findings.root_cause:
The resolver violates the binding operator ruling (NEWER-WINS + ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE) in two places, both reducible to the I32/I39 ServerWins policy being the wired default.

1) THE LIVE BUG (TKT-2dc9a17e) is in the SSE-driven materializer hot path, NOT the reconcile backstop. `materializer.rs:484-518` handles `LocalCompare::Diverges`: it computes `should_stash = class == ConflictClass::D || self.config.conflict_policy != ConflictPolicy::ServerWins`. The production default is `ConflictPolicy::ServerWins` (materializer.rs:194), so for ordinary content (Class C — any note not under Credentials/ or Protocols/Infrastructure*|Bootstrap*) `should_stash` is FALSE. It logs "materializer server-wins: overwriting non-class-D divergent local" and overwrites. This is why a freshly-edited note was reverted to the server's older copy in ~30s with no conflict file and no warning. CRITICAL: this branch only knows local != server-bytes; it does NOT consult the shadow store and does NOT establish whether the LOCAL side is newer than the shadow (i.e. a genuine just-made local edit). It is pure unconditional server-wins on the live SSE path. A new local file with no server row never hits `target.exists()`, so it survives — exactly matching the observed asymmetry (created file survived, edited file lost).

2) THE BACKSTOP is correct in DIRECTION but wrong in PRESERVATION. `verify_repair::decide_direction` (verify_repair.rs:235-257) does the proper 3-way test (drift + shadow==server ⇒ Push local edit; drift + shadow!=server/absent ⇒ Pull). But when it Pulls, the stash decision `ConflictStash::decide` under ServerWins returns `stash:false, reason:"server-wins policy: local divergent revision silently overwritten"` for Class C (conflict_stash.rs:210-215). So even the backstop's "stale local" verdict throws away the loser with no conflict copy.

3) WHY THE 3-WAY MODEL IS ALREADY SUFFICIENT (the data structure is NOT the problem). The daemon already has the full Unison archive-style 3-way state: local hash, shadow hash (sync_shadow.rs — "last-synced server hash", get/record at :89-97), and server hash. Row 3 of the I37 table ("local != server AND shadow != server ⇒ CONFLICT") is the textbook concurrent-edit detector and is exactly what Unison's archive comparison does. The bug is NOT missing causality tracking; it is (a) the live materializer path never runs the I37 Row-3 test before overwriting, and (b) the policy applied at conflict time discards the loser. You do NOT need version vectors to fix this.

The two POLICY invariants flagged as suspect (I32 ServerWins-at-CAS; I39 Class-C OVERWRITE-no-stash under ServerWins) ARE the root and must be revised per the 2026-06-09 ruling.
invariants_involved: I32 (server conflict policy = ServerWins-at-CAS, no server merge) — REVISE: server CAS stays the concurrency-control primitive, but it must NEVER be read as license for the daemon to discard the local loser; the 409 path must route to stash, not overwrite

I37 (the 3-way shadow decision table: Row 3 local!=server && shadow!=server ⇒ CONFLICT) — KEEP; this is the correct & sufficient detector. The live materializer.write() path must be made to honor it before any overwrite

I39 (stash policy matrix; under ServerWins Class C = OVERWRITE with no stash) — REVISE: delete the ServerWins/Class-C OVERWRITE cell; the floor for every overwrite of divergent content is ALWAYS-STASH
I38 (stash mechanics: <stem>.conflict-from-<device>-<lsn>.md, atomic tmp+rename, traversal-guarded) — KEEP & REUSE as the loser-preservation primitive on every overwrite

I29/I30/I31 (server-hash CAS base, atomic critical section, idempotent push) — KEEP; CAS is the right server-side concurrency guard for ~15 writers, it just must not be paired with silent client-side overwrite
I40 (echo guard) — KEEP; unaffected

"materializer server-wins: overwriting non-class-D divergent local"); }  // LIVE bug: under default ServerWins, Class-C divergent local is overwritten with NO stash and NO shadow/newer test

shadow == current server hash ⇒ server hasn't moved since we synced ⇒ a local≠server diff can only be a genuine local edit ⇒ PUSH. shadow absent, or shadow ≠ current server hash ⇒ ... PULL  // the 3-way archive datum already exists; the live path just doesn't read it
LSN is now `change_seq` -- a monotonic, freeze-proof, wrap-proof sequence stamped on every vault-note content change  // a usable server-side LOGICAL CLOCK — use this, never mtime, to order revisions

Two replicas are concurrent when neither ordering holds and vectors aren't identical, meaning updates happened independently... Traditional timestamp-based approaches (last-modified after sync) cannot detect true concurrency; they only identify which update is newer. Version vectors explicitly identify whether updates are concurrent (potential conflicts) versus causally ordered.

Version Vectors (1983) track causal relations among data items that can be concurrently updated; they relate REPLICA STATES, not events — i.e. the right tool for replicated FILES (vs vector clocks, which track events in a computation).

Unison keeps a record (archives) of the contents of each path after each successful synchronization... it compares its archive (the state when last synchronized) with current contents to determine which paths have been updated; paths updated on BOTH replicas are flagged as conflicts, not overwritten.  // == the daemon's local/shadow/server 3-way model; the reference design FLAGS, never silently overwrites

Dropbox does this [conflicted copy] to prevent data loss, with the conflicted copy acting as a safety net, ensuring no edits disappear completely.  // industry floor: on concurrent edit, preserve BOTH as a renamed copy — exactly the operator's ALWAYS-STASH ruling

sync systems like Dropbox and iCloud flag conflicting edits when you come back online and duplicate files with new file names so there is no data loss.  // both never-lose by conflict-copy; this is the dominant safe model

Clock skew can cause last-write-wins to produce unexpected results... even with NTP in place, skews can be in a half-second range... the timestamps were indistinguishable -- precisely the scenario where last-writer-wins degenerates into arbitrary-writer-wins.  // why mtime-LWW is unsafe with ~15 writers; use change_seq, not mtime

the local modifications you had before you started merge will stay the same and the index entries for them stay as they were... working tree files contain 3-way merge results with <<< === >>> markers.  // git never discards either side on conflict; the loser is always recoverable

findings.recommended_fix:
ADOPT: keep the existing 3-way local/shadow/server (Unison-archive) model — it is SUFFICIENT and correct. Do NOT introduce version vectors or vector clocks: they add per-file O(replicas) integer state and a merge protocol you do not need, because there is exactly ONE authoritative server providing a total order (change_seq), so a per-file 3-way comparison fully detects concurrency (I37 Row 3). Version vectors only pay off in multi-master peer-to-peer topologies; here they are over-engineering. Use the server `change_seq` (sync_routes_p1.py:262) as the logical clock for ordering, NEVER filesystem mtime (clock-skew → arbitrary-writer-wins). The MINIMAL per-file state that guarantees no silent loss is the triple you already persist: { local_content_hash, shadow = last-synced server hash, server_content_hash } plus the server change_seq/lsn at last sync. No new state required.

THE ALGORITHM (one decision function, used by BOTH the live materializer path AND the backstop — unify them):

  given local_hash, shadow_hash (Option), server_hash, and on overwrite the server change_seq:
   1. local_hash == server_hash                      -> NOOP (idempotent; record shadow=server)
   2. shadow == server_hash, local != server         -> PUSH  (only local moved: genuine local edit goes UP — CAS base = server hash, I29)
   3. server != shadow, local == shadow              -> PULL  (only server moved: safe overwrite, local had no unsynced edit; no stash needed)
   4. local != server AND shadow != server AND local != shadow
                                                      -> CONFLICT (true 3-way divergence; both sides moved since last sync)
   5. shadow ABSENT, local != server                 -> CONFLICT (cannot prove local was synced; treat as concurrent — never assume server wins)

  On CONFLICT (cases 4 & 5) the INVARIANT is ALWAYS-STASH-THEN-RESOLVE, regardless of policy/class:
   a. ALWAYS write the LOSER to a conflict copy FIRST via the existing I38 mechanism: `<stem>.conflict-<host>-<change_seq>.md` (operator wording: .conflict-<host>-<ts>; use change_seq not wall-clock to dodge skew), atomic tmp+rename, traversal-guarded. This happens BEFORE any overwrite so a crash mid-op can never lose the loser.
   b. Decide the winner by NEWER-WINS using change_seq (server lsn vs the local edit's basis lsn). Materialize the winner to the canonical path.
   c. Log LOUDLY (warn!) with both paths + both hashes + a tray "unresolved conflict" increment so it is never silent.
   The result: the WINNER is at the canonical name, the LOSER is preserved as a conflict copy. Zero data loss even when "newer" picks wrong, because both bytes survive on disk. This satisfies the ruling: local edits propagate UP (case 2), and any overwrite preserves BOTH (cases 4/5).

CONCRETE PATCHES:
  P1 (fixes the LIVE bug, materializer.rs:486-518): DELETE the `policy != ServerWins` shortcut. The stash gate must be content-divergence-driven, not policy-driven: if `LocalCompare::Diverges` AND this is a genuine local divergence-since-shadow (look up shadow_store.get(path); diverged if shadow absent OR shadow != local), then ALWAYS stash the loser before overwrite. Only skip the stash when local == shadow (case 3, the local copy was a clean prior materialization with no unsynced edit). The live path currently never reads the shadow store — wire `self.shadow_store` into write() and pass local_lsn so the conflict copy is named by change_seq.
  P2 (conflict_stash.rs:209-215): REMOVE the ServerWins arm's `stash:false`. Make Class C under all modes return stash:true when local diverged from shadow. Equivalently: collapse ServerWins into "newer-wins + always-stash" so I39 has no OVERWRITE-no-stash cell. Keep Class D = always-stash.
  P3 (verify_repair.rs decide_direction): keep the Push/Pull direction logic, but change case-3 Pull so that when local != shadow (i.e. local had an unsynced edit) it routes through the stash-before-overwrite path rather than a bare materialize. Add the missing case-5 (shadow absent + drift ⇒ CONFLICT, not Pull) so a never-synced-but-present file is preserved, not clobbered.
  P4 (default flip): set `conflict_policy: ConflictPolicy::NewerWins` as the production default (materializer.rs:194) and treat ServerWins as removed/aliased to NewerWins+stash. This makes the binding ruling the default rather than an opt-in.
  CONCURRENCY (~15 writers): the server CAS (I29/I30, FOR UPDATE row lock, re-read-in-txn) remains the serialization point — that is the right primitive and needs no change. Under heavy concurrent writers a stale push gets a 409; route that 409 to refetch→stash-loser→newer-wins→replay (I33), NOT to overwrite and NOT to infinite retry. The stash filename keyed by change_seq (monotonic, freeze/wrap-proof) guarantees collision-free conflict copies even when many sessions conflict on the same path in the same second (where mtime/wall-clock would collide). Reuse the existing `-2/-3` suffix collision guard as a backstop.

No em-dashes will appear in any vault content the fix emits (conflict-copy filenames use plain hyphens: .conflict-<host>-<change_seq>.md).
findings.cross_os_notes: change_seq is a server-side integer, identical on macOS/Trinity, Linux/link, Windows/Neo — so newer-wins ordering is OS-independent and immune to per-host clock skew (the reason to never use file mtime: NTFS vs APFS vs ext4 mtime resolution and timezone/skew differ, and ~15 writers across 3 OSes make mtime ties common). Conflict-copy filenames must avoid characters illegal on Windows (`< > : " | ? *`) — the `.conflict-<host>-<change_seq>.md` form is safe on all three. Atomic tmp+rename for the conflict copy is reliable on APFS/ext4; on Windows ensure the rename targets the same volume (it does — sibling of canonical). The fix is in shared Rust (materializer.rs / conflict_stash.rs / verify_repair.rs) compiled for all three hosts, so behavior is uniform.
findings.residual_risks: 1) NEWER-WINS can still pick the 'wrong' winner under genuine concurrency, but this is acceptable because BOTH sides are preserved on disk (conflict copy) — no data is lost, only the canonical-name choice may need human merge. 2) change_seq orders SERVER acceptance, not human edit intent: if host A edits at T0 and host B edits at T1>T0 but B's push lands at the server before A's, B gets the lower lsn — newer-wins by lsn may invert human-time order. Mitigation: it still never loses data (loser stashed); if true intent-ordering is ever required, that is the only scenario that would justify per-file version vectors, and it can be added later without changing the on-disk state model. 3) The 15s echo-guard TTL (I40) interplay: ensure a stash write does not itself trip a spurious push of the conflict copy — record the conflict-copy path+hash in the echo guard before writing it (the canonical write already does this at materializer.rs:460). 4) Shadow store is best-effort JSON (loads empty on corruption, sync_shadow.rs:29) — case 5 (shadow absent ⇒ CONFLICT/stash) makes corruption fail SAFE (preserve both) rather than fail to silent-overwrite, which is the desired direction. 5) Conflict-copy proliferation under a flapping connection: many .conflict-* files could accumulate; add a tray count + an owner-facing cleanup, but never auto-delete a conflict copy.

design.summary:
TKT-2dc9a17e (silent revert of a locally-edited file to the server's OLDER copy within ~30-60s, no conflict file) is caused by THREE independent silent-server-wins clobber engines, all live at v0.4.22, plus a Unicode-normalization amplifier. None is a single bug; all four must land together, server-first.

ROOT 1 - SERVER reconciler FS-wins sweep (the primary engine; sync_routes_p1.py + reconciler.py). The daemon push writes ONLY Postgres: push_file (sync_routes_p1.py:1448-1537, create/modify branch) calls _cas_write_note_and_state and returns - it NEVER writes the server FS file at VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key> (grep confirms zero write_text/write_bytes/os.replace in the push body). But GET /api/sync/note serves FS bytes when present (sync_routes_p1.py:1079-1092) and the nexus-reconciler sweeps FS->PG every 60s UNCONDITIONALLY: reconciler.py:434-438 `pg_hash=_read_pg_hash(rel); if pg_hash==local_hash: continue; ... _upsert_note_and_state(rel, body=A, local_hash=sha(A))` with NO CAS, NO recency guard, origin='reconciler-cypher'. Sequence: brain/note_writer last wrote FS file = content A; an agent edits locally, daemon pushes B, CAS passes, PG.body=B + fs_hash=sha(B); within <=60s the sweep reads the STILL-STALE FS file (sha A), sees pg!=local, and reverts PG.body back to A; the body change re-bumps change_seq (migration 2026_05_30_005, body-gated) which re-fans A to every subscriber; the daemon materializes A. A brand-new local file SURVIVES because no FS file exists so iter_eligible_files never yields it -> exactly the observed asymmetry. This is SERVER-side, OS-independent, and the daemon's newer-wins+always-stash CANNOT defend against it.

ROOT 2 - DAEMON live SSE path is shadow-blind (materializer.rs + sse.rs). sse.rs:271-275 handles enrichment_complete by api.fetch_note(path) then materializer.write(payload) directly. write() (materializer.rs:467-522) has ONLY Identical-vs-Diverges; it WRITES the shadow (lines 479-481, 601-603) but never READS it to gate direction. The only shadow-aware decision is verify_repair::decide_direction (verify_repair.rs:235-257), used ONLY by reconcile. So I37 row 2 (PUSH genuine local edit) and row 3 (CONFLICT/stash) are NOT implemented on the live path. With conflict_policy=ServerWins (default, materializer.rs:194; lib.rs:510-513 never overrides) a Class-C diverged note hits should_stash=false (materializer.rs:487-488) and the else-branch (lines 513-519) overwrites with only an info! log = the silent clobber. macOS amplifier: restore_server_times (materializer.rs:139-169) stamps the server's OLDER created/mtime back so Obsidian's Created sort shows a clean revert.

ROOT 3 - DAEMON reconcile-Pull path overwrites Class-C with no stash (verify_repair.rs + conflict_stash.rs). decide_direction Pulls on drift when shadow absent OR shadow!=server (verify_repair.rs:241-252); the Pull executes via materializer.write() which, being ServerWins+ClassC, ALSO does not stash (conflict_stash.rs:210-215 returns stash:false, reason literally 'silently overwritten'). Class C is therefore NEVER stashed at v0.4.22 - only Class D is.

AMPLIFIER - no NFC/NFD normalization anywhere in the daemon (no unicode-normalization crate; only replace('\\','/')). macOS writes NFD filenames, Linux/ext4 stores bytes verbatim; the shadow-store key, reconcile manifest, and wire path are all raw OS byte-form, so a non-ASCII filename desyncs shadow.get() across hosts -> shadow miss -> decide_direction Pulls -> silent revert. Cross-OS bite, worst on link/Neo for Trinity-authored notes.

The binding operator ruling (NEWER-WINS + ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE, never silent server-wins, local edits propagate UP, loud logging) requires revising I32 (server-side, add post-write FS materialize + version capture; reconciler must stop unconditional revert) and I39 (delete the ServerWins/Class-C OVERWRITE cell entirely; the floor for any divergent-content overwrite is ALWAYS-STASH), plus three NEW invariants (pull-completeness, liveness-progress incl. watcher, never-silent-overwrite). External precedent (Obsidian Sync = merge-or-conflict-copy never silent server-wins; Syncthing = version-vector concurrency + loser always renamed never deleted; Dropbox = conflicted-copy safety net) unanimously validates this direction; our 3-way local/shadow/server triple is a sufficient Unison-archive concurrency detector - version vectors are NOT needed (single authoritative server provides total order via change_seq).
design.conflict_resolution_algorithm:
ONE decision function, used by BOTH the live SSE materialize path AND the reconcile backstop (the two are unified - today they diverge, which is half the bug). The daemon's persisted triple is the poor-man's version vector: local_norm_hash (normalize_for_diff, I16/I34), shadow = sync_shadow last-synced-server-hash, server = payload.sha256/fs_hash. NEVER use mtime to decide newer (clock skew across 3 OSes + ~15 writers => arbitrary-writer-wins); order by server change_seq only.

NORMALIZE FIRST: canonical_sync_path(s) = s.nfc().collect().replace('\\','/'), applied to the shadow key, the reconcile manifest key, and the push wire path. Write to the platform-native on-disk path (NFD target on macOS) but KEY the shadow in NFC so shadow.get() hits regardless of OS/origin. On first load under the new daemon version, NFC-normalize every existing shadow key (migration) so the cutover does not itself mass-miss -> mass-Pull.

decide(local, shadow: Option, server, server_change_seq) -> Action:
  R1. local == server (post-normalize)                         -> NOOP (idempotent, I17/I31/I34; record shadow=server)
  R2. shadow == Some(server) AND local != server               -> PUSH  (only local moved = genuine local edit; CAS base = server hash, I29). THIS is the case currently silently reverted - it MUST go UP, never be overwritten.
  R3. local == shadow AND server != shadow                     -> PULL_CLEAN (only server moved; local had no unsynced edit) -> materialize server, no stash needed.
  R4. local != server AND shadow != server AND local != shadow -> CONFLICT (both moved since last sync = true concurrency, Syncthing Concurrent()==true)
  R5. shadow == None AND local != server                       -> CONFLICT (cannot PROVE local was ever synced -> treat as concurrent; NEVER assume server wins on an unknown-provenance divergence)

On CONFLICT (R4 + R5) the invariant is ALWAYS-STASH-THEN-RESOLVE regardless of policy or Class:
  (a) FIRST write the LOSER (the local bytes) to a conflict copy via the existing I38 ConflictStash::write_stash mechanism: <stem>.conflict-from-<host>-<change_seq>.md (operator wording: .conflict-<host>-<ts>; use change_seq not wall-clock to dodge skew + give deterministic, fleet-convergent names a la Syncthing's .sync-conflict so N concurrent writers don't spawn N re-conflicting copies; the -2/-3 collision suffix loop stays as backstop). Atomic tmp+rename, I62 traversal-guarded. Stash BEFORE any overwrite so a crash mid-op can never lose the loser. Record the stash path+hash in the echo_guard BEFORE writing it so the watcher doesn't spuriously push the conflict copy.
  (b) Decide the winner by NEWER-WINS using change_seq; materialize the winner to the canonical path. With local_lsn currently unthreaded (passed as 0/None), NewerWins treats local as older => always-stash-on-divergence, which is the safe floor until a real local_lsn is wired (acceptable; 'newer truly wins' is a later refinement, never a data-loss risk).
  (c) Log LOUDLY at warn! with both paths + both hashes; bump tray.conflict_unresolved. Never info!.
  Net: winner at canonical name, loser preserved as sibling. Zero data loss even when 'newer' picks wrong, because both byte-sets survive on disk - exactly Dropbox/Syncthing/Obsidian.

CLOSE THE SSE LIVE-PULL BYPASS EXPLICITLY (the CLOSE-IT requirement): write() must take the shadow read INSIDE itself (it already holds shadow_store) and return a richer outcome - Skipped(LocalEditPreserved) for R2, Stashed for R4/R5, Wrote for R3/create - and sse.rs must pattern-match it instead of blindly trusting write() to overwrite. On R2 (LocalEditPreserved) the SSE consumer does NOT write; it leaves the file so file_watcher/push carries the local edit UP. Reuse normalize_for_diff (materializer.rs:686) for the local hash so the comparison basis matches the push side (I16/I34).

CONCURRENCY (~15 writers): serialize the exists()->compare->read-shadow->stash->rename sequence per-path with a per-path lock (dashmap of Mutex keyed by normalized path) inside write(). Today the atomic tmp+rename guarantees no torn file but NOT no lost-update between the read at materializer.rs:469 and the rename at :544; two SSE/reconcile events on the same path could race (one stashes, the other clobbers the stash's basis). The per-path lock is portable (std Mutex / dashmap), no journal-handle unification needed.

DELETE-vs-MODIFY (add the missing I37 row): if local was edited and server says deleted (tombstone), modification wins - keep + push the local file up (Syncthing 'modified file always wins, resurrected'), consult the I74 tombstone so a reconnecting host's local-only file matching a recent tombstone is NOT re-uploaded as a phantom create. If server has content and local deleted, materialize server. In the ambiguous case, preserve the surviving edited content as a conflict copy rather than honoring a destructive delete.

invariant_changes.current: Conflict policy is ServerWins-at-the-CAS (stale base loses), with NO server-side merge; merge burden entirely on the daemon.
invariant_changes.proposed: Keep the server CAS-409 as the concurrency-control primitive (correct for ~15 writers), but it MUST NEVER be read as license for any writer to silently discard the local loser. ADD two server obligations: (1) on every accepted push, materialize the pushed canonical bytes to the server FS file at VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key> inside/right-after the committed CAS txn (atomic tmp+rename) so FS==PG==fs_hash by construction and the reconciler sweep no-ops instead of reverting; on FS-write failure return non-2xx so the daemon retries rather than believing a write landed no reader can see. (2) the FS->PG reconciler sweep MUST NOT unconditionally overwrite newer PG state with a stale FS file - it must skip-or-re-materialize when the PG row is a newer daemon push, and never blindly revert. Daemon-side conflict policy default flips from ServerWins to NewerWins+always-stash.
invariant_changes.rationale: The reconciler's unconditional FS-wins sweep (reconciler.py:434-438) is the PRIMARY engine of TKT-2dc9a17e and lives entirely server-side, outside any daemon decision. The push writing only PG (sync_routes_p1.py:1448-1537) leaves the FS file as a competing stale authority that /api/sync/note serves and the sweep hashes. Binding operator ruling forbids silent server-wins. M2 POLICY-flagged, open for revision.

invariant_changes.current: Stash policy matrix: under ServerWins, Class C (content) = OVERWRITE with no stash; Class D = STASH.
invariant_changes.proposed: DELETE the ServerWins/Class-C OVERWRITE cell entirely. There is NO safe no-stash cell for divergent CONTENT. The floor for ANY overwrite of a locally-divergent file is ALWAYS-STASH-THEN-RESOLVE: write the loser to .conflict-from-<host>-<change_seq>.md before materializing the winner, for every Class (C and D), under every mode. Collapse ServerWins into NewerWins+always-stash. Keep Class D always-stash (unchanged).
invariant_changes.rationale: This cell is the literal silent-clobber instruction executed at materializer.rs:513-519 and conflict_stash.rs:210-215 (reason text: 'silently overwritten'). It is strictly less safe than even Obsidian Sync's least-safe content path (which still merges or writes a Conflicted-copy) and than Syncthing (which renames the loser, never deletes). Binding operator ruling. M2 POLICY-flagged.

invariant_changes.current: Five-row shadow decision table; rows 2 (PUSH) and 3 (CONFLICT) specified but NOT implemented on the live SSE materialize path - materializer.write() never reads the shadow.
invariant_changes.proposed: Make the decision table authoritative on BOTH paths by unifying the live SSE path and the reconcile backstop onto one decide() (see conflict_resolution_algorithm). write() reads shadow internally and returns LocalEditPreserved (R2) / Stashed (R4,R5) / Wrote (R3); sse.rs honors the outcome instead of blind-overwriting. ADD an explicit delete-vs-modify row: modification beats deletion, consult the I74 tombstone before treating a local-only file as a phantom create. Split CONFLICT into R4 (both moved) and R5 (shadow-absent => treat as concurrent, never assume server wins).
invariant_changes.rationale: The live SSE bypass is the architectural root of ROOT 2; the missing delete-vs-modify row is a known silent-loss vector; R5 makes shadow corruption/absence fail SAFE (preserve both) instead of fail-to-silent-overwrite.

invariant_changes.proposed: NEVER-SILENT-OVERWRITE (correctness law, not policy): no component on either side - daemon live path, daemon reconcile path, server push path, server reconciler sweep - may overwrite or revert a file/row that diverges from a locally-edited or newer copy without FIRST preserving the losing bytes (daemon: .conflict-from-<host>-<ts> sibling; server: note-version row) AND logging at warn!. An info!-level overwrite of divergent content is a contract violation.
invariant_changes.rationale: Encodes the binding operator ruling as a testable invariant spanning all four writers; the bug recurred because 'silent overwrite' was permitted in three separate places.

invariant_changes.proposed: PULL-COMPLETENESS: a note that exists only on the server MUST land on every in-scope, live-mode subscriber within a bounded window. The materializer create-writes missing files (already true at v0.4.22), the R6 pull_backfill enumerates server changes since=0 and creates locally-missing safe non-substrate paths, and a live-mode subscriber's backfill+SSE creates MUST land in the VAULT (not the runtime shadow tree). should_backfill must also apply path_in_scope (today it does not - scope asymmetry vs SSE). Surface a startup tray WARN when materializer_mode != 'live'.
invariant_changes.rationale: FM2 code defect is fixed at v0.4.22 (pull_backfill.rs is real and wired, lib.rs:977-982) but remains PARTIALLY live: the server materializer_mode column DEFAULTs to 'shadow' (migration 2026_05_25_005:31) and the daemon never self-flips, so a silently-shadowed subscriber materializes nothing into the vault. Plus scope asymmetry (pull_backfill.rs:92-103 omits path_in_scope that sse.rs:262 applies) and up-to-1h latency.

invariant_changes.proposed: LIVENESS-PROGRESS (both sides of the pipeline): liveness MUST verify PROGRESS, and MUST cover the ENQUEUE/watch side, not only push-drain. The watcher emits a heartbeat into SyncHealth each loop iteration and sets a watcher_fenced atomic on the storm-fence; the watchdog fires recovery on fenced OR heartbeat-stale watcher REGARDLESS of pending count (a dead enqueue path with pending==0 currently reads as healthy idle). Dedup must be authoritative on what the SERVER ACCEPTED (shadow last-accepted hash), never on what was merely enqueued.
invariant_changes.rationale: FM1-classic is fixed (sync_health.rs:126-128 + lib.rs:888-937), but is_stalled returns false at pending==0 so a fenced/dead watcher (file_watcher.rs:739-744 break; Linux inotify ENOSPC at startup) is invisible. The enqueued_hashes dedup (file_watcher.rs:843-855) can silently DROP a real edit: enqueue shaA -> push dropped (400 skip+ack, push_client.rs:519-525) -> user reverts to shaA content -> dedup matches -> never re-enqueues. Gate dedup on the ShadowStore's last-server-accepted hash instead (failed push records no shadow => revert re-enqueues).

daemon_changes.change: Flip the production conflict policy: in the MaterializerConfig built at lib.rs:510-513, set conflict_policy: ConflictPolicy::NewerWins explicitly (do not rely on Default, which is ServerWins at materializer.rs:194). Verify no other wiring re-introduces ServerWins.
daemon_changes.why: ROOT 2/3: production silently runs ServerWins because Default is ServerWins and lib.rs never overrides it. NewerWins+always-stash is the operator ruling. (Also: treat ServerWins as removed/aliased so a stray config value can't re-arm the clobber.)

daemon_changes.change: Rewrite the Diverges arm (lines 484-519): (a) read self.shadow_store.get(normalized path) INSIDE write(); apply decide() R1-R5. R2 (shadow==server, local!=server) => return new MaterializeOutcome::Skipped(LocalEditPreserved) WITHOUT writing. R3 (local==shadow) => write server, no stash. R4/R5 => ALWAYS write_stash the local bytes first (every Class, not just D), then write server, warn! loudly, bump tray.conflict_unresolved. (b) Change should_stash from policy-driven to divergence-driven: stash whenever local diverges from shadow (or shadow absent). (c) Add a per-path lock (dashmap<String, Mutex<()>>) around the exists->compare->stash->persist sequence. (d) Use change_seq (from payload/SSE id) as the stash lsn instead of the 0 placeholder at line 503; record the stash path+hash in echo_guard before writing it. (e) Fix restore_server_times (lines 139-169): derive mtime from payload.modified (RFC3339) per I64, fallback file_mtime->created; when all absent emit a WARN with the path instead of early-returning to 'now' (the macOS Created-sort clobber).
daemon_changes.why: ROOT 2: implements I37 rows 2/3/delete on the live path, closes the SSE bypass, makes the stash mandatory for Class C (the silent half), serializes concurrent same-path writes (~15 writers), and stops the macOS birthtime mis-restore.

daemon_changes.change: Replace the blind materializer.write() at lines 271-275 with outcome-aware handling: match the new MaterializeOutcome - on Skipped(LocalEditPreserved) do nothing (let file_watcher/push carry the edit up), on Stashed warn!, on Wrote debug!. Thread the SSE envelope's lsn/change_seq into the fetch->write so write() can name the stash by change_seq.
daemon_changes.why: ROOT 2: the SSE consumer must NOT trust write() to overwrite; it must honor a 'local edit preserved' verdict so a genuine local edit propagates UP instead of being reverted.

daemon_changes.change: Remove the ServerWins arm's stash:false (lines 210-215). Make Class C under all modes return stash:true when local diverges from shadow. Update the misleading 'silently overwritten' reason text (no em-dashes). Keep Class D always-stash and the -2/-3 collision suffix.
daemon_changes.why: ROOT 3: the policy module currently encodes the silent-overwrite decision the operator forbids; this is the I39 revision.

daemon_changes.change: In decide_direction (lines 235-257): keep R2 PUSH (drift+shadow==server) and R3 PULL_CLEAN (local==shadow), but route the drift case where local!=shadow (and the shadow-absent case, lines 248-252) through a stash-before-overwrite path rather than a bare Pull/materialize. Since the Pull executes via materializer.write() (line 478-480), the materializer.rs change above already forces the stash - but ALSO add R5 here so shadow-absent => CONFLICT (stash) not Pull. Stamp sync_health.mark_progress() (or a reconcile-specific heartbeat) so reconcile activity is a liveness signal.
daemon_changes.why: ROOT 3: the reconcile-Pull arm is the second executor of the Class-C no-stash overwrite; it must always-stash and treat shadow-absent as concurrent, matching the live path.

daemon_changes.change: On load() (lines 57-85), under a new daemon version, NFC-normalize every existing key before inserting into the map (one-time migration). Add a key accessor that normalizes its argument to NFC so record/get are normalization-invariant.
daemon_changes.why: AMPLIFIER + cutover safety: existing shadow keys may be NFD (macOS-origin); without re-normalizing, the NFC cutover would mass-miss -> mass-Pull -> the exact data-loss event. Must ship together with always-stash so any residual miss preserves the local copy.

daemon_changes.change: (a) Gate the enqueue dedup on the ShadowStore's last-server-accepted hash, not the in-memory enqueued_hashes (lines 843-855, 879-883) - or invalidate the enqueued_hashes entry on any non-Accepted push outcome - so a revert-to-previously-enqueued-content after a dropped push re-enqueues. (b) Add a watcher heartbeat (mark_watcher_alive each loop iteration ~line 701-755) and a watcher_fenced atomic set on the storm fence (line 740) into the shared SyncHealth Arc; extend the watchdog to fire on fenced OR heartbeat-stale regardless of pending. (c) Apply NFC normalization in normalize_path (lines 604/931) so PUSH paths are NFC.
daemon_changes.why: FM1 residual: dedup can silently drop a real edit; a fenced/dead watcher is invisible to the pending-based watchdog (worst on Linux inotify ENOSPC, Windows storm-fence). Plus the push-side half of the NFC fix.

daemon_changes.change: Add path_in_scope(scope_roots, scope_excludes) to should_backfill (lines 92-103), threading the scope lists into spawn_pull_backfill_task exactly as sse.rs has them (lib.rs:595-596). Fold PullBackfillStats.failed into a tray counter and emit a one-shot owner notification when failed>0. Lower DEFAULT_BACKFILL_INTERVAL_SECS from 3600 to ~300.
daemon_changes.why: FM2 residual: scope asymmetry over-pulls; WARN-only failures are invisible on the Windows GUI subsystem; 1h discovery latency for server-created notes.

daemon_changes.change: Add unicode-normalization (NFC) as a direct dependency, and dunce as a DIRECT dep (currently transitive-only) for Windows long-path/\\?\ handling.
daemon_changes.why: AMPLIFIER + Windows: no normalization crate exists today; dunce is needed to prefix \\?\ on the daemon's own paths so >260-char notes don't silently fail CreateFileW on a host without LongPathsEnabled.

daemon_changes.change: On cfg(windows), replace bare tempfile::persist (=MoveFileExW) with ReplaceFileW-preferred atomic replace (preserves destination ACLs/attributes), MoveFileExW fallback, bounded backoff retry on ERROR_SHARING_VIOLATION (Obsidian lock) then a real error (never a silent .tmp orphan). Prefix the materialize/stash target + the config vaults_root with \\?\ via dunce. Add sub-second/nonce to the soft_delete timestamp suffix (line 666) to avoid same-second stash collisions on ext4.
daemon_changes.why: Windows correctness (Neo, code-read only - must re-verify on a booted Neo): MoveFileExW is non-atomic vs an open handle and drops ACLs; long-path is unhandled; second-granularity delete suffix collides.

server_changes.change: Fix the unconditional FS-wins sweep (sweep_once, lines 424-438). PREFERRED (with the push FS-materialize below): leave the skip-on-equal logic; once push writes FS==PG, pg_hash==local_hash and the sweep no-ops, closing the revert window. DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH regardless: before _upsert_note_and_state, read vault_notes.origin + vault_reconcile_state.pg_modified/last_reconciled_at; if the PG row is a daemon push ('nexus-sync-push') and PG is NEWER than the FS file's st_mtime, the FS is STALE -> SKIP the revert and instead re-materialize PG->FS (write body to the FS file) so FS catches up; NEVER let an older FS file overwrite a newer PG body. Minimum stop-gap if neither lands: on a divergence where the PG row exists and is newer, write a .conflict-cypher-<ts> copy of the FS-side content and KEEP the PG body (server-side mirror of always-stash). Also parse frontmatter and persist created/modified in _upsert_note_and_state (lines 377-402, mirroring _exec_note_and_state_upserts) so every sweep upsert/revert stops blanking created -> NULL-created birthtime clobber (I36).
server_changes.why: ROOT 1 (PRIMARY): this 60s sweep is the engine that reverts a pushed B back to the stale-FS A and re-fans it via change_seq. It is the single highest-leverage fix and is server-side/OS-independent.

server_changes.change: In push_file, after a successful _cas_write_note_and_state returns 'written' (and in the delete branch), write/remove VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key> atomically (NamedTemporaryFile in the same dir + os.replace, create parent dirs) using the SAME content_bytes already decoded, so sha256(file)==raw_hash==fs_hash==vault_notes.body by construction. Guard under_root exactly like get_note_body's resolve check (lines 1083-1089). Sequence the FS replace AFTER the CAS commit so two racing pushes apply in CAS-commit order; on FS-write failure, log loudly and return a non-2xx so the daemon retries (never a silent landed-but-unreadable write). VERIFY the API/reconciler container that serves /api/sync/push has rw access to the vault path before relying on this; if only the reconciler can write FS, route materialization there or adopt the PG-body-preferred read alternative.
server_changes.why: ROOT 1 + FM3: the push writes ONLY PG (lines 1448-1537), leaving the FS file a stale competing authority that /api/sync/note serves and the reconciler hashes. Materializing on push collapses the two authorities, makes /api/notes/{token} and /api/sync/note both serve new content, and lets the reconciler no-op. Restores I14 by construction.

server_changes.change: VERIFY the note-versioning trigger (migration 2026_05_31_002_version_trigger) fires on BOTH the reconciler's _upsert_note_and_state path and _cas_write_note_and_state, so every body change incl. a revert is recoverable from versions; extend it if it does not cover the reconciler write. This is the server-side recoverability backstop that makes any residual overwrite undoable (Obsidian/Dropbox version-history model).
server_changes.why: I32 revision + never-silent-overwrite: even with the fixes, a server-side version snapshot before any body change guarantees a silent revert is always reversible. (Could not confirm trigger scope statically - required follow-up read.)

server_changes.change: Set lint_state explicitly on push in _exec_note_and_state_upserts (e.g. 'clean') OR have the notify trigger (migration 2026_05_20_004) treat origin='nexus-sync-push' as a content-ready phase, so pushed rows are not stranded at phase=lint_pending; and surface 'last fanout emit age' in /api/sync/lag so a dormant nexus-sync daemon (the separate fanout-producing container) alarms.
server_changes.why: FM3 secondary: pushed rows tagged origin='nexus-sync-push' with lint_state defaulting to 'pending' only emit phase=lint_pending; if the separate nexus-sync container is down, fanout dies with zero error surface. Does not affect queryable content but fixes live-SSE wake latency.

server_changes.change: Unify scope across all four endpoints (I52): switch reconcile_batch (line 394) from Depends(require_bearer) to Depends(_require_bearer_with_scope) and filter returned deltas through path_in_scope; apply scope_roots/scope_excludes in get_changes (lines 307-334) advancing the cursor past skipped rows (I21); collapse the two scope engines (api/scope_filter_match.py fnmatch vs services/vault_sync/scope_filter.py pathspec/gitwildmatch) onto gitwildmatch as canonical so a path included on push is never excluded on pull (perpetual push/no-pull churn for scoped subscribers).
server_changes.why: Scope asymmetry (TKT-3f13c769) can cause cross-scope leaks and push/no-pull churn; the daemon's pull_backfill lacking scope (see daemon_changes) interacts with this. Audit live vault_subscribers.scope_roots before deploying - behavior-changing for fnmatch-reliant subscribers.

server_changes.change: Either change the materializer_mode DEFAULT off 'shadow' for user-facing vault subscribers, or run a one-time check + PATCH /api/sync/subscribers/me to set Trinity/link/Neo subscriber rows to 'live'. Run SELECT subscriber_id, materializer_mode FROM vault_subscribers first.
server_changes.why: FM2 deployment gate: a live daemon whose server row is 'shadow' materializes into the runtime shadow tree, NOT the vault - FM2 is fully live for it despite correct R6 code. Highest-leverage verification (config, not code). Could not run live SQL from this worktree - MUST be checked on Cypher.

server_changes.change: After deploying the reconciler created/modified fix, run an idempotent UPDATE vault_notes SET created = COALESCE(NULLIF(created,''), modified, indexed_at, ...) WHERE (created IS NULL OR created='') AND path LIKE '%.md'. RUN ORDER: deploy the reconciler fix FIRST (stop the bleed), THEN backfill. created-only updates do NOT touch body so they will NOT bump change_seq (trigger is body-gated) - verify no other AFTER trigger fans on a created-only change. Count first: SELECT count(*) FROM vault_notes WHERE (created IS NULL OR created='') AND path LIKE '%.md' (prior record ~6,213, not re-measured).
server_changes.why: I36 NULL-created backfill: the reconciler blanked created on every revert, causing NULL-created birthtime clobber on re-materialize. Bounded once the reconciler stops creating new NULLs.

design.cross_os_handling:
The PRIMARY engine (reconciler FS-wins sweep) and ROOT 2/3 are all OS-INDEPENDENT (shared Rust in materializer.rs/sse.rs/verify_repair.rs + server-side Python), which is why the bug reproduced on Trinity (macOS) but is not macOS-specific - the loss for a link (Linux) or Neo (Windows) edit is identical.

NORMALIZATION (the cross-OS amplifier): macOS persists filenames NFD; ext4/NTFS store bytes verbatim with no normalization. Canonicalize ALL keys/wire/lookups to NFC (Linux/Windows-native, and the server's canonical form per I35) via unicode-normalization. CRITICAL: normalize the KEY (shadow, manifest, wire) to NFC but WRITE to the platform-native on-disk path (NFD target on macOS) so the daemon does not create duplicate decomposed/precomposed files on disk. The server must agree on NFC on ingest, and reconcile-batch/SSE must echo back the CLIENT-sent (NFC) path, or normalize delta.path on receipt, else delta.path (server stored form) still mismatches the daemon NFC manifest. Case-sensitivity (ext4 case-sensitive vs APFS/NTFS case-insensitive) is a latent same-root divergence, out of scope for the live bug.

TIMESTAMPS / NEWER-WINS: order by server change_seq (a server-side integer, identical on all three hosts, immune to per-host clock skew) - NEVER file mtime (NTFS 100ns vs APFS/ext4 ns + skew + ~15 writers make mtime ties common). If any fallback ever uses mtime, apply a modTimeWindow tolerance (Syncthing's ModTimeEqual) or macOS-vs-Windows rounding generates phantom conflicts. Conflict-copy filename .conflict-from-<host>-<change_seq>.md uses only chars legal on Windows (no < > : \" | ? *) and a deterministic component so all hosts compute the same name and the copy converges (Syncthing .sync-conflict property). Normalize CRLF/LF before any future 3-way merge so a Neo vs Trinity edit doesn't false-conflict on line endings.

BIRTHTIME (I65): macOS (setattrlist ATTR_CMN_CRTIME) + Windows (SetFileTime) restore it; Linux is kernel-read-only (mtime-only, frontmatter created as mitigation) - correct as-is. The restore_server_times mtime-source fix (use payload.modified) and the server NULL-created backfill matter most on macOS/Windows because that is where the Obsidian Created-sort clobber is user-visible (Trinity).

WINDOWS (Neo, DOWN - all claims code-read only, MUST re-verify on a booted Neo): tempfile::persist=MoveFileExW is non-atomic vs an open Obsidian handle (ERROR_SHARING_VIOLATION) and drops destination ACLs/attributes - switch to ReplaceFileW with backoff. No \\\\?\\ long-path handling exists for the daemon's own paths (dunce transitive-only) - add it. notify 7 on Windows uses ReadDirectoryChangesW (no Access/Metadata kinds) so the I41 read-walk filter is a no-op there and is unproven on Neo; the storm-fence + inotify-ENOSPC liveness blind spot is reachable on Windows mainly via the storm fence or a task panic. I81 (UTF-8/UTF-16, no CP437) is genuinely clean in the daemon.

LINUX (link): inotify watch-exhaustion is detected only at the initial recursive watch (file_watcher.rs:681-690); dynamic per-dir growth after startup surfaces as a WARN-only batch error (lines 746-751) and that subtree silently stops being watched - add a periodic watch-count vs read_inotify_limit() probe (~80% threshold) and document fs.inotify.max_user_watches=524288. ext4 atomic rename + Linux birthtime soft-fail are CONFIRMED CORRECT. The materializer create-on-missing (FM2) is implemented and correct on all three.

The fix is shared Rust + shared Python, so behavior is uniform across hosts; the mandatory periodic full reconcile (kept as backstop) is what makes correctness OS-independent (Syncthing: inotify/FSEvents/ReadDirectoryChangesW all drop events, so never trust the watcher alone).

test_matrix.scenario: edit-same-file-on-two-hosts (THE live bug, TKT-2dc9a17e): host A edits note X to content B and pushes; while A's edit is in flight, the server reconciler sweep runs against the stale FS file (content A).
test_matrix.expected: PG.body stays B (reconciler skips or re-materializes PG->FS, never reverts to A). After push FS materialize, /api/sync/note and /api/notes/{token} both return B with sha256==fs_hash. No subscriber receives A. Zero conflict files when only A edited.

test_matrix.scenario: genuine local edit while a server enrichment_complete SSE fires for the same path (shadow==server, local!=server) on Trinity (macOS).
test_matrix.expected: Live SSE path returns Skipped(LocalEditPreserved); the local edit is NOT overwritten and propagates UP via the push pipeline. macOS birthtime is NOT stamped back to the server's older created. (Currently red: silent revert.)

test_matrix.scenario: true concurrent edit on two hosts (local!=server AND shadow!=server AND local!=shadow), Class C content note.
test_matrix.expected: Local bytes stashed to <stem>.conflict-from-<host>-<change_seq>.md FIRST (atomic, traversal-guarded), THEN server canonical materialized, warn! logged, tray.conflict_unresolved incremented. Both byte-sets survive on disk. (Currently red: Class C overwrites with no stash - update stash_not_written_for_class_c_under_server_wins at materializer.rs:1160 to expect Stashed under NewerWins.)

test_matrix.scenario: shadow-store wiped/corrupt (shadow absent, R5) then a drift on a locally-edited file.
