test_matrix.expected: decide() routes to CONFLICT (stash local, then materialize server) - NOT a silent Pull. Fail-SAFE: preserve both. (Currently red: shadow-absent => Pull => overwrite, both on the live path and verify_repair.rs:248-252.)

test_matrix.scenario: offline-then-reconnect: host offline for hours, server-only note created meanwhile; host reconnects.
test_matrix.expected: Catchup (I22) + pull_backfill creates the server-only note in the LIVE vault path (not the runtime shadow tree) on a long-offline host, in-scope only. Local offline edits replay+stash-on-conflict, never silently reverted.

test_matrix.scenario: new-note-pull-create: a note exists ONLY on the server (Nexus-origin), subscriber in live mode, in scope.
test_matrix.expected: materializer create-writes it into the vault within the backfill window; out-of-scope server notes are NOT pulled (scope parity with SSE). A 'shadow' materializer_mode subscriber surfaces a startup tray WARN. (FM2 add-count lands in LIVE vault path test, currently missing per I72 row.)

test_matrix.scenario: delete-vs-edit: host A deletes note X (tombstone), host B edited X offline; B reconnects.
test_matrix.expected: Modification wins - B's edited X is kept and pushed up (not silently deleted); a reconnecting B's local-only file matching a recent I74 tombstone is NOT re-uploaded as a phantom create (Obsidian-resurrection guard).

test_matrix.scenario: clock-skew: two hosts with skewed clocks edit the same note; pushes land out of wall-clock order.
test_matrix.expected: Newer-wins decided by server change_seq, never mtime; loser always stashed regardless of skew. No arbitrary-writer-wins, no data loss.

test_matrix.scenario: ~15 concurrent agent writers across the fleet hammering overlapping paths, including rapid A->B->A byte sequences and same-path SSE+reconcile events.
test_matrix.expected: Per-path lock serializes exists->compare->stash->rename so no stash-basis lost-update; deterministic change_seq-keyed conflict filenames so N writers don't spawn N re-conflicting copies; bounded push counts, zero steady-state churn, every-local-sha==fs_hash convergence (N4 property).

test_matrix.scenario: cross-OS unicode/NFC-NFD: a note with an accented/emoji title authored on Trinity (NFD), edited on link (NFC).
test_matrix.expected: Shadow.get() hits across hosts (NFC-keyed); the link edit classifies as PUSH (R2), NOT a silent Pull/revert; on-disk filename stays platform-native (NFD on macOS) with no duplicate decomposed/precomposed file. (The nexus-sync-postupdate-test MUST plant a deliberately non-ASCII filename as the e2e gate.)

test_matrix.scenario: crash-mid-write: process killed between stashing the loser and renaming the winner.
test_matrix.expected: Loser conflict copy already persisted (stash-before-overwrite); on restart the reconcile backstop re-resolves idempotently. No torn file (atomic tmp+rename), no lost loser.

test_matrix.scenario: dedup-drops-real-edit (FM1): enqueue shaA -> push dropped (HTTP 400 skip+ack) -> user reverts file to shaA content.
test_matrix.expected: The revert RE-ENQUEUES and pushes (dedup gated on server-accepted shadow hash, not enqueued_hashes). (Currently red.)

test_matrix.scenario: fenced/dead watcher (Linux inotify ENOSPC at startup, or storm-fence break) with pending==0.
test_matrix.expected: Watchdog fires recovery on fenced OR heartbeat-stale watcher even at pending==0 (today is_stalled returns false). New-subdir-after-startup inotify exhaustion surfaces a loud tray banner + watch-count probe.

test_matrix.scenario: server push->serve reflection (FM3 + I14): push new bytes for an existing path, then immediately query both read surfaces.
test_matrix.expected: GET /api/sync/note returns the NEW bytes with sha256==returned fs_hash AND GET /api/notes/{token} returns the NEW body (no stale, no 404 on create). (I14 push->serve test currently MISSING per spec §17.)

test_matrix.scenario: server FS-write failure on push (e.g. container lacks rw vault mount).
test_matrix.expected: push returns non-2xx and the daemon retries; never a silent 'accepted' for a write no reader can see (no new silent-loss class).

design.rollout_plan:
SERVER-FIRST, because the PRIMARY engine (reconciler FS-wins sweep) and the FS-materialize-on-push are server-side and a daemon-only fix cannot defend against a server-side revert. Phased, fail-CLOSED on every gate.

PHASE 0 - VERIFY (no code; closes FM2 + grounds the backfill, highest leverage): (a) On Cypher run SELECT subscriber_id, materializer_mode FROM vault_subscribers; if Trinity/link/Neo are 'shadow', that alone makes FM2 fully live for them - PATCH them to 'live' or change the default. (b) Confirm the API/reconciler container that serves /api/sync/push has rw access to VAULT_ROOT before relying on push FS-materialize. (c) SELECT count(*) FROM vault_notes WHERE (created IS NULL OR created='') AND path LIKE '%.md' for the backfill size. (d) Confirm the note-versioning trigger (2026_05_31_002) covers both the reconciler and push write paths. (e) Confirm the separate nexus-sync fanout container is running + fanout_notifies advancing.

PHASE 1 - SERVER deploy (via cypher-deploy.sh with the conductor deploy lease, fail-CLOSED): land (1) the reconciler created/modified parse + the recency/provenance guard (or at minimum the always-stop-the-revert skip), (2) push FS-materialize-on-accept with atomic os.replace + non-2xx on failure, (3) version-trigger coverage, in that order. Reconciler fix FIRST stops the bleed. Then run the NULL-created backfill (created-only UPDATE, body-gated trigger => no change_seq fan; suppress fanout if any other AFTER trigger fans). CI gates: DB Matrix + API Contract + Docker green; new I14 push->serve test + reconciler-no-revert test must pass. This alone should stop TKT-2dc9a17e in production even before the daemon ships.

PHASE 2 - DAEMON build for 3 OSes (signed assets per the v0.4.15 release pattern): the unified decide()/always-stash/NFC/per-path-lock/dedup-on-shadow/watcher-heartbeat changes + ConflictPolicy::NewerWins flip + the Windows ReplaceFileW/long-path changes. Build macOS arm64 + Linux amd64 AppImage + Windows. ALL Rust tests green incl. the updated stash_not_written_for_class_c (now expects Stashed) and the new conflict/shadow-absent/dedup/watcher tests. Re-verify Windows on a BOOTED Neo (currently DOWN - the Windows claims are code-read only and are a HARD re-verify gate before Neo re-enables sync).

PHASE 3 - SHADOW-MODE BAKE on Trinity: install the new daemon in materializer_mode bake/shadow-aware-but-non-destructive long enough to confirm: shadow-store NFC migration applied cleanly (no mass-miss/mass-Pull spike), zero unexpected .conflict-from-* storms, decide() classifies known local edits as PUSH not Pull. The shadow-store migration MUST ship together with always-stash so any residual key miss preserves the local copy rather than reverting it.

PHASE 4 - RE-ENABLE GATE (per-host, fail-CLOSED): a host re-enables live sync ONLY when - server Phase 1 is live AND green; the host's subscriber row is materializer_mode='live'; the host's daemon is the Phase-2 build; the shadow migration baked clean on that host; (Neo only) Windows re-verified on real hardware. Run /nexus-sync-postupdate-test once ALL hosts are on the same version, WITH a deliberately non-ASCII filename planted, asserting CREATE/EDIT/DELETE propagation + byte-faithfulness + the edit-same-file-on-two-hosts no-revert property + the new-note-pull-create-into-vault property. Keep the ACTIVE-ALERT manual cross-host propagation until that e2e passes green.

ROLLBACK: server via cypher-deploy.sh to the prior sha (the reconciler fix is additive/guard-only; push FS-materialize is additive); daemon via the prior signed release tag; the shadow-store NFC migration is forward-only per key but the always-stash floor makes a rollback non-lossy (worst case extra conflict copies). Triple-write the outcome (vault FIRST) + full handoff.
open_questions_for_owner: Server FS-materialize on push vs PG-body-preferred read: do you want the push path to write the canonical bytes to VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key> on every accepted push (cleanest, restores I14 by construction, but adds a SECOND FS writer alongside brain/note_writer/reconciler that must be serialized under the CAS row lock), OR the less-invasive 'serve vault_notes.body when fs_hash != sha(FS file)' read change? I recommend FS-materialize-on-push; confirm the push container actually has rw vault mount first (Phase 0b).

materializer_mode default: flip the server DEFAULT off 'shadow' for user-facing vault subscribers, or keep 'shadow' default and just PATCH the three host rows to 'live'? A silently-shadowed subscriber materializes NOTHING into the vault - this is the single highest-leverage FM2 fix and needs your call on the default.

Conflict-copy filename component: is .conflict-from-<host>-<change_seq>.md acceptable (deterministic, fleet-convergent, Windows-legal), or do you prefer the exact operator wording .conflict-<host>-<ts>? The change_seq form is what stops ~15 concurrent writers from spawning N re-conflicting copies; a wall-clock ts re-introduces skew collisions.

I32 daemon-side: confirm flipping the production default from ServerWins to NewerWins+always-stash for ALL Class-C content (the operator ruling implies yes). Server CAS-409 stays as the concurrency primitive; only the daemon's overwrite-vs-stash behavior changes.

Scope-engine unification (fnmatch -> gitwildmatch/pathspec, I52 fix 5c) is behavior-changing for any subscriber relying on the looser fnmatch /note semantics - OK to audit live vault_subscribers.scope_roots/scope_excludes and switch, or defer scope unification to a separate change since it is orthogonal to the data-loss bug?

Neo is DOWN: the entire Windows correctness story (ReplaceFileW vs MoveFileExW, \\?\ long-path, SetFileTime birthtime on real NTFS, ReadDirectoryChangesW liveness) is code-read only. Is bringing Neo up to e2e-verify a blocker for the re-enable gate, or do we ship the Windows changes 'best-effort, re-verify when Neo boots' and keep Neo on manual sync until then?

Tombstone retention I74 (currently 24h): a host offline >24h misses deletes until the next reconcile-class mechanism (reconcile never pulls). With delete-vs-modify now resurrection-guarded, do you want to extend retention (e.g. 7d for multi-day-offline hosts) or accept the documented gap?

local_lsn threading: the real 'newer truly wins' semantics need a per-note local_lsn threaded through (today passed as 0/None so NewerWins => always-stash-on-divergence, which is safe but means 'newer' is not yet real). Acceptable to ship always-stash-on-divergence now and thread real local_lsn later, or do you want true newer-wins in this change?

attack_scenarios.scenario: PRIMARY-ENGINE RACE WINDOW STILL OPEN BETWEEN CAS-COMMIT AND FS-MATERIALIZE. The design's keystone fix is push FS-materialize 'sequenced AFTER the CAS commit' (sync_routes_p1.py change). But verified: _cas_write_note_and_state commits the txn (line ~1361 conn.commit()) and only THEN returns 'written'; the FS write happens in push_file after the return. The reconciler sweep runs every 60s UNCONDITIONALLY and reads vault_reconcile_state.fs_hash=raw_hash(B) (confirmed _read_pg_hash:365-374 reads fs_hash, which the CAS already set to B) while recomputing sha(on-disk file)=sha(A) until the post-commit FS write lands. If the sweep fires in that window (commit done, os.replace not yet done) it sees pg_hash(B) != local_hash(A) and REVERTS body to A AND fs_hash to sha(A) -- re-arming the exact bug. The design's own DEFENSE-IN-DEPTH reconciler guard ('skip if PG row is a newer daemon push') is the only thing that closes this, and it is described as optional/'regardless'. If only the push-materialize ships (Phase 1 lists reconciler-fix-first, but the recency guard is hedged as 'or at minimum the always-stop-the-revert skip'), the window is real. MUST-FIX: the reconciler recency/provenance guard is NOT optional defense-in-depth; it is load-bearing and must ship in the SAME deploy, because os.replace is not transactional with conn.commit().
attack_scenarios.gap: Push FS-materialize is post-commit and post-return; the 60s reconciler can read the CAS-updated fs_hash(B) against the stale on-disk A in the commit-to-replace gap and revert. The recency guard that closes it is hedged as optional.

attack_scenarios.scenario: SERVER-SIDE OVERWRITE HAS NO CONFLICT COPY -- only a version row. Operator ruling: ALWAYS-STASH-ON-OVERWRITE, preserve BOTH sides on disk. The design satisfies this on the DAEMON (.conflict-from-<host> sibling) but on the SERVER the 'minimum stop-gap' writes a .conflict-cypher copy only 'if neither [push-materialize nor guard] lands', and the primary recoverability is the note-versioning trigger (2026_05_31_002) whose coverage the design itself ADMITS it 'could not confirm statically'. If a server-side concurrent write does overwrite (e.g. brain/note_writer writes FS=A while a daemon push commits B, then the reconciler picks one), the loser survives ONLY as a PG version row, NOT as an on-disk .conflict copy a human sees in Obsidian. That is weaker than the daemon floor and arguably violates the literal ruling ('preserve BOTH sides, .conflict-<host>-<ts> copy'). MUST-FIX: confirm the version trigger fires on the reconciler _upsert path AND the brain/note_writer FS-write path, or the server-side 'both sides preserved' guarantee is unproven.
attack_scenarios.gap: Server-side preservation relies on a PG version trigger of UNVERIFIED coverage; there is no on-disk conflict copy for server-side overwrites, unlike the daemon. brain/note_writer is a third FS writer the design never serializes against the push-materialize.

attack_scenarios.scenario: SHADOW-STORE NFC MIGRATION IS ITSELF A MASS-REVERT TRIGGER IF always-stash is buggy. The cutover NFC-normalizes every shadow key on first load (sync_shadow.rs change). If even one bug in the new always-stash path exists (e.g. an exception in write_stash leaves should_stash logic to fall through), every NFD-keyed macOS-origin note momentarily misses shadow.get() -> R5 (shadow absent) -> CONFLICT. The design says R5 stashes, so it 'fails safe' -- BUT a fleet-wide simultaneous R5 on thousands of notes means thousands of .conflict-from-Trinity-* copies spawned at once (the design even concedes 'no unexpected .conflict storms' is a Phase-3 bake CHECK, i.e. it is a known risk, not a proven absence). That is not data loss, but it IS a usability catastrophe and could mask a real concurrent edit in the noise. MUST-FIX: the shadow NFC migration must be verified idempotent and the always-stash path must be exception-proof BEFORE the migration runs, else the safety mechanism becomes a conflict-copy flood.
attack_scenarios.gap: No data loss (R5 preserves), but the NFC migration + always-stash interaction can produce a fleet-wide conflict-copy storm that buries genuine conflicts; only a Phase-3 bake check guards it, not a proven invariant.

attack_scenarios.scenario: ~15 WRITERS: PER-PATH LOCK IS DAEMON-LOCAL ONLY; CROSS-HOST AND CROSS-WRITER (brain/reconciler/push) RACES UNGUARDED. The dashmap<path,Mutex> serializes the daemon's OWN SSE-vs-reconcile events. But the ~15 agent writers are SERVER-SIDE (they write the vault/PG via brain tools, note_writer, edit_note), not through this one daemon. The real lost-update race is: daemon push commits B (CAS, fs_hash=B), brain edit_note writes FS=C and PG=C a millisecond later WITHOUT going through sync CAS (brain tools write PG directly), reconciler then sees fs(C) vs pg(C) match and B is gone -- no daemon lock touches that. The server CAS (FOR UPDATE on vault_reconcile_state) only guards the /push endpoint; brain/note_writer/reconciler write vault_notes on OTHER code paths that the design does NOT bring under the CAS row lock. MUST-FIX: the design must enumerate EVERY server-side vault_notes writer and bring them under the same CAS/version discipline, or '~15 writers safe' is false -- the per-path Mutex is necessary but addresses only the daemon's internal race.
attack_scenarios.gap: Per-path lock is in-process daemon-local. The ~15 concurrent writers are server-side brain/note_writer/edit_note paths that bypass the /push CAS entirely; a non-sync writer can clobber a freshly-pushed body and the reconciler will bless it. The design never audits these writers.

attack_scenarios.scenario: NEWER-WINS DEGRADES TO ALWAYS-STASH-ON-DIVERGENCE BUT local_lsn IS NEVER WIRED -> R4/R5 winner is ALWAYS the server. Verified conflict_stash.rs:224-249: NewerWins with local_lsn=None treats local as older (line 227 'unknown local lsn = treat as older') -> stashes local, materializes SERVER. The design accepts this ('safe floor, newer is not yet real'). The data-loss critique: this is operationally identical to ServerWins-with-a-stash, NOT newer-wins. Under heavy concurrency the user's freshly-saved local edit (genuinely newer) gets demoted to a .conflict-from-<host> sibling on EVERY divergence while the server copy takes the canonical name. Over 15 writers and frequent SSE fan-out, the canonical file will repeatedly flip to server-content and the user's real work accretes as a pile of conflict siblings they must manually reconcile. No bytes are lost, but the operator ruling 'local edits MUST propagate UP, not be reverted' is only HALF honored: R2 (shadow==server) pushes up correctly, but any R4/R5 (shadow!=server, which a wiped/stale shadow or any prior unsynced edit produces) demotes the live edit to a sibling. MUST-FIX: either thread real local_lsn now, or make R2 detection robust enough that genuine local edits almost never fall into R4/R5 -- otherwise 'newer wins' is a misnomer and the canonical-name churn is severe.
attack_scenarios.gap: Zero byte loss, but local_lsn=None makes 'NewerWins' = 'ServerWins + always-stash'. Genuine newer local edits are demoted to conflict siblings on every shadow!=server divergence; canonical name flips to server. The ruling's 'local edits propagate UP' is satisfied only for the R2 case.

attack_scenarios.scenario: DELETE-vs-MODIFY RESURRECTION GUARD DEPENDS ON I74 TOMBSTONE (24h) BUT THE SERVER DELETE PATH DROPS THE RECONCILE ROW ENTIRELY. Verified _delete_note_and_state:1366-1376 DELETEs both vault_notes AND vault_reconcile_state. So after a delete, a reconnecting host with a locally-edited copy of X pushes it with base_hash='' (no row) -> the server treats it as a fresh CREATE (push_file create branch, current=None, base_hash='' accepted). The design's claim 'consult the I74 tombstone so a reconnecting host's local-only file matching a recent tombstone is NOT re-uploaded as a phantom create' requires the tombstone to still EXIST and be consulted on the PUSH path -- but the verified delete path leaves NO tombstone row, and push_file has no tombstone lookup. The proposed I74 consult is described daemon-side ('reconnecting host's local-only file'), yet the daemon cannot know the server deleted X unless it received the DELETE SSE while offline (it didn't -- it was offline) or a reconcile told it (reconcile never pulls deletes per the design's own note 'reconcile never pulls'). MUST-FIX: a delete must leave a durable, retention-bounded tombstone that BOTH the daemon reconcile catchup AND the server push path consult; otherwise delete-vs-modify on a >tombstone-retention offline host either resurrects a deleted note as a phantom create OR (if the host honored a stale local delete) loses the edit. The design's resurrection guard is asserted but the substrate (row-drop delete + no push-path tombstone check) contradicts it.
attack_scenarios.gap: Server delete drops the reconcile_state row and there is no tombstone lookup on the push create path; reconcile never pulls deletes. A long-offline host's edited-then-server-deleted file resurrects as a phantom create, and the proposed I74 consult has no durable tombstone to read after retention or after the row is dropped.

attack_scenarios.scenario: OFFLINE-THEN-RECONNECT WITH MANY LOCAL EDITS: shadow reflects PRE-OFFLINE state, server moved, local moved -> mass R4 CONFLICT, but the SSE catchup replays server events in order and EACH triggers a materialize that the per-path lock serializes -- yet the conflict copy is named by change_seq, and a single offline file may have MULTIPLE server change_seqs queued. The first replayed event stashes local and writes server-v1; the second event (server-v2, higher change_seq) hits a file that now == server-v1's basis in shadow, classifies as R3 PULL_CLEAN, overwrites cleanly. Net: the user's offline edit survives as ONE .conflict-from-<host>-<seq1> sibling -- acceptable. BUT if the replay order is not strictly monotonic (SSE Last-Event-ID resume can re-deliver, and the design relies on persist-before-assign idempotency), a re-delivered older event could stash AGAIN with a different seq, producing duplicate conflict siblings of the SAME local content. Not data loss, but conflict-copy proliferation. Survives on the zero-loss bar.
attack_scenarios.gap: Zero byte loss, but non-monotonic SSE re-delivery on resume can spawn duplicate conflict siblings of the same offline edit (one per re-delivered change_seq), since the stash name keys on server change_seq, not on local content hash.

attack_scenarios.scenario: CRASH MID-WRITE: STASH-BEFORE-RENAME ORDER IS CORRECT, BUT THE WINNER RENAME + restore_server_times + integrity-check SEQUENCE CAN LEAVE A WINNER WITH THE LOSER ALREADY GONE FROM SHADOW. The design stashes the loser first (good). But verified materializer.rs order: stash (512) -> atomic tmp+rename of WINNER (544) -> restore_server_times (553) -> integrity check (556-585). If the process crashes AFTER rename but BEFORE the shadow is recorded (shadow.record at 479/601 is on the success/identical paths), the canonical file is now server-content, the loser sits as a .conflict sibling, but shadow still holds the PRE-write hash. On restart, decide() sees local(server-content) != shadow(pre) -> if server unchanged, R3-ish or R4 again -> could RE-STASH the now-server-content file as another conflict sibling. Loser is safe (already on disk), so ZERO data loss, but idempotency-under-crash produces extra conflict copies. The design claims 'on restart the reconcile backstop re-resolves idempotently' -- that is true for byte-safety but NOT for conflict-copy count. Survives the zero-loss bar; fails a strict 'no spurious conflict copies' bar.
attack_scenarios.gap: Stash-before-overwrite makes crash-mid-write byte-safe (loser preserved), but shadow is recorded only after a fully successful write, so a crash between rename and shadow-record can spawn an extra conflict copy on restart. Idempotent in bytes, not in conflict-copy count.

attack_scenarios.scenario: SERVER SERVES STALE BYTES VIA get_note WHILE FS AND PG DISAGREE (I14 gap during the fix's own window). Verified get_note:1083-1093 serves the FS file bytes when present and sets content_hash=sha(FS). With push writing PG first (commit) and FS after, there is a window where PG.body=B but get_note returns FS=A (sha A) with content_hash=sha(A). A subscriber doing a live SSE fetch_note in that window pulls A, and its decide() (shadow may == its last-synced) could classify A as a legitimate server state and overwrite a DIFFERENT host's correct B. This is the multi-subscriber amplification of the commit-to-replace gap: the bug is not just 'reconciler reverts' but 'every reader of get_note in that window propagates A'. The design's fix (push-materialize) shrinks the window but does not eliminate it without ordering FS-write INSIDE the committed txn (which os.replace cannot join). MUST-FIX: either serve vault_notes.body when sha(FS)!=fs_hash-of-record (the design's rejected 'less-invasive read alternative'), or hold a per-path server lock spanning commit+replace so no reader sees the split. The design picks push-materialize and explicitly leaves this read-window open.
attack_scenarios.gap: get_note serves FS bytes; push commits PG before writing FS, so a read in the commit-to-replace gap returns the OLD bytes with a self-consistent old hash, and any subscriber fetching then can propagate the stale copy. os.replace cannot be made transactional with conn.commit(), so the window is intrinsic to the chosen design unless reads are gated or prefer PG body.

attack_scenarios.scenario: CONTENT NORMALIZATION (CRLF/BOM) NOT IN THE DIFF BASIS -> FALSE CONFLICTS BETWEEN Neo (CRLF) AND Trinity/link (LF). Verified normalize_for_diff:686-718 strips only frontmatter fields; it does NOT normalize line endings or BOM. The design mentions 'normalize CRLF/LF before any future 3-way merge' as a FUTURE item. But the conflict DETECTION (local != server) runs on raw bytes minus frontmatter. A Neo editor that saves CRLF and a Trinity copy in LF are byte-different -> every cross-edit between them classifies as R4 CONFLICT and stashes, forever. Not data loss, but a permanent conflict-copy generator for any note touched on both Windows and Unix hosts. The design under-weights this: it is not a 'future merge' nicety, it is a present false-conflict source the moment Neo rejoins. Survives zero-loss, fails steady-state-convergence (the test matrix's 'zero steady-state churn' claim is FALSE for any CRLF-divergent note).
attack_scenarios.gap: Line-ending/BOM not in the diff basis; any note edited on both Neo (CRLF) and a Unix host is permanently classified as a conflict and stashed on every sync, violating the 'zero steady-state churn' / N4 convergence property even though no bytes are lost.

attack_scenarios.scenario: DEDUP-DROPS-REAL-EDIT FIX RELIES ON 'gate dedup on server-accepted shadow hash' BUT THE 400-SKIP PATH RECORDS NO SHADOW AND ALSO DOES NOT CLEAR enqueued_hashes. Verified: enqueued_hashes is inserted only on SUCCESSFUL journal append (file_watcher.rs:879-883), and a push HTTP 400 returns Skipped (push_client.rs:519-525) without recording shadow. The design proposes gating dedup on the ShadowStore last-accepted hash. But there is a SECOND path: the file_watcher inserts enqueued_hashes at ENQUEUE time (successful append to journal), BEFORE the push is attempted. So the sequence is: edit shaA -> append succeeds -> enqueued_hashes[p]=shaA -> push later returns 400 (dropped) -> shadow NOT recorded -> user reverts to shaA. Under the PROPOSED gate (dedup on shadow), shadow has no shaA entry so the revert re-enqueues -- the fix works ONLY IF the in-memory enqueued_hashes gate is fully REPLACED by the shadow gate. The design hedges ('or invalidate the enqueued_hashes entry on any non-Accepted push outcome') -- that invalidation requires push_client to call back into file_watcher's map, a cross-module mutation the design does not wire concretely. If the in-memory gate REMAINS as a first check (likely, since it is the hot path), shaA is still in enqueued_hashes and the revert is still dropped. MUST-FIX: specify the exact removal/invalidation of enqueued_hashes on non-Accepted outcomes; a shadow-gate ADDED in front of a retained enqueued_hashes gate does not fix the drop.
attack_scenarios.gap: The dedup fix is under-specified: enqueued_hashes is set at enqueue time and not cleared on a 400-drop; unless it is fully replaced by (not layered with) the shadow-accepted gate, the revert-to-dropped-content edit is still silently dropped. The proposed cross-module invalidation is not concretely wired.

attack_scenarios.scenario: LIVENESS: WATCHER HEARTBEAT CLOSES is_stalled BLIND SPOT, BUT THE SERVER-SIDE FANOUT-DORMANCY (FM3) GUARD IS A METRIC, NOT A FENCE. Verified is_stalled:126-127 requires pending>0 (the FM1 residual). The daemon-side heartbeat fix is sound. But FM3's server side -- the separate nexus-sync fanout container that turns committed rows into SSE -- is addressed only by 'surface last-fanout-emit age in /api/sync/lag so a dormant daemon ALARMS'. An alarm is not a recovery. If that container dies, pushes still commit (queryable), but NO subscriber gets the SSE wake, so live propagation silently stops fleet-wide until a human reads the lag metric. The design's own liveness invariant I-NEW-85 covers the DAEMON enqueue/drain side; it does NOT put a watchdog on the server fanout producer. MUST-FIX: the server fanout producer needs an auto-restart/self-heal watchdog (like the conductor-sidecar-watchdog pattern), not just a lag gauge -- otherwise FM3's 'push accepted but never reflected to subscribers' recurs as a silent-liveness failure with only a passive metric.
attack_scenarios.gap: Server-side fanout-producer dormancy (the FM3 root) gets a lag METRIC, not a self-healing watchdog. A dead fanout container silently halts all live propagation fleet-wide; queryable-but-not-pushed recurs until a human notices the gauge.

attack_scenarios.scenario: materializer_mode='shadow' SUBSCRIBER: the design's Phase-0 SQL check + startup tray WARN is the FM2 guard. But the WARN is advisory; nothing PREVENTS a 'shadow'-mode daemon from running live and materializing nothing into the vault. If the Phase-0 PATCH is missed for one host (e.g. Neo when it boots later, or a newly added subscriber that defaults to 'shadow' per migration 2026_05_25_005:31), that host pulls NOTHING into its vault and the operator sees only a tray warning they may dismiss. This is silent pull-incompleteness (FM2) persisting for any host not manually patched. MUST-FIX: flip the server DEFAULT off 'shadow' for user-facing subscribers (the design lists this as an open question, i.e. UNDECIDED) -- leaving the default at 'shadow' + relying on per-host manual PATCH guarantees FM2 recurs on the next host that joins without the manual step. A tray WARN is not a fix for a default that fails closed-to-silent.
attack_scenarios.gap: FM2 fix depends on a manual per-host PATCH because the server default stays 'shadow' (left as an open question). Any host added/booted without the manual flip silently materializes nothing into the vault; the only guard is a dismissible tray warning.

must_fix_before_ship: RECONCILER RECENCY/PROVENANCE GUARD IS NOT OPTIONAL. os.replace in push_file runs AFTER conn.commit() returns, so vault_reconcile_state.fs_hash=B is visible to the 60s sweep while the on-disk file is still A. The sweep (_read_pg_hash reads fs_hash=B, recomputes sha(disk)=A, reverts) re-arms TKT-2dc9a17e in the commit-to-replace gap. Ship BOTH the push FS-materialize AND the reconciler 'skip-or-re-materialize when PG row is a newer nexus-sync-push' guard in the SAME server deploy; the design's hedge ('or at minimum the always-stop-the-revert skip') must resolve to the full provenance+recency guard, not the weaker variant.

CLOSE THE get_note READ WINDOW. While PG=B but FS=A (commit-to-replace gap), get_note:1083-1093 serves A with a self-consistent sha(A); any subscriber's live fetch_note in that window propagates A and can overwrite another host's correct B. Either gate get_note to prefer vault_notes.body when sha(FS) != the fs_hash-of-record, or hold a per-storage_key server lock spanning commit+os.replace. os.replace cannot join the PG txn, so this window is intrinsic and must be explicitly closed, not just shrunk.

AUDIT AND SERIALIZE ALL SERVER-SIDE vault_notes WRITERS, NOT JUST /push. The ~15 concurrent agent writers go through brain edit_note / note_writer / reconciler paths that bypass the /push CAS (FOR UPDATE on vault_reconcile_state). A non-sync writer can clobber a freshly-pushed body and the reconciler will then bless the clobber. The daemon's per-path dashmap Mutex is in-process only and does nothing for this. Enumerate every writer of vault_notes/vault_reconcile_state and bring them under the same CAS + version-snapshot discipline, or '~15 writers safe' is unproven.

PROVE THE note-version TRIGGER COVERS THE RECONCILER _upsert AND brain/note_writer FS-write PATHS. The design admits it 'could not confirm statically' that 2026_05_31_002 fires on the reconciler write. Server-side 'preserve both sides' rests entirely on this trigger (there is no on-disk .conflict copy for server-side overwrites). If the trigger does not snapshot before a reconciler revert or a brain overwrite, a server-side silent overwrite is UNRECOVERABLE -- a direct violation of the operator ruling. Verify and, if needed, extend the trigger before shipping.

FULLY REPLACE (NOT LAYER) THE enqueued_hashes DEDUP WITH THE SERVER-ACCEPTED SHADOW GATE. enqueued_hashes is set at successful journal-append time (file_watcher.rs:879-883) and is NOT cleared on a 400-drop (push_client.rs:519-525 returns Skipped without touching the map). Adding a shadow-hash gate in FRONT of a retained enqueued_hashes check still drops a revert-to-previously-enqueued-content edit. Specify the concrete removal/invalidation of enqueued_hashes on every non-Accepted push outcome, or the FM1 dedup-drop persists.

FIX THE DELETE TOMBSTONE SUBSTRATE BEFORE CLAIMING delete-vs-modify SAFETY. _delete_note_and_state DROPS the vault_reconcile_state row, push_file's create branch accepts base_hash='' as a fresh create, and reconcile 'never pulls deletes'. There is no durable tombstone for the push path to consult. A long-offline host's edited-then-server-deleted file resurrects as a phantom create (or, the converse, honors a stale local delete and loses the edit). Implement a retention-bounded tombstone that BOTH the daemon reconcile catchup and the server push-create path check; the proposed I74 consult currently has nothing durable to read.

PUT A SELF-HEALING WATCHDOG ON THE SERVER FANOUT PRODUCER (FM3 root), NOT JUST A LAG GAUGE. The separate nexus-sync container that converts committed rows to SSE has no auto-restart in the design -- only a /api/sync/lag metric. If it dies, all live propagation halts fleet-wide (pushes commit, queryable, but never reach subscribers) until a human reads the gauge. I-NEW-85 covers only the daemon enqueue/drain side. Add an out-of-band watchdog (conductor-sidecar-watchdog pattern) that restarts the producer on stalled fanout_notifies.

DECIDE THE materializer_mode DEFAULT NOW; DO NOT SHIP IT AS AN OPEN QUESTION. Migration 2026_05_25_005:31 defaults new subscribers to 'shadow', which silently materializes nothing into the vault (FM2). A tray WARN is dismissible and is not a fix. Flip the server default to 'live' for user-facing vault subscribers (or add a hard startup REFUSE-to-run-live-while-shadow gate). Relying on a manual per-host PATCH guarantees FM2 recurs on the next host that joins.

HARDEN THE SHADOW NFC MIGRATION + always-stash INTERACTION AGAINST A CONFLICT-COPY STORM. The one-time NFC re-key of shadow runs simultaneously with the new always-stash floor. Any single bug in write_stash (or any transient shadow miss during the re-key) turns into mass R5 CONFLICT and a fleet-wide flood of .conflict-from-Trinity-* copies that buries genuine conflicts. Make the migration provably idempotent and the always-stash path exception-proof, and bake it in a non-destructive shadow mode (Phase 3) with an explicit conflict-rate ceiling that fails the gate, before any host re-enables live sync.

THREAD A REAL local_lsn OR PROVE R2 CAPTURES EFFECTIVELY ALL GENUINE LOCAL EDITS. With local_lsn=None, NewerWins (conflict_stash.rs:227 'unknown local lsn = treat as older') is operationally ServerWins-plus-a-stash: every shadow!=server divergence demotes the live local edit to a conflict sibling and gives the server copy the canonical name. The ruling's 'local edits MUST propagate UP' is honored ONLY for R2 (shadow==server). A wiped/stale shadow, or any pre-existing unsynced edit, drops a genuine newer edit into R4/R5 and demotes it. Either wire real local_lsn so newer truly wins, or demonstrate that R2 detection is robust enough that genuine local edits essentially never fall to R4/R5.

ADD LINE-ENDING/BOM TO THE CONFLICT-DETECTION BASIS BEFORE Neo REJOINS. normalize_for_diff (materializer.rs:686-718) strips only frontmatter fields; CRLF vs LF is a raw-byte difference. Any note edited on Neo (CRLF) and a Unix host (LF) is permanently classified R4 CONFLICT and stashed on every sync -- a perpetual conflict-copy generator and a direct violation of the design's own 'zero steady-state churn' / N4 convergence claim. This must be in the diff normalization now (it is mis-scoped as a 'future 3-way merge' item), gated by the Neo re-verify.
critiques.verdict: REFUTED as a fix that 'ends the bug for good.' The design correctly identifies all three silent-clobber engines plus the Unicode amplifier, and its DIRECTION (always-stash floor, unified decide(), NFC keying, change_seq ordering, server FS-materialize) is sound and well-precedented (Syncthing/Obsidian/Dropbox). On the strict zero-DATA-LOSS bar it largely holds: I verified the always-stash-before-overwrite and stash-before-rename ordering preserve both byte-sets for the daemon-mediated cases, so most attacks survive without losing bytes. BUT the claim that this ENDS the bug is false for at least four concrete, code-verified reasons: (1) os.replace in push_file runs AFTER conn.commit() returns, leaving a real commit-to-replace window where the 60s reconciler reads the already-updated fs_hash(B) against the still-stale on-disk A and reverts -- the SAME TKT-2dc9a17e mechanism -- unless the reconciler recency guard (hedged as optional) ships in the same deploy; (2) get_note serves FS bytes in that same window, so any subscriber fetching then propagates the stale copy fleet-wide, an amplification the design shrinks but does not close because os.replace cannot join the PG txn; (3) the ~15 concurrent writers are SERVER-SIDE brain/note_writer/edit_note paths that bypass the /push CAS entirely, and the proposed per-path lock is daemon-in-process-only, so a non-sync writer can still clobber a freshly-pushed body with no stash and the reconciler will bless it; (4) server-side overwrites have NO on-disk conflict copy and rely on a version trigger of self-admittedly UNVERIFIED coverage, so a server-side silent overwrite may be unrecoverable -- a direct violation of the operator ruling. Additional non-loss but ruling-violating gaps: local_lsn is never wired so 'NewerWins' is really 'ServerWins+stash' that demotes genuine newer local edits to siblings on any shadow!=server divergence; the delete tombstone substrate (row-drop delete, base_hash='' create accepted, reconcile never pulls deletes) contradicts the asserted resurrection guard; CRLF/BOM is absent from the diff basis so every cross-OS-edited note becomes a permanent conflict generator the moment Neo rejoins; FM3's server fanout producer gets a lag metric not a watchdog; FM2's fix depends on a manual per-host PATCH because the materializer_mode default stays 'shadow'. Net: SHIP the server-first reconciler+materialize fix (it stops the observed Trinity revert in the common path), but do NOT represent it as permanent until the eleven must-fix items -- especially the non-optional reconciler recency guard, the get_note read-window closure, the server-side-writer audit, and the version-trigger coverage proof -- are landed and the Phase-3 shadow bake + Neo re-verify pass. CAN-STILL-LOSE-DATA: true, narrowly but really, via the server-side non-/push writer clobber (no stash, version-trigger coverage unproven) and the unrecoverable-server-overwrite-without-on-disk-conflict-copy path.

attack_scenarios.scenario: THE live bug (TKT-2dc9a17e), but the operator SKIPS Phase 0 / never runs cypher-deploy for the server changes. The design is SERVER-FIRST by its own admission: 'a daemon-only fix cannot defend against a server-side revert.' ROOT 1 is the reconciler.py sweep_once (verified lines 435-438: `if pg_hash==local_hash: continue; body=path.read_text(); _upsert_note_and_state(...)` origin='reconciler-cypher', zero recency/CAS guard) PLUS push_file writing ONLY Postgres (verified lines 1448-1537: `_cas_write_note_and_state(...)` then `return`, no write_text/os.replace anywhere in the body) while get_note_body serves FS bytes (verified lines 1089-1092: `raw=fs_resolved.read_bytes()`). The entire daemon-side fix package (NewerWins, always-stash, NFC, per-path-lock) is powerless against this: the revert happens in Python on cypher within <=60s and re-fans via change_seq. If the rollout is partial / a host re-enables before Phase 1 lands, the bug reproduces verbatim.
attack_scenarios.gap: The data-loss engine is server-side and OUTSIDE every daemon decision. Survival is 100% contingent on the server deploy (reconciler guard + push FS-materialize) landing AND being verified FIRST. The design ACKNOWLEDGES this and sequences it Phase 1, but the architecture means there is a live window where a daemon upgrade alone gives false confidence. must_fix: gate every host re-enable on a POSITIVE server-side proof that the reconciler no longer reverts (the new reconciler-no-revert test must run against the deployed cypher, not just CI).

attack_scenarios.scenario: New-note-pull-create onto a subscriber whose vault_subscribers.materializer_mode row is the DEFAULT 'shadow' (verified: migration 2026_05_25_005 line 31-32 / line 72 `server_default="shadow"`). The daemon's pull_backfill.rs is real and correct (verified: run_pull_backfill enumerates /changes since=0, plan_backfill selects missing-safe-nonsubstrate, materializer.write create-writes). BUT Materializer.target_for (verified materializer.rs:382-389) routes Shadow mode to `<workspace>/.lattice-runtime/<slug>/shadow/<rel>` — NOT the vault. So a 'shadow' subscriber faithfully materializes every server-only note into a runtime tree the user never opens. FM2 is fully live for it despite the fixed R6 code. The daemon NEVER self-flips the mode (verified lib.rs:547 reads snap.materializer_mode and passes it through; no PATCH-to-live anywhere).
attack_scenarios.gap: FM2 is a CONFIG-state bug, not a code bug, and the design cannot fix it in the shipped binary. It depends on a manual Phase-0b SQL check + PATCH of three rows, or changing the server DEFAULT. If that verify step is skipped, server-origin notes silently never land in the vault — exactly FM2. The design flags this as the single highest-leverage FM2 fix and an open_question, which is honest, but 'ends the bug for good' is false while the default is 'shadow' and the flip is manual. must_fix: change the server DEFAULT off 'shadow' for user-facing subscribers OR make the daemon assert+self-heal its mode to 'live' on startup and tray-WARN otherwise (the design proposes the WARN but leaves the flip manual).

attack_scenarios.scenario: ~15 concurrent agent writers hammering one note, mixed with SSE+reconcile events on the same path. The design adds a per-path dashmap<String,Mutex<()>> inside write() to serialize exists->compare->stash->rename. This closes the in-process lost-update window the current atomic-tmp+rename does NOT cover (verified: materializer.rs:469 read, :544 persist, with no lock between). The conflict filename keyed on change_seq (not wall-clock) is deterministic so N writers converge on one name instead of N re-conflicting copies.
attack_scenarios.gap: Two residual holes. (1) The per-path lock is IN-PROCESS only; it does NOT serialize the daemon's write() against Obsidian or another local process writing the same file between the compare (materializer.rs:469 fs::read) and the rename — a TOCTOU still exists vs external writers, though atomic rename prevents a torn file. (2) change_seq-keyed stash names are deterministic ACROSS hosts but the design threads local_lsn as 0/None (verified conflict_stash.rs:198,231 use local_lsn.unwrap_or(0)), so on a SINGLE host two genuinely-different local divergent versions at the same server change_seq collide on the base name and rely on the -2/-3 suffix loop (verified resolve_collision conflict_stash.rs:315-336) — correct but means the 'deterministic fleet-convergent name' property is weaker than claimed when local content differs. No data loss (both survive via suffix), but the convergence guarantee is partial.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Genuine local edit while a server enrichment_complete SSE fires for the same path (R2: shadow==server, local!=server) — the exact silent-revert shape on the live path. The design rewrites the Diverges arm to read shadow inside write(), return Skipped(LocalEditPreserved), and have sse.rs honor it (not write). This directly closes the verified bypass (sse.rs:271-275 blind write; materializer.rs:484-519 never reads shadow; lib.rs:510-513 leaves ServerWins default).
attack_scenarios.gap: Survives IF implemented as specified AND the shadow entry is actually present and correct for that path. The shadow is recorded only on a prior successful materialize/accepted-push (verified materializer.rs:479-481,601-603; push_client records on Accepted). For a file the daemon has NEVER synced (fresh local creation that also exists on server with different content), shadow is absent -> R5 -> CONFLICT/stash, which is the safe outcome the design intends. So this scenario is genuinely fixed by the design. The only caveat is correct R2-vs-R5 classification hinging on shadow fidelity, addressed by the next scenario.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Shadow store wiped/corrupt, OR the NFC-key migration mass-misses on cutover. Current code: ShadowStore::load on corrupt/missing returns EMPTY (verified sync_shadow.rs:57-85, logs warn, no panic). Today empty-shadow on the reconcile path => decide_direction Pulls (verified verify_repair.rs:248-252) => ServerWins overwrite => silent loss. The design's R5 (shadow absent => CONFLICT => stash, never silent Pull) makes this fail SAFE, and ships the NFC re-key migration together with always-stash so a residual miss preserves the local copy.
attack_scenarios.gap: The design's own AMPLIFIER analysis is the live risk: there is genuinely NO unicode-normalization anywhere in the daemon (confirmed: no unicode-normalization crate; file_watcher normalize_path only does the lexical strip at lines 604/609; sync_shadow keys raw). macOS persists NFD, the shadow/manifest/wire keys are raw OS byte-form, so for any non-ASCII filename, Trinity-authored shadow keys (NFD) will NOT match a link/Neo NFC lookup -> shadow miss on EVERY accented/emoji note cross-host. With R5+always-stash this degrades to a conflict-copy STORM (every non-ASCII note spawns a .conflict-from on the first post-cutover reconcile) rather than data loss — survivable but noisy and operationally alarming. The mass-miss is bounded ONLY if the NFC migration (sync_shadow.rs load-time re-key) ships in the SAME release as always-stash, which the design correctly mandates as a hard co-ship gate.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Dead/fenced watcher with pending==0 (FM1 residual / dormancy lens core). Verified: the storm circuit-breaker BREAKS the watcher event loop permanently (file_watcher.rs:739-744 `if me.is_fenced() { error!; break; }`) and Linux inotify ENOSPC fails the initial watch (file_watcher.rs:683-689). Verified: sync_health::is_stalled returns `pending > 0 && elapsed >= threshold` (line 127), and the watchdog only reads push-journal depth (lib.rs:888 spawn_progress_stall_watchdog). A fenced watcher stops ENQUEUEING, so pending stays 0, so is_stalled is FALSE forever — the daemon reports healthy while silently capturing zero local edits. This is a SILENT-FAILURE (not data loss yet, but every subsequent local edit is invisible until something else pushes it).
attack_scenarios.gap: The current watchdog cannot see a dead enqueue path — it is structurally blind to the producer side. The design's I-NEW-85 (watcher heartbeat + watcher_fenced atomic into SyncHealth; watchdog fires on fenced OR heartbeat-stale REGARDLESS of pending) is the correct fix and is specified. But it is NEW code not yet present, and it is the kind of liveness instrumentation that is easy to under-test. Until it ships, a fenced watcher is an invisible silent failure. must_fix: the watcher heartbeat + fenced-flag must land AND have a test that fires recovery at pending==0.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Dedup drops a real edit (FM1, the enqueued_hashes hole). Verified: file_watcher.rs:843-855 dedups on `enqueued_hashes.get(path) == Some(sha)` and records on successful append (879-883). Verified: a 400-rejected push returns Skipped(ServerRejected{400}) and ACKS without recording a shadow (push_client.rs ~line 515-520). Sequence: enqueue shaA -> push 400-dropped+acked -> enqueued_hashes still holds shaA -> user reverts file to shaA content -> dedup matches -> edit NEVER re-enqueues. Silent loss of a legitimate revert.
attack_scenarios.gap: Real and live at v0.4.22. The design's fix (gate dedup on the ShadowStore last-server-ACCEPTED hash, not enqueued_hashes; invalidate on any non-Accepted outcome) is correct but NEW. Note a subtlety the design glosses: the dedup is keyed on the WATCHER's enqueued_hashes which is in-memory and lost on restart, so a restart partially papers over this — but within one process lifetime the hole is exactly as described. must_fix: re-key dedup on server-accepted shadow OR invalidate enqueued_hashes on any non-Accepted push outcome.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Scope asymmetry: a subscriber with scope_roots/excludes. Verified TWO divergent scope engines exist: api/scope_filter_match.py uses `fnmatch` (lines 23,60 — `*` crosses `/`), services/vault_sync/scope_filter.py uses pathspec/GitWildMatchPattern (lines 17-18,43 — gitignore semantics, `*` does NOT cross `/`). Verified get_note_body + get_changes use _require_bearer_with_scope + path_in_scope (sync_routes_p1.py:1009-1037, 705-718) while reconcile_batch uses bare require_bearer with NO scope (line 394). Verified the daemon's pull_backfill should_backfill (pull_backfill.rs:92-103) applies is_safe_path + substrate fence but NO path_in_scope at all, whereas sse.rs:262 DOES apply path_in_scope.
attack_scenarios.gap: Three-way asymmetry (push-scope via fnmatch on /note, pull-enumeration via pathspec on /changes, reconcile-batch unscoped, daemon pull_backfill unscoped) means a path can be in-scope for push but out-of-scope for pull (or vice versa) -> perpetual push/no-pull churn for scoped subscribers, and a cross-scope leak risk where pull_backfill pulls server notes the subscriber's scope intended to exclude. Not strictly data LOSS, but a silent-divergence + churn class the 'ends the bug' claim ignores. The design proposes unifying on gitwildmatch (I52 fix 5c) + adding path_in_scope to should_backfill (I-NEW-84), correctly flagged as behavior-changing and gated on auditing live subscriber rows. Survivable only because the fleet hosts currently sync the whole vault (no narrow scopes) — a latent trap the moment anyone sets scope_roots.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Delete-vs-edit race: host A deletes note X (server tombstone), host B edited X offline, B reconnects. Design adds the missing I37 delete-vs-modify row (modification wins, consult I74 tombstone to avoid phantom-create resurrection).
attack_scenarios.gap: The design SPECIFIES this but it is entirely NEW behavior — verified the current sse.rs DELETE path (sse.rs:266-269) calls soft_delete unconditionally, and soft_delete (materializer.rs:650-674) renames target to `.deleted-<ts>` with SECOND granularity (line 666 `%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ`) with NO check of local divergence. So today a server delete silently soft-deletes a locally-edited file (recoverable via the .deleted- sibling, so not permanent loss, but the edit is removed from the vault with only an info! log). The design's modification-wins rule fixes it, but until shipped the delete-vs-edit silent removal is live. Also note: tombstone retention is 24h (open_question #7) so a host offline >24h misses the delete entirely and the resurrection guard has nothing to consult -> re-uploads a phantom create. Bounded gap, acknowledged.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Clock skew across the 3 OSes with ~15 writers. Design orders newer-wins by server change_seq (a server-side integer, verified to be the cursor basis in get_changes line 271-274 and catchup line 709-725), never by file mtime. This is the correct skew-immune choice.
attack_scenarios.gap: No gap on the DECISION basis — change_seq is monotonic server-side and identical on all hosts. The only residual is restore_server_times (verified materializer.rs:139-169) still STAMPS mtime+birthtime from payload.created/file_mtime, and when both are absent it early-returns (line 147-149) leaving the atomic-rename's birthtime=now, which is the macOS Created-sort clobber the design's restore_server_times fix targets. That is a cosmetic/sort issue, not data loss, and decisioning never uses mtime. Survives for data-integrity purposes.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Crash mid-write between stashing the loser and renaming the winner. Design mandates stash-before-overwrite (write the .conflict loser via atomic tmp+rename FIRST, then materialize the winner), so a crash leaves the loser persisted and the reconcile backstop re-resolves idempotently.
attack_scenarios.gap: Survives for the daemon path as specified (the existing stash + materialize are both atomic tmp+rename, verified conflict_stash.rs:306-310 and materializer.rs:541-544). One residual: the SERVER push FS-materialize the design ADDS (write VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key> after the CAS commit) introduces a NEW crash window — CAS commits to PG, then the process dies before the os.replace lands, leaving PG=B but FS=A. On restart the reconciler sweep would then revert PG to A unless the reconciler guard (skip-when-PG-newer) is also in place. The design's reconciler recency/provenance guard covers this, but ONLY if both server changes ship together; the push-materialize alone, without the reconciler guard, would re-open ROOT 1 on a mid-write crash. must_fix: server reconciler guard and push FS-materialize are co-dependent and must deploy atomically.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Server FS-write failure on push (container lacks rw vault mount). Design returns non-2xx so the daemon retries, never a silent 'accepted' for an unreadable write.
attack_scenarios.gap: The design's open_question #1 explicitly flags it could NOT confirm the push container has rw access to VAULT_ROOT. If only the reconciler container can write FS (plausible — the reconciler IS described as 'the authoritative FS->PG writer' in reconciler.py:379-380), then push FS-materialize is impossible as specified and the whole ROOT 1 primary fix must fall back to the 'serve vault_notes.body when fs_hash != sha(FS)' read-change alternative, which the design mentions but does not fully design. This is an unresolved architectural fork that determines whether the PRIMARY fix is even viable. must_fix: Phase-0b must confirm rw vault mount on the push container BEFORE committing to FS-materialize-on-push.

attack_scenarios.scenario: local_lsn is threaded as 0/None so 'newer-wins' is not actually newer-wins yet — it is always-stash-on-divergence. Design accepts this as the safe floor and defers true newer-wins (open_question #9).
attack_scenarios.gap: Not a data-loss gap (always-stash preserves both sides). But it means a legitimate, intended server update to a file the user has NOT locally edited but whose shadow is stale (e.g. after the NFC cutover miss, or a wiped shadow) will be treated as a CONFLICT and spawn a conflict-copy rather than cleanly pulling — converting the old silent-data-LOSS into a conflict-copy PROLIFERATION. Operationally the fleet (~15 writers, many notes) could see a large burst of .conflict-from-* files on first post-deploy reconcile, which the operator may misread as a new bug. Survivable (no loss) but the 'clean' claim is overstated; needs the bake phase (Phase 3) to confirm no conflict storm, which the design does include.

critiques.verdict: REFUTED as 'ends the bug for good' — but the design is fundamentally SOUND and its root-cause analysis is accurate. I independently verified every cited file:line: ROOT 1 (reconciler.py sweep_once lines 435-438 unconditional revert + push_file lines 1448-1537 writes ONLY Postgres + get_note_body lines 1089-1092 serves FS bytes) is REAL and is the primary, server-side, OS-independent data-loss engine. ROOT 2 (lib.rs:510-513 leaves ServerWins default + sse.rs:271-275 blind write + materializer.rs:484-519 never reads shadow) is REAL. ROOT 3 (verify_repair.rs:248-252 Pull-on-shadow-absent + conflict_stash.rs:210-215 ServerWins/Class-C no-stash, reason literally 'silently overwritten') is REAL. The Unicode amplifier is REAL (no normalization crate anywhere). The fix DIRECTION — NewerWins+always-stash, unify the SSE and reconcile paths onto one shadow-aware decide(), stash-before-overwrite for ALL divergent content, server reconciler guard + push FS-materialize, NFC keys — is correct and matches external precedent. IF the full four-part package ships server-FIRST, atomically within its co-dependent groups, AND the three Phase-0 config/infra verifications pass, the design closes the verified bug. My refutation rests on residual gaps that survive a PARTIAL or mis-sequenced rollout, plus latent traps the 'for good' framing ignores: (1) FM2 is a CONFIG-state bug — materializer_mode DEFAULTs to 'shadow' (migration line 31/72) and the daemon never self-flips, so server-origin notes silently never reach the vault unless three rows are manually PATCHed; the shipped binary cannot fix this. (2) The dormancy lens core — a fenced/dead watcher (file_watcher.rs:739-744 break; inotify ENOSPC) with pending==0 reads HEALTHY because is_stalled gates on pending>0 (sync_health.rs:127); the watcher-heartbeat fix (I-NEW-85) is specified but NOT yet present, so silent local-edit-blindness is live. (3) The enqueued_hashes dedup-drop (file_watcher.rs:843-855 + 400-skip-ack) can silently swallow a legitimate revert. (4) Three-way scope asymmetry (fnmatch on /note vs pathspec on /changes vs unscoped reconcile-batch + unscoped pull_backfill) is a latent churn/leak trap the moment any subscriber sets scope_roots. (5) The PRIMARY ROOT 1 fix hinges on an UNVERIFIED assumption — open_question #1 admits it could not confirm the push container has rw vault access; if it doesn't, FS-materialize-on-push is impossible and the fallback read-change is under-designed. The design is honest about most of these (they appear as open_questions / Phase-0 gates), which is to its credit, but 'ends the bug for good' is false: it ends the bug IF AND ONLY IF the human executes a precise, fail-closed, server-first rollout with three live verifications, and even then leaves a non-ASCII conflict-copy-storm risk and an unverified push-container-mount dependency.
must_fix_before_ship: SERVER-FIRST, ATOMIC CO-DEPLOY (non-negotiable): the reconciler recency/provenance guard (stop unconditional revert in reconciler.py sweep_once 435-438) and push FS-materialize-on-accept (sync_routes_p1.py 1448-1537) are CO-DEPENDENT — push-materialize alone re-opens ROOT 1 on a mid-write crash (PG=B, FS=A, sweep reverts), and the reconciler guard alone leaves the FS stale. Ship them in the SAME server release, reconciler-guard logic first, and BLOCK every daemon re-enable until a reconciler-no-revert test passes against the DEPLOYED cypher (not just CI).

RESOLVE open_question #1 BEFORE building the server fix: confirm the API/push container that serves /api/sync/push has rw access to VAULT_ROOT. If it does NOT (the reconciler is described as the authoritative FS writer in reconciler.py:379-380), FS-materialize-on-push is impossible and you MUST fully design the 'serve vault_notes.body when fs_hash != sha(FS file)' read-change alternative instead. This is an unresolved architectural fork that determines whether the PRIMARY fix is even viable — do not start Phase 1 until it is answered.

FIX FM2 AS A SHIPPED-BEHAVIOR, not a manual PATCH: change the vault_subscribers.materializer_mode server DEFAULT off 'shadow' (migration 2026_05_25_005 line 31/72) for user-facing subscribers, AND make the daemon assert its effective mode is 'live' on startup, self-heal via PATCH /api/sync/subscribers/me, and tray-WARN loudly if it cannot. A 'shadow' subscriber materializes server-only notes into the runtime tree, never the vault (materializer.rs:382-389) — leaving the manual flip as the only safeguard means FM2 is one un-run SQL check away from staying live.

LAND I-NEW-85 watcher liveness WITH a test that fires recovery at pending==0: add a watcher heartbeat + watcher_fenced atomic into the shared SyncHealth, and have the watchdog fire on fenced-OR-heartbeat-stale REGARDLESS of pending. Today a fenced watcher (file_watcher.rs:739-744 break, inotify ENOSPC at file_watcher.rs:683-689) is invisible because is_stalled gates on pending>0 (sync_health.rs:127). Without this, the dormancy class that produced the 2026-06-13 incident is only half-closed (push side covered, enqueue side blind).

SHIP THE NFC SHADOW-KEY MIGRATION IN THE SAME RELEASE AS ALWAYS-STASH, and bake on Trinity (Phase 3) long enough to confirm NO conflict-copy storm: with zero normalization today and local_lsn unthreaded (always-stash-on-divergence), the cutover will spawn a .conflict-from-* for every non-ASCII-named note whose shadow key misses across NFD/NFC hosts. Verify the load-time re-key (sync_shadow.rs:57-85) NFC-normalizes existing keys and that the post-cutover reconcile produces no unexpected conflict burst BEFORE re-enabling any second host.

FIX THE DEDUP-DROP (file_watcher.rs:843-855): gate the enqueue dedup on the ShadowStore last-server-ACCEPTED hash, or invalidate the enqueued_hashes entry on ANY non-Accepted push outcome (the 400-skip-ack at push_client returns Skipped without recording a shadow). Otherwise enqueue shaA -> 400-drop -> revert-to-shaA silently never re-enqueues.

DECIDE the scope-engine unification (fnmatch in api/scope_filter_match.py vs pathspec/gitwildmatch in services/vault_sync/scope_filter.py) and add path_in_scope to pull_backfill should_backfill (pull_backfill.rs:92-103, currently unscoped) + reconcile_batch (sync_routes_p1.py:394, currently bare require_bearer). Even if deferred as orthogonal, AUDIT live vault_subscribers.scope_roots first and document that narrow scopes are UNSAFE until unified — the asymmetry is a latent churn/leak the moment anyone sets a scope.

Implement the delete-vs-modify row (modification wins + I74 tombstone resurrection guard) BEFORE re-enable: today sse.rs:266-269 -> soft_delete (materializer.rs:650-674) unconditionally renames a locally-edited file to a second-granularity .deleted-<ts> sibling on a server delete, with only an info! log. Add sub-second/nonce to the soft_delete suffix to avoid same-second collisions on ext4, and surface tombstone-retention (24h, open_question #7) as a documented multi-day-offline gap.

Neo (Windows) is DOWN: the ENTIRE Windows correctness story (ReplaceFileW vs non-atomic MoveFileExW, \\?\ long-path via dunce, SetFileTime birthtime on real NTFS, ReadDirectoryChangesW liveness which makes the read-walk filter a no-op) is code-read only. Keep Neo on MANUAL sync and treat a booted-Neo e2e re-verify as a HARD gate before Windows re-enables — do not ship Windows 'best-effort' into the re-enable path.

attack_scenarios.scenario: THE-LIVE-BUG re-armed by the cure: server-side FS writer race. The fix's centerpiece is 'FS-materialize-on-push' (sync_routes_p1.py push_file writes VAULT_ROOT/<storage_key> right after the CAS commit). But the vault FS already has MANY uncoordinated server-side writers -- confirmed by grep: references.py, brain/tools.py, brain/engine.py, brain/digest.py, vault_adapter.py, meetbot/wrap_up.py AND reconciler.py all write under VAULT_ROOT. The CAS row lock (sync_routes_p1.py:_cas_write_note_and_state) serializes ONLY the Postgres row; it does NOT hold any lock on the FS file. So: agent pushes B -> CAS commits PG.body=B -> push handler begins its os.replace(FS<-B); concurrently brain/note_writer enrichment writes FS<-A' for the same path (no shared lock). Last os.replace wins on the FS, but PG already committed B. Now FS=A', PG=B, fs_hash recomputed by the next reconciler pass = sha(A') != PG -> the reconciler reverts PG to A' (reconciler.py:434-438 still unconditional unless the recency guard lands) OR /api/sync/note serves A' (FS-preferred, sync_routes_p1.py:~1075) while PG says B. The daemon's per-path dashmap lock (materializer.rs proposal) is IN-PROCESS RUST on one host -- it is structurally incapable of serializing N Python writers in the server container. The design acknowledges 'a SECOND FS writer that must be serialized under the CAS row lock' in open_questions but provides NO mechanism: the CAS lock is a PG row lock, not an FS lock, and brain/note_writer does not take it. This is the single biggest gap -- the cure introduces a new silent-revert race on the exact bug it claims to kill.
attack_scenarios.gap: No server-side FS-write serialization across the >=8 existing VAULT_ROOT writers. The CAS row lock covers PG only; the daemon per-path lock is one host's in-process mutex. FS-materialize-on-push adds an Nth racing writer with no advisory file lock / single-writer funnel. Must funnel ALL server FS writes (brain, references, depositor, reconciler, push) through one serialized writer keyed by storage_key (e.g. a PG advisory lock pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(storage_key)) held across BOTH the PG commit AND the FS os.replace), or adopt the PG-body-preferred read alternative and stop serving/​hashing the FS file entirely.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Concurrent same-file edit on two hosts, BOTH classify as R2 PUSH (the live-bug's mirror image). Host A and Host B each have shadow==server (both last synced the same server rev S), then both edit locally and push near-simultaneously. decide() R2 says PUSH for BOTH (shadow==server, local!=server on each). A's push lands first: CAS base=S passes, server now at S+1=A. B's push: CAS base=S != current(A) -> 409 ConflictUnrecoverable (push_client.rs:511 returns Failed). Good so far -- but what does B DO with the 409? push_client returns Failed(ConflictUnrecoverable); the journal entry is consumed/failed. B's local edit is now NOT stashed (stash only happens on the PULL/materialize side, never on a push 409) and B has NO trigger to re-pull-and-stash. On B's next SSE enrichment_complete for that path, B fetches server=A, local=B, shadow=S: that's R4 CONFLICT -> stash B, write A. So B's edit survives ONLY IF an SSE event fires for that path on B AND the unified decide() is actually wired into the SSE path. If the 409 instead silently drops B's journal entry and no SSE re-fires (server fanout dormant -- a known FM3/secondary risk the design flags), B's edit is lost with no conflict copy. The design's stash-on-conflict is materialize-path-only; the push-409 path has no symmetric stash.
attack_scenarios.gap: A push CAS-409 loser has no stash. The design stashes only on the pull/materialize side (R4/R5). A genuine local edit that loses the CAS race relies on a subsequent SSE event to re-classify as R4 and stash -- but if fanout is dormant (the design itself flags 'separate nexus-sync container down -> fanout dies with zero error surface') or the journal entry is dropped on 409, the loser is silently gone. Must stash the local bytes on ConflictUnrecoverable in push_client BEFORE consuming the journal entry, not only on the pull side.

attack_scenarios.scenario: NFC cutover migration itself causes mass data loss on first boot. The design NFC-normalizes every existing shadow key on first load under the new version (sync_shadow.rs migration). But the shadow store is keyed by the SAME path string the materializer recorded -- which today is raw OS byte-form (NFD on macOS). After NFC migration the keys become NFC. Meanwhile the reconcile manifest paths and the server's stored path may STILL be in whatever form the server holds (the design says 'normalize delta.path on receipt' -- but that is a SERVER change that must deploy in lockstep). If the daemon migrates shadow keys to NFC but the server still echoes NFD-stored delta.path (or vice versa), shadow.get(NFC-key) misses on an NFD delta.path -> decide() R5 (shadow absent) -> CONFLICT-stash for EVERY non-ASCII note on first reconcile. That is not data loss (always-stash floor holds) but it IS a conflict-copy storm across the whole vault for every accented/emoji filename -- and the design's own test_matrix lists 'no mass-miss/mass-Pull spike' as a Phase-3 bake gate, implicitly admitting the migration can spike. Worse: if the always-stash floor is NOT yet deployed when the NFC migration ships (rollout ordering), a residual miss -> R5 on the OLD ServerWins path -> silent overwrite of every non-ASCII local edit. The design says they 'MUST ship together' but the rollout plan deploys SERVER first, then daemon -- the daemon NFC migration and the daemon always-stash land in the SAME Phase 2, so this is internally consistent ONLY if no host ever runs an intermediate build.
attack_scenarios.gap: Survives ONLY if always-stash and the NFC shadow-key migration are atomically the same daemon build AND the server path-normalization deploys before/with it. The lockstep is fragile: any host running an intermediate daemon (NFC keys, old ServerWins) silently overwrites every non-ASCII local edit. Add a hard version gate: refuse to run reconcile/SSE-overwrite if shadow_schema_version < NFC_MIGRATED. Also: case-folding (APFS/NTFS case-insensitive vs ext4 case-sensitive) is explicitly out-of-scope but is the same desync class -- a Note.md vs note.md pair will shadow-miss identically.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Crash mid-write between stash and rename, with the stash on a DIFFERENT inode-allocation path. The design says 'stash the loser FIRST (atomic tmp+rename), THEN write the winner' so a crash preserves the loser. Verified: ConflictStash::write_stash (conflict_stash.rs:306-310) is tmp+rename, atomic. BUT the echo_guard ordering the design mandates ('record the stash path+hash in echo_guard BEFORE writing it') is racy: echo_guard.record(path,sha) is keyed by PATH (echo_guard is path+sha), and the stash file has a DIFFERENT path (<stem>.conflict-from-...). The current echo_guard.record at materializer.rs:460-462 records payload.path (the canonical), not the stash path. If the design records the stash under the stash path, fine; if it reuses the canonical-path record, the watcher will see the stash-file create event, find no echo entry for the stash path, and PUSH the conflict copy UP to the server -- now the conflict copy propagates to all hosts and re-conflicts (Syncthing's exact N-copies-explosion the design claims to prevent via deterministic naming). Deterministic change_seq naming bounds the NAME, but if the stash is pushed and re-materialized on host C, host C sees a new file it lacks, creates it, and its watcher pushes it again only if not echo-guarded -- the convergence depends entirely on every host treating *.conflict-from-* as either substrate-fenced (NOT in rasp_fence today -- conflict files are NOT excluded in verify_repair HARDCODED_EXCLUDES) or echo-recorded under the correct stash path.
attack_scenarios.gap: Conflict-copy files are NOT excluded from the push manifest (verify_repair.rs HARDCODED_EXCLUDES lacks a *.conflict-from-* rule) and the echo_guard records the canonical path, not the stash path. Result: stash files get pushed up and re-fanned, re-conflicting on other hosts. Must (a) add *.conflict-from-*.md to the file_watcher AND verify_repair excludes so conflict copies are LOCAL-ONLY and never sync, and (b) echo-record under the actual stash path, not the canonical path.

attack_scenarios.scenario: ~15 concurrent writers + the per-path lock does not cover the cross-engine race it claims to. The design adds a dashmap<String,Mutex> inside materializer.write() to serialize exists->compare->stash->rename. But write() is called from TWO call sites (sse.rs:273 live path, verify_repair.rs:478 reconcile pull) AND the file_watcher/push pipeline writes NOTHING locally (it only reads + pushes). The real concurrent local writers on a single host are: (1) the SSE consumer task, (2) the reconcile backstop task, (3) ~15 agent processes editing the vault via their OWN file handles (Obsidian, python scripts, Edit tool) -- which do NOT go through materializer.write() at all. The dashmap lock serializes only (1) vs (2). An agent's external write to the same path between materializer's compare (materializer.rs:469 fs::read) and rename (:544) is a classic TOCTOU: materializer reads local=X, decides PULL/overwrite, agent writes local=Y, materializer renames server-bytes over Y -> Y is lost with NO stash (the compare already happened against the stale X). The atomic rename prevents a TORN file but not this lost update, and the design's own note admits 'two SSE/reconcile events could race' while missing that the dominant racer is the 15 external agents the mission explicitly calls out.
attack_scenarios.gap: The per-path lock is intra-daemon and cannot guard against the ~15 EXTERNAL agent writers (Obsidian/Edit/scripts) that write the vault directly, never through materializer.write(). The compare-then-rename window (materializer.rs:469->544) is a TOCTOU against external edits: an edit landing in that window is overwritten with no stash. Mitigation: re-read + re-compare the local file UNDER the per-path lock IMMEDIATELY before the rename, and if it changed since the initial compare, abort-and-restash (treat as fresh divergence). Even then, an external write strictly between the final re-read and the rename is unguardable without an OS file lock the editors don't take -- so the periodic reconcile backstop MUST be the safety net, and it must itself re-verify, not trust a stale manifest hash.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Host offline-then-reconnect: long-offline edits replay AFTER the server moved, CAS-409 cascade with no stash. Host B offline 6h, made 40 local edits; server advanced those same 40 notes via other hosts/brain. B reconnects: catchup + reconcile run. For each of the 40, B's journal/reconcile sees drift, shadow==old-server-rev (B's last sync), server==new -> shadow != server -> decide() R4/R5 CONFLICT -> stash B's local, pull server. GOOD -- IF decide() R4 fires. But the reconcile PUSH path (verify_repair.rs build_modify_push) for a drift-with-shadow==server case enqueues a PUSH with base_hash=server_current; if B's shadow is STALE (== old server rev, not current), decide_direction returns... let's trace: shadow=old, server=new, shadow!=server -> Direction::Pull (verify_repair.rs:248-252) -> stash+overwrite. OK that path stashes under the proposal. The danger is the OPPOSITE ordering: B's 40 edits also sit in B's push_journal from before it went offline. On reconnect push_client drains them with base_hash=B's-old-server-rev -> all 40 CAS-409 (server moved) -> Failed, journal entries consumed, NO stash (push-409 has no stash, see scenario 2). The reconcile pull then overwrites B's local with server, and B's 40 edits are GONE -- they were never stashed because the push path 409'd them away before reconcile could classify them as R4. Order of operations (drain-journal vs reconcile) determines whether the edit is stashed or silently lost.
attack_scenarios.gap: On reconnect, the pre-offline push_journal drains FIRST and 409s every edit the server has moved past, consuming the journal entry with no stash, BEFORE the reconcile backstop can classify the same path as R4-conflict-and-stash. The two pipelines (journal drain, reconcile) are not coordinated. Must either (a) stash-on-409 in push_client (scenario 2 fix), or (b) on reconnect, run reconcile-and-stash BEFORE draining the stale pre-offline journal, or (c) invalidate the pre-offline journal on reconnect and let reconcile re-derive direction from the local/shadow/server triple.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Clock skew is correctly dodged for ORDERING but the macOS birthtime restore still corrupts the Created sort under conflict. The design orders by change_seq (server integer) not mtime -- correct, immune to skew. restore_server_times (materializer.rs:139-169) is fixed to use payload.modified. But on a CONFLICT-stash-then-write, the WINNER materialized to the canonical path gets restore_server_times stamping the SERVER's created/mtime, while the LOSER stash file gets the tmp+rename birthtime=now (write_stash does NOT call restore_server_times). So after a conflict, Obsidian's Created-sort shows the conflict copy as 'created today' and the canonical as its true old date -- the operator sees a mysterious 'today' file (the conflict copy) at the top of the list. Minor vs data loss, but it re-introduces a flavor of the very Created-sort confusion the birthtime fix was meant to end, now on conflict copies. Not a survives=false (no data lost) but a UX regression that will generate 'why is this random file dated today' tickets.
attack_scenarios.gap: Conflict-copy stash files get birthtime=now (write_stash skips restore_server_times), so they sort to 'today' in Obsidian Created view -- cosmetic confusion, not data loss. Optionally stamp the loser's birthtime from its own last-known mtime.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Shadow store wiped/corrupt mid-flight -> R5 conflict storm is SAFE but unbounded. ShadowStore::load (sync_shadow.rs:57) on corrupt JSON starts EMPTY (verified, no panic). Under the new design every drift then hits R5 (shadow absent) -> CONFLICT -> stash+pull for EVERY divergent file. That is the safe direction (no silent loss) -- the design is RIGHT here, this is its strongest point. BUT: with ~28k notes and ANY divergence (e.g. after the operator's manual cross-host propagation per the ACTIVE-ALERT, many files differ), a wiped shadow means potentially thousands of conflict copies in one reconcile pass, each an atomic write + an integrity check. The unresolved_count tray scan (conflict_stash.rs:340 walk_dir over the whole vault) then runs on every 30s refresh over a vault now containing thousands of new files -> O(N) walk thrash. No data loss, but a self-inflicted conflict-copy avalanche + tray-scan CPU spike that looks like a new incident. The design has no rate-limit or 'shadow rebuild' fast-path (re-derive shadow from a fresh server manifest BEFORE classifying drift) to avoid mass-R5 after a shadow wipe.
attack_scenarios.gap: Shadow-wipe -> mass-R5 is data-SAFE (always-stash) but operationally explosive on 28k notes: thousands of conflict copies + O(N) tray walk_dir thrash. Add a shadow-rebuild fast-path: on detected empty/corrupt shadow, first PULL a full server manifest and seed shadow=server for all paths whose local already==server (idempotent), so only genuinely-divergent files conflict. Otherwise a single corrupt-shadow event = a vault-wide conflict-copy storm.

attack_scenarios.scenario: Delete-vs-edit with tombstone retention shorter than offline window. Design: modification beats deletion, consult I74 tombstone (24h) to avoid resurrecting. Host A deletes note X (tombstone t0). Host B offline, edited X. B reconnects at t0+30h. Tombstone expired (24h retention). B's edited X is now local-only-with-no-server-row -> reconcile classifies 'missing-on-server' -> Direction::Push (verify_repair.rs:253) -> B re-uploads X as a fresh create. The intended delete is UNDONE (zombie resurrection) -- the design's own open_questions flags this ('host offline >24h misses deletes'). For a DELETE that was intentional, resurrection is arguably data-CORRUPTION (the operator deleted it on purpose; it comes back). Not silent data LOSS of an edit, but silent UNDO of a delete = equally a correctness violation under 'never silently diverge from intent'. The design defers this to an open question rather than fixing it.
attack_scenarios.gap: Tombstone retention (24h) < max offline window means an intentional delete is silently resurrected by a >24h-offline host's missing-on-server push.
